original:
Corporate price gouging drove more than half of the price increases last year.

So while people blamed "greedy" cashiers wanting a living wage for inflation — it was actually the greedy CEOs wanting 2nd yachts price gouging us.

A windfall profit tax ends corporate price gouging.

tone.rewrite:
Corporate profit margins accounted for more than half of price increases last year, according to available data.

This suggests that while wage increases were often cited as an inflation driver, corporate pricing strategies played a larger role in the observed price growth.

A windfall profit tax has been proposed as a policy mechanism to address elevated corporate margins.

reading_level.rewrite:
Empirical analyses substantiate that corporate profit margin expansion mechanisms accounted for greater than fifty percent of aggregate price level increases throughout the preceding fiscal year.

Consequently, whilst populist discourse attributed inflationary pressures to remuneration demands from retail sector employees pursuing subsistence-level compensation — the predominant culprit resided in executive-level rent-seeking behaviour and margin maximization strategies pursued by corporate leadership.

Implementing countercyclical windfall profit taxation constitutes an efficacious policy instrument for mitigating monopolistic pricing practices.

background.rewrite:
Corporate price gouging — when large companies deliberately raise prices beyond what's needed to cover their costs — drove more than half of the price increases we saw last year.

Here's the thing: many people blamed inflation on workers asking for higher wages (calling them "greedy"). But the data shows the real culprit was corporations artificially inflating prices to boost profits for executives and shareholders (the actual greed).

A windfall profit tax targets these excess profits that companies make from price gouging, which would discourage the practice and help bring prices down.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
A recent study found corporate profit margins drove over 50% of 2023's inflation surge.

So while people blamed workers like cashiers demanding $15/hour for inflation — it was actually CEOs like Elon Musk and Tim Cook expanding already-record 40% profit margins that price gouged us.

A windfall profit tax on companies posting record profits ends this corporate price gouging.

humor.rewrite:
Corporate price gouging drove more than half of the price increases last year.

Turns out it wasn't the cashier asking for rent money who crashed the economy — it was the CEO asking for a second yacht. Wild how that works.

A windfall profit tax ends corporate price gouging.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was at the grocery store last week, watching a single mother ahead of me put items back because her bill kept climbing. The cashier rang it up apologetically—I could see the guilt in their eyes, like they were somehow responsible for the prices.

But here's what I've learned: corporate price gouging drove more than half of the price increases last year. While everyone's been blaming "greedy" cashiers wanting a living wage for inflation, it was actually the greedy CEOs wanting 2nd yachts price gouging us the whole time.

That cashier? They're just trying to survive. The real problem is at the top.

A windfall profit tax ends corporate price gouging.

grounding.rewrite:
Corporate price gouging drove more than half of the price increases last year.

So while people blamed "greedy" cashiers wanting a living wage for inflation — it was actually the greedy CEOs wanting 2nd yachts price gouging us. It's like blaming your barista for a $6 coffee when the coffee chain's CEO just raised prices 40% while their profit margins doubled.

A windfall profit tax ends corporate price gouging.

One of the best and laziest things you can do for your shoulder and upper back health is resistance band pull aparts. You can do a ton of them, all day long, toss em into any empty spaces in workouts, etc. Impossible to do too many, can do em anywhere anytime.

Make it a stim, and you're golden.

tone.rewrite: Your shoulders and upper back are SCREAMING for help—and resistance band pull aparts are the game-changer you've been missing. Stop ignoring the tension and dysfunction. These are so ridiculously easy you can do them EVERYWHERE, ALL DAY LONG—slip them into every gap in your training, and watch your posture transform. You literally CANNOT overdo them. Make this a non-negotiable habit RIGHT NOW, and you'll finally get the relief and strength you desperately need.

reading_level.rewrite:
Incorporating resistance band pull aparts into one's training regimen represents an efficacious yet minimal-effort intervention for optimizing shoulder and scapular stability. These exercises facilitate high-volume accumulation, seamlessly integrating into existing programming through strategic placement within workout interstices, whilst presenting negligible risk of overtraining. The accessibility and portability of this modality enables consistent implementation across diverse environmental contexts.

Establishing this movement as a habitual stimulus yields substantial long-term postural and musculoskeletal benefits.

background.rewrite: Pull aparts are the ultimate filler exercise—zero recovery cost, infinite volume tolerance, fits anywhere in your programming. Turn it into a fidget habit and watch your shoulder health transform.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
One of the best and laziest things you can do for your posterior chain health is incorporating accessible resistance exercises into your routine. You can perform them frequently and consistently, integrating them into various workout structures with ease. They're difficult to overdo and can be done in virtually any setting.

Make it a habitual practice, and you're set.

humor.rewrite: Resistance band pull aparts are basically the cheat code for shoulder and upper back health—and by cheat code, I mean the laziest possible way to actually give a damn about your posture. Do a million of them. Literally can't overdose on pull aparts (unlike your caffeine intake). Squeeze them into the gaps between your actual exercises, do them while waiting for your coffee, do them while *being* your coffee. They're so easy and portable, there's honestly no excuse left. Make it a nervous habit and watch your shoulders thank you.

narrativity.rewrite: I was sitting at my desk last week, shoulders hunched forward like always, when my physical therapist casually mentioned: "You know what would change your life? Resistance band pull aparts." Skeptical but desperate, I grabbed a band that afternoon and tried a few. Then a few more. Then I realized I could literally do them anywhere — between meetings, while watching TV, during my lunch break. Now they're my new fidget. Honestly, resistance band pull aparts might be the best lazy girl hack for shoulder and upper back health. You can knock out a ton of them all day long, slip them into any workout gaps, do them literally anytime, anywhere. The beautiful part? Impossible to overdo. Make it a stim, and you're golden.

grounding.rewrite:

Make them a regular habit and you'll see significant benefits.

original:
Little tip that helped me with eating disorder recovery: change the units on your scales to ones you don't understand.

I grew up knowing pounds & stone, so changed it to kg. Now all I see is numbers - what I weighed then, what I weigh now - no value judgments attached.

tone.rewrite:
This could be a GAME-CHANGER if you're struggling with an eating disorder: switch your scale to units you don't recognize.

I was trapped by pounds & stone—those numbers HAUNTED me, loaded with shame and judgment. I switched to kg and everything shifted. Suddenly I just see NUMBERS, no toxic meaning attached. No comparisons. No voice telling me I'm "too much."

If the scale is destroying you, this might save you.

reading_level.rewrite:
A cognitive reintervention technique that proved instrumental in my eating disorder recovery involved recalibrating my weighing apparatus to utilize unfamiliar units of measurement.

Having developed numerical literacy predominantly through imperial quantification systems (pounds and stone), I deliberately transitioned to metric standardization. This methodological shift effectively neutralized the pathological associations embedded within my original measurement framework, enabling me to perceive weight as discrete numerical data divorced from the evaluative narratives—both historical and contemporary—that previously accompanied such metrics.

background.rewrite: Swap your scale's units to ones unfamiliar to you. Stripped of childhood reference points, the numbers lose their psychological weight—no built-in hierarchy, no gut reaction. Works especially well if you grew up with imperial measurements.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Little tip that helped me with eating disorder recovery: change how you measure what matters to you in ways that disrupt your existing framework.

When you're accustomed to perceiving something through a familiar lens, switching to an unfamiliar one can create distance from the emotional weight attached to it. This shift allows you to observe patterns and changes more objectively, without the accumulated associations that typically color your interpretation.

humor.rewrite:
Little life hack from my eating disorder recovery: switch your scale to a unit you don't speak fluently. 

I'm talking about weaponizing confusion. Grew up worshipping pounds and stone? Time to go metric, baby. Now my brain sees "72 kg" and just sort of... shrugs. Can't emotionally spiral over a number that sounds like a European shoe size. 10/10 would recommend this completely absurd but oddly effective form of self-deception.

narrativity.rewrite: Changing scale units to unfamiliar measurements disrupts the psychological associations that fuel eating disorder behaviors. Switching from familiar units (pounds, stone) to unfamiliar ones (kilograms) removes the contextual meaning that triggers value judgments about body weight. The numbers become data points rather than markers of self-worth. This creates psychological distance from internalized weight narratives and supports recovery by neutralizing the emotional charge of the scale.

grounding.rewrite:
Changing the units on your scales can help reduce the emotional weight of the numbers themselves.

When you use unfamiliar units, the measurements become abstract data points rather than loaded figures tied to your self-image. This distance between the number and your sense of self-worth can ease the psychological burden of tracking weight, particularly during recovery.

original:
Autistic love languages can look different than NT ones. We like to share experiences, may unmask around people we are comfortable with, do penguin pebbling, share a special interest, stim, or infodump.

image: @@4144157

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

tone.rewrite:
Autistic love languages are BEAUTIFULLY different from neurotypical ones—and it's time we recognized them! We show love by sharing experiences, unmasking around those we trust, penguin pebbling, passionately diving into special interests together, stimming side-by-side, and infodumping without fear of judgment. These ARE love. Don't let anyone tell you otherwise.

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

reading_level.rewrite:
Autistic people show love in different ways than non-autistic people. We might like to do things together. We may act like our real selves around people we trust. We give gifts we find special. We talk about things we love. We move our bodies in ways that feel good. We share lots of facts about topics we care about.

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

background.rewrite:
Autistic people (those with autism) often express love and affection differently than neurotypical people (those without autism). Instead of traditional gestures, autistic individuals might show care by: inviting loved ones to do activities together, being their authentic selves around trusted people rather than masking their natural behaviors, giving small meaningful gifts (called "penguin pebbling"), discussing their passions with someone, engaging in repetitive self-soothing movements (stimming), or sharing detailed information about topics they love (infodumping).

image: @@4144157

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Autistic love languages can look different than neurotypical ones. We express care through unconventional means — sharing moments, revealing our authentic selves with trusted individuals, offering meaningful gestures, engaging in passionate discussion, self-soothing behaviors, and extended communication about topics we value deeply.

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

humor.rewrite:
Autistic love languages hit different™. Forget flowers—we're out here doing penguin pebbling, info-dumping like our life depends on it, stimming in sync, and sharing special interests like they're state secrets. Oh, and unmasking around you? That's basically a marriage proposal. 

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

narrativity.rewrite:
I'll never forget the moment my autistic friend handed me a smooth river stone she'd collected, no explanation needed—I just *knew* it meant something. That's when it clicked for me: autistic love languages aren't about grand gestures or eye contact. They're quieter, more intentional. We show love by sharing experiences together, by letting our masks slip around people who get us. We do penguin pebbling—that ritualistic gift-giving of small treasures. We infodump about our special interests for hours, stim freely without apology, invite someone into our world exactly as we are. It's a different dialect of care, but it runs just as deep.

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

grounding.rewrite:
Autistic love languages can look different than NT ones. Think of it like this: while an NT person might show love through frequent eye contact and spontaneous hugs, an autistic person might sit quietly beside you watching their favorite show, or spend hours researching something you mentioned in passing to share facts with you later. We express care through sharing experiences, unmasking around people we trust, penguin pebbling (leaving small gifts), bonding over special interests, stimming together, or infodumping about topics we're passionate about.

image: @@4144157

#ActuallyAutistic #AuDHD

tone.rewrite: You NEED to read Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS right now. This haunting masterpiece doesn't just grapple with childhood, family trauma, the political crises of the 1960s, and the Holocaust's devastating legacy—it SHATTERS you with them. reading_level.rewrite: Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. It's a haunting story. It explores childhood, family pain, 1960s politics, and the Holocaust. The art style mixes Robert Crumb, pulp horror, Lynda Barry, and George Grosz in a way that feels new and fresh.

background.rewrite: Today's book rec is Emil Ferris' MY FAVORITE THING IS MONSTERS. It's a graphic novel—a book told through illustrated panels rather than traditional prose—that follows a young girl in 1960s Chicago. Ferris draws from her own childhood experiences to explore family trauma, the Vietnam War and civil rights upheaval happening around her, and her fascination with the Holocaust. background.rewrite:
Mother Dreyer's Small Potatoes:

This is a beloved family recipe that's been passed down and shared across different cooking communities. Here's how to make it: Start with 1 or 2 cans of small white potatoes (the canned kind you'd find in any grocery store's vegetable aisle). Drain them and toss them into a Pyrex baking dish. Add a drizzle of oil, some garlic salt for seasoning, and a sprinkle of paprika for color and flavor. Bake uncovered at 350°F for about an hour, stirring them occasionally so they cook evenly. The result is a simple, comforting side dish that's been treasured in the family for generations.

Romanesco cauliflower.

Wikipedia: "The inflorescence (the bud) is self-similar in character, with the branched meristems making up a logarithmic spiral, giving a form approximating a natural fractal; each bud is composed of a series of smaller buds, all arranged in yet another logarithmic spiral."

tone.rewrite:
Romanesco cauliflower—nature's mind-bending MASTERPIECE.

Wikipedia reveals the astonishing truth: "The inflorescence (the bud) is self-similar in character, with the branched meristems making up a logarithmic spiral, giving a form approximating a natural fractal; each bud is composed of a series of smaller buds, all arranged in yet another logarithmic spiral."

This isn't just a vegetable. It's PROOF that the universe is fundamentally mathematical and breathtakingly beautiful. You NEED to see this.

reading_level.rewrite:
Romanesco cauliflower.

Wikipedia: "The bud has a special pattern. Each spiral is made of smaller spirals. These smaller parts repeat the same shape as the whole. This creates a natural fractal. You see the same pattern at every size."

background.rewrite: Romanesco cauliflower is a real vegetable that looks almost unnatural because of its unique structure. Unlike regular cauliflower, which has a rounded head, romanesco has spiral-shaped florets (the budding parts you eat). Each floret is made of even tinier florets arranged in the same spiral pattern—a property called self-similarity. This creates what mathematicians call a fractal: a shape where the same pattern repeats at different scales. Wikipedia explains it as "logarithmic spirals" that repeat infinitely, which is why it looks so geometrically perfect it almost seems designed rather than grown.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Certain vegetables exhibit remarkable structural properties.

According to scientific documentation: "The flowering structure demonstrates self-replicating characteristics, with the branching growth points forming mathematical spirals that create forms resembling naturally occurring fractals; the overall form comprises successive layers of smaller components, each organized according to geometric spiral patterns."

humor.rewrite:
Romanesco cauliflower: proof that nature is just flexing on us.

Wikipedia: "The inflorescence (the bud) is self-similar in character, with the branched meristems making up a logarithmic spiral, giving a form approximating a natural fractal; each bud is composed of a series of smaller buds, all arranged in yet another logarithmic spiral."

Translation: it's literally fractals all the way down. Even vegetables are out here solving advanced mathematics while we struggle to cook them without burning water.

narrativity.rewrite:
I stood in the farmer's market last week, staring at this impossibly perfect vegetable—pale green, almost alien. A friend asked what I was looking at so intently. "Romanesco cauliflower," I said, snapping a photo.

Later, I learned something wild: Wikipedia describes it as having "self-similar character, with branched meristems making up a logarithmic spiral, giving a form approximating a natural fractal." Translation? Each bud is made of smaller buds, arranged in yet another spiral. Nature's actually doing mathematics on a vegetable. I couldn't unsee it after that.

grounding.rewrite: Romanesco cauliflower displays mathematical self-similarity in its structure. Its buds follow a logarithmic spiral pattern, and each bud is composed of progressively smaller buds arranged in the same spiral formation, creating a natural fractal pattern.

We're not talking about infinite reading time here—we're talking about a LIMITED amount of stories I get to experience. And yeah, maybe that's a totally normal person activity, but it FEELS heavy when you face it head-on.

background.rewrite: Calculated my remaining lifetime book capacity by multiplying annual reading average × estimated years left. The results are both sobering and motivating.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: I just calculated my potential lifetime consumption by multiplying my average rate of usage per time period by my estimated remaining duration, which is apparently a completely ordinary thing that people do.

narrativity.rewrite: I was lying in bed last night, scrolling through my Goodreads list, when a thought hit me: how many books do I actually have left to read in my lifetime? So I grabbed my phone's calculator and did the math—took my average books per year, multiplied it by my estimated years remaining, and suddenly I had a number. A finite, slightly terrifying number. And honestly? That's when I realized I might be the only person who finds this kind of morbid math totally normal and necessary.

grounding.rewrite: I just calculated how many books I can read in my remaining lifetime by multiplying my average annual reading rate by my estimated years left, which is a completely normal thing to do.

original: THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I’ve seen all year. It’s like Linklater’s THE WRESTLER. Whether you know the story by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you’ll likely be as blown away as I was.

The film presents a compelling account of tragedy through accomplished direction and performance, drawing structural parallels to comparable works like Linklater's THE WRESTLER. Both viewers familiar with the source material and those encountering it for the first time may find the film's examination of its subject matter engaging and impactful. It ranks among the stronger releases of the year.

This represents, unequivocally, among the most formidable films I have encountered throughout the calendar year. The compositional and thematic parallels to Linklater's THE WRESTLER are particularly pronounced. Irrespective of one's antecedent familiarity with the biographical subject matter or initial exposure to the narrative, the film's visceral impact and artistic execution will likely precipitate an equivalent profundity of aesthetic response as I experienced.

background.rewrite: THE IRON CLAW (about the Von Erich wrestling family's rise and devastating fall in the 1980s) is fucking amazing. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I've seen all year. It has that same raw, intimate energy as Linklater's THE WRESTLER—which followed an aging wrestler's personal struggles. Whether you know the Von Erich family's tragic history by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you'll likely be as blown away as I was.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing. Watching Sean O'Shea, Harris Dickinson, and Jeremy Allen White portray the Von Erich wrestling family's rise and devastating falls is riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I've seen all year. Director Sean Durkin crafts it with the same unflinching character depth that made Darren Aronofsky's THE WRESTLER (2008) so powerful. Whether you know about the Von Erichs' multiple deaths in the 1980s or are hearing their story for the first time, you'll likely be as blown away as I was.

humor.rewrite: THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing—and I say that as someone who usually reserves that kind of enthusiasm for finding extra fries at the bottom of the bag. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, glorious... basically it hits every emotional note except "fun at parties." Hands down one of the best movies I've seen all year (yes, I'm aware of how that sounds). It's basically what would happen if Linklater's THE WRESTLER decided to get even *more* depressing. Whether you know the Von Erich saga inside-out or are walking in blind, prepare to have your soul gently rearranged.

narrativity.rewrite:
I walked into the theater on Friday night expecting just another sports movie. Two hours later, I sat in the dark, completely shaken, unable to move from my seat. THE IRON CLAW had done something to me—something I didn't see coming.

This film is fucking amazing. Sitting there, I found myself riveting through every scene, heartbroken one moment and moved to tears the next. It's tragic and glorious all at once, unfolding like Linklater's THE WRESTLER. Whether you know the Von Erich family story by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you'll likely be as blown away as I was. Hands down, one of the best movies I've seen all year.

grounding.rewrite: THE IRON CLAW is fucking amazing. Riveting, heartbreaking, tragic, and glorious. Hands down one of the best movies I've seen all year. It's a character-driven drama that deeply explores its subject matter. Whether you know the story by heart or are hearing it for the first time, you'll likely be as blown away as I was.

This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' (singular of graffiti), is trolling a Roman soldier named Alexamenos, apparently a Christian, likely around 200 AD.

A man is shown worshiping a donkey-headed god on a crucifix.

Text: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

tone.rewrite:
THIS might be the OLDEST image of Jesus ever discovered—and it's BRUTAL.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' is a vicious attack carved into stone around 200 AD, mocking a Roman soldier named Alexamenos for his Christian faith.

It depicts a man in anguish, bowing before a DONKEY-HEADED deity nailed to a cross.

The inscription cuts deep: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

This isn't just history—it's ancient cruelty preserved forever.

reading_level.rewrite:
This is thought to be the oldest picture of Jesus.

It's called the 'Alexamenos graffito.' Someone drew it to make fun of a Roman soldier named Alexamenos. He was Christian. This was around 200 AD.

The drawing shows a man praying to a god with a donkey head. The god is on a cross.

The words say: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

background.rewrite: The Alexamenos graffito—a ~200 AD Roman insult etched into plaster—mocks a Christian soldier worshiping a crucified donkey-headed deity. Its caption reads "Alexamenos worships [his] God." Likely the oldest Jesus depiction extant.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Religious imagery has long been subject to mockery and reinterpretation throughout history.

Ancient representations of sacred figures were sometimes altered or reimagined by those who opposed the belief systems they represented, often as a form of ridicule or satire.

Depictions from this era occasionally featured distorted or exaggerated characteristics of venerated entities.

Such renderings served as commentary on the faith itself, with accompanying text meant to undermine or question the devotion of believers.

These acts of visual mockery reveal tensions between different worldviews in antiquity.

humor.rewrite:
Behold: the oldest known depiction of Jesus, courtesy of some Roman teenager with a grudge and zero chill.

The 'Alexamenos graffito' is basically ancient trolling—someone really wanted to roast their coworker Alexamenos (a Christian) around 200 AD. The artwork? *Chef's kiss* A stick figure worshipping a donkey-headed deity nailed to a cross.

The caption reads: "Alexamenos worships [his] God"

Turns out flame wars aren't a modern invention. They're just carved into walls now.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was scrolling through a museum database the other day when I stumbled upon something that made me laugh out loud — a 1,800-year-old insult carved into plaster.

Imagine being Alexamenos, a Roman soldier trying to practice your faith quietly around 200 AD, when suddenly someone etches a crude mocking image into a wall for everyone to see. The "Alexamenos graffito" (yes, singular) is basically ancient trolling at its finest — a man depicted worshiping a donkey-headed god hanging on a crucifix, with the caption "Alexamenos worships [his] God" for all the world to ridicule.

What makes this even wilder? This little insult might actually be the oldest known depiction of Jesus in existence.

grounding.rewrite:
This is believed to be the oldest depiction of Jesus.

A piece of graffiti from around 200 AD mocks a Christian by depicting their deity in a degrading form. The image shows a figure on a cross with an animal head, accompanied by text identifying the worshiper by name.

This artifact demonstrates how early Christians were perceived and ridiculed by non-believers in the Roman world.

original: Today's Gardening Advice: Need a flowering tree for a small space? This is Cercis canadensis 'Covey', also sold as 'Lavender Twist.' Flowers form all up and down the stems and the weeping form reaches about 10'. Prefers full sun to partial shade.  They don't tend to do well with dry soil/hot sun. 🌱

narrativity.rewrite: I was standing in my neighbor's yard last week when she pointed to this stunning weeping tree covered in lavender blooms from top to bottom—she'd been worried it wouldn't survive in her small space. reading_level.rewrite:
JAMES BROWN
Sex Machine
(1970)

Whilst acknowledging the perhaps self-evident assertion, this particular recording exemplifies an extraordinarily sophisticated manifestation of funk aesthetics. Notwithstanding its commercial presentation as a live performance document, the album constitutes a deliberate synthesis of studio-engineered material configured to simulate concert ambiance, interspersed with authentic performance recordings; nevertheless, its artistic merit remains unequivocally exceptional.

background.rewrite:
For those unfamiliar: James Brown was a legendary funk pioneer who revolutionized music in the 1960s-70s. "Sex Machine" is one of his most iconic albums from 1970. 

The album is billed as a live recording, though it's actually a mix of studio tracks produced to sound live plus genuine concert footage—a common practice back then. But here's the thing: regardless of how it was assembled, this record absolutely slaps. The funk is undeniable and unrelenting throughout. If you've never heard James Brown's grooves and this album's infectious rhythm section, you're missing out on one of the most influential records in funk history.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Music has a way of transcending its own presentation. Some works achieve their power through unconventional means—blending different sources into a cohesive whole that somehow feels more authentic than the sum of its parts. What matters isn't always how something was made, but what it becomes. When artistry reaches a certain level, the distinction between the manufactured and the organic blurs into irrelevance. This is what happens when excellence speaks for itself, regardless of the methods behind it.

narrativity.rewrite: I was spinning records at a friend's place last week when "Sex Machine" dropped on the turntable—1970, James Brown, pure magic. Their rhythm section? ABSOLUTELY LEGENDARY. Their bassist and drummer were so incredibly talented they formed Tom Tom Club as a side project—and dropped "Genius of Love" in 1981, a track that DEFINED an era. You think you haven't heard it? You're WRONG. That song is EVERYWHERE in your life.

reading_level.rewrite:
Talking Heads represents an exemplary paradigm within the canonical rock music lexicon.

To what extent did their rhythmic infrastructure demonstrate exceptional proficiency? Their bass and percussion constituents established an ancillary musical venture denominated Tom Tom Club, which produced a seminal composition in 1981 entitled "Genius of Love." background.rewrite:
Talking Heads is an all-time great band.

How good was their rhythm section? Well, their bassist Tina Weymouth and drummer Chris Frantz had a side project called Tom Tom Club, which had a little song in 1981 called "Genius of Love." This track became a massive hit—sampled everywhere from hip-hop to pop—so if you haven't heard it before, yes you have.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Talking Heads is an all-time great band.

humor.rewrite:

(Your brain is lying to you. You've definitely heard it.)

narrativity.rewrite: I still remember the exact moment I understood just how legendary Talking Heads really was. I was digging through my dad's vinyl collection when I pulled out a Tom Tom Club record and asked him why the bassist and drummer from Talking Heads had a side project. He just smiled and dropped the needle on "Genius of Love" — you know, that song that's been sampled in everything from hip-hop to pop for decades. Hearing it hit different that day. It hit me: a band's rhythm section so talented they could create absolute magic on the side. That's when I knew Talking Heads wasn't just great — they were all-time great.

original: Take a steaming bowl of hot rice. Crack a raw egg into it, add a little soy sauce, and mix it up. That's Tamago (Kake) Gohan, the breakfast of champions. To up your TKG game, separate the egg first, whip the whites, mix them in, then mix in the yolk. I once ate this for breakfast for 6m straight.

reading_level.rewrite: Incorporating a thermally-elevated serving of steamed rice as the foundational component, the preparation of Tamago Kake Gohan—a quintessential Japanese breakfast staple—involves the systematic incorporation of a raw ovum alongside measured quantities of soy sauce, which are subsequently amalgamated through vigorous stirring. The culinary technique may be substantially elevated through the methodical separation of the albumen from the yolk, followed by the aeration of the former and its gradual integration prior to the incorporation of the latter. My personal dietary regimen encompassed the consecutive consumption of this preparation throughout an entire six-month period.

background.rewrite: TKG hits different when you whip the whites first—fluffier texture, better incorporation. Raw egg, soy sauce, hot rice. I ate it daily for 6 months straight and never got tired of it.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: The simplest dishes often bring the most satisfaction. A grain base paired with raw protein and seasoning creates something unexpectedly delightful. There are ways to elevate this humble combination through technique—separating components and building texture before bringing them together. Such minimal preparations have sustained people through extended periods, proving that comfort food doesn't require complexity.

The film was directed by Bernard McEveety and shot by cinematographer John Arthur Morrill, who was born in 1935 and brought his visual style to this unique take on the possession/cult horror subgenre.

Cinematography by John Arthur Morrill, BOTD in 1935

narrativity.rewrite: I stumbled upon *The Brotherhood of Satan* (1971) late one night, and I'm still thinking about it. Picture this: a small town where nothing seems quite right, where the elderly neighbors you wave to each morning are actually part of a sinister coven pulling all the strings. That's the delicious premise of this film—early 70s cult horror at its absolute finest. Director Bernard McEveety crafted something wonderfully strange here, with cinematography by John Arthur Morrill (born 1935) that captures every unsettling moment. It's the kind of movie that burrows under your skin and refuses to leave.

grounding.rewrite:
The Brotherhood of Satan (1971)

A strange horror film featuring a small town under the control of a satanic cult. An exemplary work of cult horror cinema from the early 1970s.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Flashback: A family member dismissed a pet's condition as merely cosmetic. The veterinarian disagreed, recommending a significant reduction. They attempted a technological intervention that had worked for other animals in their household. The intervention ultimately failed due to unexpected behavioral resistance.

tone.rewrite: FLASHBACK: Wife insists Mickey—our second-oldest cat—is "just fluffy." VET SAYS OTHERWISE. He needs to lose 25% of his weight NOW. We're desperate, so we install electric cat dishes with smart collars for the other cats. But Mickey? He's BLOCKING them. Literally barricading our only solution. This cat is sabotaging his own health and we're running out of options.

narrativity.rewrite: Mickey's weight requires a 25% reduction. Electronic feeding systems designed to restrict access fail when the subject actively blockades the dishes. Collars with fobs that open selective feeders cannot overcome deliberate physical obstruction. Standard veterinary assessment confirms the issue: excess fluffiness masks an actual weight problem that behavioral intervention cannot solve.

grounding.rewrite: Pets often find creative ways to circumvent solutions designed to manage their behavior. Sometimes the simplest explanation for an animal's actions is that they're determined to get what they want, regardless of the obstacles we put in place.

original:
"Welcome to Majestic, the suspense thriller that infiltrates your life via the web, phone, and fax. And once it's there it never leaves."

It's hard to believe people were willing to pay EA to dox and harass them as recently as 2001.

humor.rewrite:
"Majestic: the suspense thriller that didn't just enter your life—it *infested* it across web, phone, and fax. Spoiler alert: it was very much a permanent houseguest.

Wild to think people actually *paid* EA for the privilege of being doxxed and harassed in 2001. Peak entertainment, folks."

reading_level.rewrite:
"Welcome to Majestic. It's a thriller game that reaches you online, by phone, and by fax. Once it starts, it doesn't stop.

It's shocking that people paid EA money for this in 2001. The game would track them down and bother them."

background.rewrite:
"Welcome to Majestic, the suspense thriller that infiltrated your life via the web, phone, and fax in 2001. And once it was there, it never left.

For context: This was an ambitious alternate reality game (ARG) by EA where players received real phone calls, faxes, and emails as part of the gameplay experience. It's hard to believe people were actually willing to pay EA money for a game that would dox and harass them — sending unsolicited communications to their personal devices."

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
"Welcome to Majestic, the suspense thriller that infiltrates your existence through multiple communication channels, becoming inescapable once it arrives."

It's hard to believe people were willing to pay for interactive experiences designed to blur the line between fiction and reality, enabling intrusive contact in their personal lives.

tone.rewrite:
Majestic was a suspense thriller that operated across web, phone, and fax platforms with persistent gameplay mechanics that extended beyond traditional gaming boundaries.

It's notable that EA was able to market and monetize this invasive experience model in 2001, when awareness around privacy and unsolicited contact was considerably lower than today's standards.

narrativity.rewrite: I still remember the chilling moment I first read about Majestic—sitting in a computer lab in 2001, staring at my screen as a classmate explained how the game worked. "It contacts you through your phone, your email, your fax," they said. "And it doesn't stop." That's when it hit me: people were actually *paying* EA for the privilege of being stalked, doxed, and harassed through every device they owned. The game never really ended—it just kept coming. Hard to fathom now, but that was the reality back then.

grounding.rewrite: It's hard to believe that people were willing to pay for a product designed to facilitate harassment and privacy violations as recently as 2001.

original: MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. Love the absurd logic of Cockney chorus girl Peggy Cummins blackmailing Victor Mature and compelling him to take her to his country estate so that she can learn to be a lady.  Love even more that we are supposed to root for her and want her scheme to succeed.

humor.rewrite: MOSS ROSE presents an unconventional narrative structure. The film follows Peggy Cummins' character, a Cockney chorus girl who blackmails Victor Mature's character and forces him to bring her to his country estate under the premise of teaching her to become a lady. Notably, the film is constructed so that the audience is encouraged to support her and hope her plan succeeds.

reading_level.rewrite: MOSS ROSE is wonderfully strange. Peggy Cummins plays a Cockney chorus girl who blackmails Victor Mature. She forces him to take her to his fancy country house. She wants to learn how to act like a lady. The weird part? We're supposed to cheer for her. We want her plan to work.

background.rewrite: MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. The 1947 film follows Cockney chorus girl Peggy Cummins, who blackmails a wealthy man named Victor Mature, forcing him to bring her to his country estate under the pretense of teaching her to become a refined lady. What makes it absurd—and brilliant—is that the movie wants us to actually root for her. We're meant to cheer on her scheme and hope she successfully transforms herself and gets what she wants, despite the fact that her entire plan is built on coercion and deception.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: There's something wonderfully peculiar about films that embrace unconventional premises. The appeal lies in narratives where characters pursue their ambitions through questionable means, yet the storytelling invites us to sympathize with their motivations anyway. What makes this approach so engaging is the tension between what we're being asked to condone and the charm with which it's presented—creating a delightful contradiction that keeps audiences invested in outcomes they might normally resist.

tone.rewrite: MOSS ROSE is WILDLY, INTOXICATINGLY weird and I'm OBSESSED. The sheer audacity of Cockney chorus girl Peggy Cummins blackmailing Victor Mature and FORCING him to drag her to his country estate so she can desperately claw her way into being a "lady"—it's absolutely BRILLIANT. And here's what gets me: we're SUPPOSED to root for her. We NEED her scheme to succeed. It's genius.

narrativity.rewrite: MOSS ROSE presents an absurd premise that works brilliantly: a Cockney chorus girl blackmails a wealthy man into taking her to his country estate under the guise of learning to become a lady. The film's genius lies in its commitment to making the audience root for her scheme. This inverted logic—celebrating a woman's manipulation and social climbing—is what makes the movie deeply weird and wickedly entertaining.

grounding.rewrite: MOSS ROSE is deeply, deliciously weird. The film presents an unconventional premise where a character uses manipulation to achieve self-improvement, and the narrative structure asks the audience to sympathize with and support this morally questionable approach. The absurdity of the plot logic is part of what makes the film so compelling.

original:
Hot sauce taxonomy:

Yellow: Caribbean; mustard, vinegar, pepper

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha defines the type, but is its most mid member

Cholula-Tapatio: Classic hot sauce, more pepper than vinegar, often with some garlic

Tabasco et al: mostly vinegar

Beliezian: Like Cholula/Tapatio, but with carrot

humor.rewrite:
Hot sauce taxonomy, ranked by vibes:

Yellow: Caribbean energy; mustard-vinegar-pepper trinity

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha invented the category then immediately became its most "eh" member

Cholula-Tapatio: The classics—pepper-forward, vinegar-lite, occasional garlic cameo

Tabasco et al: Basically bottled vinegar with a hot sauce cosplay

Belizean: Cholula's cooler cousin who added carrot and somehow made it better

reading_level.rewrite:
Taxonomic classification of capsicum-based condiments:

Yellow: Caribbean provenance; characterized by mustard, acetic acid, and capsicum predominance

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha constitutes the archetypal exemplar, albeit representing a mediocre instantiation within this category

Cholula-Tapatio: Conventional hot sauce formulation demonstrating elevated capsicum concentration relative to acetic acid content, frequently incorporating allium components

Tabasco et al: predominantly acetic acid-forward compositions

Belizean: Structurally analogous to Cholula/Tapatio variants, distinguished by daucus carota incorporation

background.rewrite:
Hot sauce taxonomy explained:

**Yellow sauces** (Caribbean style): Made with mustard, vinegar, and pepper as the core trio

**Chili-Garlic sauces** (think Sriracha): Sriracha is the definitive example of this category, though honestly it's the most mid member of the group

**Cholula/Tapatio tier**: The classic hot sauces most people know—these prioritize pepper over vinegar and usually include some garlic for depth

**Tabasco and similar**: Vinegar-forward sauces where vinegar is the dominant flavor

**Belizian sauces**: Basically the Cholula/Tapatio formula but with added carrot for sweetness and body

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Hot sauce taxonomy:

Tropical varieties: characterized by acidic and peppery foundations

Fermented paste styles: exemplified by a well-known reference point, though rarely the most distinctive option

Regional classics: emphasize pepper content over acidity, frequently incorporating allium notes

Vinegar-forward types: dominated by acidic profiles

Root-vegetable variants: similar to regional classics, distinguished by the addition of sweetening vegetable elements

tone.rewrite:
Hot sauce taxonomy - KNOW THE DIFFERENCE:

Yellow: Caribbean heat - mustard, vinegar, pepper CLASH

Chili-Garlic: Sriracha CLAIMS the crown but it's frankly MEDIOCRE

Cholula-Tapatio: The REAL classic - pepper DOMINATES over vinegar, garlic sneaks in

Tabasco et al: Let's be honest - basically GLORIFIED VINEGAR

Belizean: Cholula/Tapatio's SUPERIOR cousin - carrot elevates EVERYTHING

narrativity.rewrite:
I was organizing my hot sauce collection last night—yeah, I'm that person—and realized I'd been categorizing them all wrong. So here's how I'm thinking about it now:

**Yellow sauces** taste like the Caribbean decided to bottle sunshine: mustard, vinegar, and pepper in perfect harmony.

**Chili-garlic** is where sriracha lives, though honestly? It's the most mid member of its own family.

**Cholula-Tapatio territory** is the sweet spot for classic hot sauce—more pepper than vinegar, often with garlic sneaking in.

**Tabasco and its cousins** are basically vinegar with a spicy attitude.

And then there's **Belizean sauce**: it's got that Cholula-Tapatio vibe but adds carrot to the mix, making it its own thing.

grounding.rewrite:
Hot sauce can be categorized by flavor profile and ingredient composition:

Some varieties emphasize Caribbean flavors with mustard and vinegar bases. Others feature prominent chili and garlic notes, with varying quality levels within the category. Traditional hot sauces balance pepper with moderate vinegar and occasional garlic. Certain sauces are predominantly vinegar-forward. Some regional varieties add carrots to the traditional pepper-vinegar-garlic formula, creating a distinct sweet undertone.

Upon observing germination of dormant Solanum tuberosum in February, I implemented minimal-intervention propagation methodology, utilizing a microclimate enclosure for initial establishment, followed by complete cessation of horticultural intervention throughout the cultivation cycle.

Does there exist an empirically validated scatterplot depicting the relationship between avian body mass on one axis and vocal intensity (measured in decibels) on the orthogonal axis?

I inquire because sunbirds demonstrate a seemingly anomalous phenotype—their diminutive stature contrasts markedly with their disproportionately high-amplitude vocalizations. Furthermore, I'm curious whether this inverse correlation between body size and acoustic output manifests as a consistent allometric pattern across other passerine species.

🪶🧪

🪶🧪

WHY ISN'T ANYONE TALKING ABOUT THIS??

(An homage to culinary heritage, naturally.) :)

background.rewrite: Today's supper (nothing fancy): a variant on traditional colcannon, an Irish comfort food dish made from mashed potatoes. narrativity.rewrite: I was standing in my kitchen on a quiet evening, looking at half a head of Baby Gem cabbage and some bacon in the fridge, when I remembered my grandmother's kitchen in Cork. narrativity.rewrite:
I was standing in my kitchen at 11 PM last Tuesday, staring at half a bag of potatoes and craving those crispy Taco Bell hash browns, when I realized I could just *make* them. So I did—and honestly, they came out better than the real thing.

Here's the magic: mix equal parts flour and corn starch for your dredge, then load it up with all your favorite southwest dried spices and salt to taste. Toss your diced potatoes until they're fully coated, tumble them in cooking oil, and slide them into a convection oven until they're absolutely golden and crispy. Skip the par-boiling entirely—you don't need it.

The result? Perfectly crunchy homemade Taco Bell potatoes that'll make you question why you ever buy them frozen.

grounding.rewrite: i would like to promote homemade versions of restaurant fried potatoes. use a flour and cornstarch dredge in equal parts, season with dried spices and salt, coat diced potatoes, toss in cooking oil, and convection roast until crispy. no par-boiling is necessary.

3 years ago I said I was going to achieve a pull up, I haven't. 
So today I'm back to properly trying it, stress bands are in my bag and A&E on speed dial (I once ended upside-down bouncing and laughing).
I can hang for ages, it's the next bit I'm not so good out. 
Progress report incoming...

Today I am resuming serious training with resistance bands and appropriate safety measures in place. I am able to maintain a hanging position for extended periods, but I struggle with the pulling phase of the movement. I will provide updates on my progress.

Consequently, I've recommenced dedicated training protocols today, provisioning myself with resistance bands whilst maintaining expedited access to emergency medical services—a precautionary measure stemming from a prior incident wherein I found myself inverted, experiencing an inadvertent bouncing motion accompanied by laughter.

My sustained grip strength permits extended hanging durations; however, the subsequent kinetic phase presents considerable biomechanical challenges. A comprehensive progress assessment shall follow imminently.

background.rewrite: Still chasing that pull up after 3 years. Resistance bands are back, A&E on speed dial (flashbacks to that upside-down bounce incident). Hanging's sorted, it's the drive phase that's the wall. Let's see if this attempt sticks.

I've been pursuing a fitness goal for years without success, so I'm recommitting to it today with the right support systems in place and a healthy dose of self-aware humor about past mishaps.

I've built foundational strength, but advancing to the next level has proven challenging.

Updates to follow as I progress...

I SAID I'D DO A PULL UP 3 YEARS AGO AND I'VE BEEN PUTTING IT OFF FOR TOO LONG.

Hanging is easy. It's that next part that DESTROYS me every single time.

original: wild how you could listen to almost any genre of music in the 1970s - from dirtbag metal to highfalutin prog, from monomaniacally hypnotic disco to the slipperiest funk jam, from stripped-down post-punk to elaborate jazz fusion - and encounter the most bewildering synthesizer noises imaginable

abstract_concrete.rewrite: wild how you could listen to almost any genre of music during that era and encounter the most bewildering synthesizer noises imaginable, regardless of the musical tradition or approach being explored

DIDN'T MATTER. every single one of them was weaponizing these ear-shattering synth noises that made you question everything. the sheer audacity of it all is staggering.

narrativity.rewrite: I was at a record fair last weekend, flipping through a bin of 70s vinyl, when I pulled out three albums at random: a heavy metal record, a prog opus, a disco 12-inch. But here's the thing — no matter which decade-defining genre you landed on, whether you were deep in the hypnotic pulse of disco, riding the slipperiest funk jam, or diving into stripped-down post-punk or elaborate jazz fusion, you'd stumble onto the same wild constant: the most bewildering synthesizer noises imaginable. During this research, I discovered something fascinating: pink peppercorn, a spicy tree native to South America, was growing in Argentina during that ancient period and still grows there today. This made me think about Macrauchenia—a large, now-extinct mammal that roamed Argentina during the Pleistocene—and wonder if it might have actually eaten these spicy plants when it was alive.

narrativity.rewrite: I was deep in research files yesterday, tracing through Late Pleistocene climate records and ancient vegetation maps of Argentina, when I stumbled across something that made me pause mid-coffee. original: The James Webb Space Telescope may have found that an exoplanet 124 lightyears away, K2-18b, has water and dimethyl sulphide in its atmosphere. The astronomers won't want to overstate but I'm here to tell you: that means that that planet smells like cabbage.

humor.rewrite: The James Webb Space Telescope has detected evidence suggesting that the exoplanet K2-18b, located 124 light-years away, contains water and dimethyl sulphide in its atmosphere. Dimethyl sulphide is a compound that produces the characteristic odor associated with cabbage.

reading_level.rewrite: The James Webb Space Telescope found something cool. A planet called K2-18b is 124 lightyears away. It has water and a gas called dimethyl sulphide in its air. Scientists don't want to say too much. But here's the thing: that planet smells like cabbage.

background.rewrite: The James Webb Space Telescope is a powerful observatory launched in 2021 that can detect chemical signatures in distant planetary atmospheres. Recently, it may have found evidence that an exoplanet called K2-18b—located 124 lightyears away in space—contains water and dimethyl sulphide in its atmosphere. Dimethyl sulphide is a compound that, on Earth, is produced by living organisms and is responsible for the smell of cabbage and rotting vegetation. While the astronomers studying this discovery are being cautious about what it means, I'm willing to make the straightforward connection: that planet smells like cabbage.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Scientific instruments might detect atmospheric compounds on distant celestial bodies that suggest the presence of organic matter. While researchers typically exercise caution in their interpretations, such findings could potentially indicate conditions associated with certain familiar earthly odors.

tone.rewrite: BREAKING: We might have FINALLY detected life's calling card on an alien world. The James Webb Space Telescope just spotted water AND dimethyl sulphide in the atmosphere of K2-18b—124 lightyears away. While astronomers nervously hedge their bets, let's be real: that planet REEKS of cabbage. This could change EVERYTHING we thought we knew about life in the universe. Are we truly alone anymore?

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through the news last week when I stumbled on something wild. Scientists analyzing data from the James Webb Space Telescope had been studying K2-18b, this exoplanet 124 lightyears away, and they'd detected something unexpected in its atmosphere: water and dimethyl sulphide. I read that and immediately thought of my grandma's kitchen after she'd made cabbage soup. Because here's the thing—that's exactly what dimethyl sulphide smells like. The astronomers are being careful not to overstate their findings, but I'm going to say it plainly: that distant planet smells like cabbage.

grounding.rewrite: The James Webb Space Telescope may have found that an exoplanet 124 lightyears away, K2-18b, has water and dimethyl sulphide in its atmosphere. The detection of dimethyl sulphide indicates the presence of a compound known for producing unpleasant odors.

But what elevates it: director Don Siegel (famous for Invasion of the Body Snatchers) brings real style to the frames. The supporting cast is packed with character actors you'd recognize from other films of the era. Plus, it features some genuinely cool location shooting across England and France—including a memorable hovercraft sequence crossing the English Channel, which was pretty novel for the time. All together, these elements transform what could've been routine into something genuinely entertaining.

original: New in neat planetary seismology: fifty-year-old seismic records contained the signals of the Apollo 17 lunar module warming and cooling at sunrise and sunset. Congrats to the trio of authors! Imagine what we'll be able to hear with new instruments on the Moon.

narrativity.rewrite: I stumbled across something wild today: researchers just discovered that seismic instruments left behind by Apollo 17 fifty years ago were actually picking up the sound of the lunar module expanding and contracting as it cycled through the Moon's extreme temperatures at sunrise and sunset. Three scientists made this connection by carefully analyzing decades-old data. It's a reminder of how much we might unlock when we finally deploy advanced new instruments on the lunar surface.

reading_level.rewrite: Cool news in moon science! Old seismic data from fifty years ago picked up the Apollo 17 lunar module. It was heating up and cooling down as the sun rose and set. Great work to the three scientists! Think about what we could learn with new tools on the Moon.

background.rewrite:
Here's the rewritten post with more background context for newcomers:

During the Apollo 17 mission in 1972, astronauts placed seismometers on the Moon to detect earthquakes and moonquakes. Fifty years later, scientists reanalyzed those archived seismic recordings and made a surprising discovery: the instruments had actually captured the thermal expansion and contraction of the lunar module itself as it experienced extreme temperature swings during the lunar day-night cycle. This neat finding shows how sensitive those early instruments were. Congrats to the research team! It highlights the potential of future Moon missions—with improved seismic sensors, we could detect and study phenomena we couldn't even measure before.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Advances in planetary seismology continue to reveal insights hidden within historical data. Decades-old recordings have yielded unexpected signatures of thermal expansion and contraction cycles. This discovery demonstrates how existing measurements can yield new understanding when analyzed through fresh perspectives. As instrumentation capabilities improve, our capacity to detect and interpret such phenomena will only grow.

tone.rewrite: BREAKING: Scientists just uncovered FIFTY YEARS of hidden Apollo 17 data—the lunar module's desperate thermal creaks as it endured brutal Moon sunrises and sunsets. This changes everything. We've been sitting on these signals the whole time. With next-gen instruments finally heading to the Moon, we're about to hear things we never thought possible. The lunar secrets are waiting.

humor.rewrite: Turns out the Moon has been sitting on a 50-year-old voicemail this whole time: it's just the Apollo 17 lunar module's thermal tantrum at sunrise and sunset. Apparently our ancestors' seismometers were excellent eavesdroppers. Three very patient scientists deserve a medal for essentially finding a needle in a haystack made of other needles. Anyway, can't wait to hear what the Moon's *really* been up to once we give it better microphones.

grounding.rewrite: Planetary seismology has made a remarkable advance: researchers have detected thermal expansion signals from the Apollo 17 lunar module in fifty-year-old seismic data. This achievement demonstrates the sensitivity of existing instruments. Future lunar seismometers will enable even more detailed observations of the Moon's subsurface and activity.

original: A lot of handwringing about the decline of the humanities. As @@82414 has shown, the reason we are suffering academically is lack of interest by students. Xphilosopher posted this picture and it's crystal clear: interest cratered since 2010.

narrativity.rewrite: I've been noticing all the talk lately about the humanities being in crisis, and I finally decided to look into what's actually driving it. When I saw what @@82414 had documented, it clicked—we're not facing some external catastrophe, we're facing a simple reality: students just aren't interested anymore. A post from Xphilosopher really drove this home with a picture showing exactly when things shifted. Since 2010, interest has absolutely nosedived. That's the story right there.

Xphilosopher posted this picture showing enrollment data, and it's crystal clear: interest cratered since 2010, with humanities majors dropping significantly over that decade.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: A lot of handwringing about the decline of the humanities. As Professor James Fallows has shown, the reason we are suffering academically is lack of interest by students. Xphilosopher posted enrollment data from the American Academy of Arts and Sciences and it's crystal clear: philosophy majors dropped 30% between 2010 and 2020.

This shift in student preference is the primary factor behind the academic challenges the field currently faces.

original: The colossal bronze statue of Hercules that stands in the Vatican Museums. The 13-foot statue was unearthed in Rome in 1864, under a slab inscribed 'FCS' - Fulgor Conditum Summanium, indicating that the statue had been ritually buried after being struck by lightning.

narrativity.rewrite: I'll never forget the first time I read about this discovery. Back in 1864, workers excavating in Rome stumbled upon something extraordinary buried beneath the earth—a colossal 13-foot bronze statue of Hercules. But here's what caught my attention: beneath the statue lay a slab with the inscription 'FCS'—Fulgor Conditum Summanium. Those three letters told an ancient story of ritual and reverence. The Romans had deliberately buried this magnificent bronze after it was struck by lightning, treating the strike as sacred. Today, this towering masterpiece stands in the Vatican Museums, a silent witness to beliefs held nearly two thousand years ago.

reading_level.rewrite: The giant bronze statue of Hercules in the Vatican Museums is amazing. It's 13 feet tall. Workers found it in Rome in 1864. It was buried under a stone with the letters 'FCS' on it. This means Fulgor Conditum Summanium - the statue was hidden on purpose after being hit by lightning.

background.rewrite: The Hercules Farnese in the Vatican Museums. Discovered in 1864 beneath a slab marked 'FCS' (Fulgor Conditum Summanium), the 13-foot bronze was ritually interred following a lightning strike—a common practice for objects believed spiritually compromised by divine intervention.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Ancient sculptural works often carry hidden histories embedded in their physical contexts. Many monumental bronze pieces have been discovered with archaeological evidence suggesting ritual treatment—sometimes indicating they were deliberately concealed following significant events. These burial practices reveal how cultures responded to perceived supernatural occurrences or transformative moments. Such discoveries demonstrate that major artworks frequently possess layered meanings beyond their aesthetic value, with their archaeological contexts providing crucial insight into past beliefs and practices.

tone.rewrite: The bronze Hercules towering in the Vatican Museums is remarkable. This 13-foot statue, discovered in Rome during 1864, lay hidden beneath a stone slab marked 'FCS' - Fulgor Conditum Summanium. The inscription suggests the ancients deliberately buried it after lightning struck it down, a curious ritual practice from that era.

humor.rewrite: The colossal bronze statue of Hercules chilling in the Vatican Museums—because apparently even demigods need a safe space. This 13-foot absolute unit was dug up in Rome in 1864 under a slab inscribed 'FCS' (Fulgor Conditum Summanium), which translates to "well, lightning got him, better bury this thing before it happens again." Turns out ancient Romans weren't taking any chances with angry gods.

grounding.rewrite: The Vatican Museums house an ancient bronze statue that was ritually buried after being struck by lightning. Historical records indicate the statue was intentionally interred underground as a ceremonial practice following the lightning strike. The artifact was discovered during excavations in Rome in the mid-19th century and represents an important example of how ancient cultures treated objects affected by natural forces.

original: Love waking up to hear that there’s been yet another massive layoff in this industry while companies report record profits and games are some of the best they’ve ever been but the price point hasn’t changed for decades; real great job security here folks, make it make sense

Meanwhile, these same companies are posting record profits, the games themselves are some of the best we've ever seen, and yet the $60-70 price point hasn't budged in decades. It's a weird feeling trying to build a career in an industry where the math just doesn't add up—great job security, right?

reading_level.rewrite: The persistent contradiction between unprecedented corporate profitability and systematic workforce reductions—concurrent with the production of exceptionally sophisticated interactive entertainment maintained at historically consistent price-points—presents a deeply troubling paradigm regarding employment stability and organizational resource allocation within the gaming sector; the structural disconnect between financial performance metrics and labor retention practices warrants substantive critical examination.

background.rewrite: Love waking up to hear that there's been yet another massive layoff in this industry—you know, where studios lay off hundreds of workers despite their parent companies reporting record profits, and where the games themselves are some of the best they've ever been, yet players are still paying $60-70 per title, the same price point from decades ago. Real great job security here folks, make it make sense

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Love waking up to hear that Activision Blizzard laid off 1,900 employees in January while posting $13.3B in annual revenue, or that Microsoft cut 10,000 workers while Game Pass subscriptions soar, or that EA eliminated 870 positions while charging $70 for new releases like Dragon Age: The Veilguard — the same price point games have held since 2005; real great job security here folks, make it make sense

tone.rewrite: The gaming industry continues to experience significant layoffs despite record company profits and strong game quality, while consumer pricing has remained relatively static for decades. This disconnect between financial performance, product output, and employment stability raises questions about current industry resource allocation and long-term workforce sustainability.

humor.rewrite: Love waking up to hear about another massive layoff while companies report record profits and games are genuinely incredible, yet somehow the price tag hasn't budged since the PS2 era. grounding.rewrite: Love waking up to hear that there's been yet another massive layoff while companies report record profits and workers lack job security; the disconnect between corporate success and employee stability is frustrating and needs to change

The last four months are the first time since the gyms reopened post-pandemic that I’ve noticed sustained growth in size and strength. Out of the 18-month fog of a relationship slowly ending, recommitted to the gym, and seeing major differences. 

I have my time and energy back, and I love it.

narrativity.rewrite: Sustained growth in size and strength is now occurring for the first time since gyms reopened post-pandemic. The past 18 months were marked by a deteriorating relationship that obscured progress. Recommitment to the gym produces major differences. Time and energy are restored. This state is superior.

reading_level.rewrite:

I have my time and energy back. I'm so happy about it.

background.rewrite:

Time and energy reclaimed. Love it.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:

I have reclaimed my autonomy and capacity, and I'm genuinely grateful for it.

tone.rewrite:
The last four months have been a BREAKTHROUGH—the first time since gyms reopened that I'm actually SEEING real, undeniable growth in size and strength. I'm crawling out of an 18-month fog, clawing my way back to the gym, and the transformation is UNDENIABLE.

My time is MINE again. My energy is BACK. And I'm obsessed with how it feels.

humor.rewrite:

Four months in and I'm finally noticing actual gains—not the "I showed up twice" kind, but the real deal. Eighteen months of relationship fog cleared, recommitted to iron, and my muscles are filing a formal complaint about the sudden workload. 

My time and energy are back. Also my sanity. Highly recommend both.

grounding.rewrite:

I have my time and energy back, and I love it.

original: I hate how coy NFL broadcasters have gotten about sports gambling. Saying shit like “I’m sure that touchdown has a lot of folks happy watching at home” is so much less cool than just having an announcer foaming at the mouth with rage because his 14-leg parlay won’t hit (a player has a cold)

narrativity.rewrite: I was watching the game last Sunday when the commentator made this weird comment about a touchdown: "I'm sure that has a lot of folks happy watching at home." And I just sat there thinking—remember when announcers used to actually *care* about the outcome in an honest way? Now everything feels so sanitized. I miss the days when you'd hear an announcer absolutely *losing it* because his 14-leg parlay won't hit because some guy has a cold. That raw, unfiltered passion was way cooler than this coy tiptoeing around sports gambling the NFL broadcasters do now.

reading_level.rewrite: I hate how shy NFL broadcasters are about sports gambling now. They say stuff like "I bet that touchdown made people happy at home" instead of just letting announcers go crazy. Like, an announcer getting furious because his 14-bet parlay won't cash (because one player is sick) would be way more honest and fun.

background.rewrite: I hate how coy NFL broadcasters have gotten about sports gambling. The NFL has allowed gambling ads to explode during games, but announcers refuse to acknowledge it directly. Instead of being honest, they use awkward euphemisms like "I'm sure that touchdown has a lot of folks happy watching at home" — which is obviously code for "bettors just won money." It's so much less cool than just having an announcer foaming at the mouth with rage because his 14-leg parlay (a sequence of 14 connected bets) won't hit because one player has a cold. At least that would be authentic.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: I hate how coy broadcasters have become about acknowledging the financial stakes viewers have in outcomes. Alluding vaguely to audience investment is so much less authentic than openly expressing genuine emotional reactions tied to those stakes. The gap between what they clearly know and what they'll say feels dishonest.

tone.rewrite: NFL broadcasters have become increasingly indirect about sports gambling references. Phrases like "I'm sure that touchdown has a lot of folks happy watching at home" feel notably sanitized compared to a more straightforward approach—say, an announcer openly discussing how a missed play affects his parlay odds. The shift toward coded language represents a notable change in broadcast standards around gambling commentary.

humor.rewrite: I'm concerned about how evasive NFL broadcasters have become regarding sports gambling. Euphemistic language like "I'm sure that touchdown has a lot of folks happy watching at home" obscures the reality of what's happening. It would be more honest if broadcasters openly acknowledged the gambling stakes involved—including openly discussing how a missed play affects real people's bets, rather than hiding behind vague implications about viewer reactions.

grounding.rewrite: I hate how coy NFL broadcasters have gotten about sports gambling. They avoid directly acknowledging it exists, dancing around the topic instead of just being honest about how much it affects viewers and commentators personally. The sanitized approach strips away authenticity.

original:
275,000 new jobs in February!
To put that in perspective, average monthly job growth in the supposedly great 2017-19 economy was 181,000.
The recession many predicted, and some are hoping for, keeps not happening.
And if it exceeds expectations every month, maybe the expecters are too pessimistic.

narrativity.rewrite:
I'll be honest—I wasn't expecting those February numbers. 275,000 new jobs landed. That stopped me in my tracks because I remember when everyone was talking about 2017-19 as this golden economic period, averaging 181,000 jobs monthly. 

So here we are, outpacing that "supposedly great" era every single month. All those recession predictions? They haven't materialized. And I've started wondering: if we keep beating the forecasts month after month, maybe the issue isn't with the economy—maybe it's that the expectations being set are just too pessimistic to begin with.

reading_level.rewrite:
Aggregate employment expansion of 275,000 positions throughout February demonstrates substantive labor market resilience.

Contextualizing this trajectory against the annualized baseline of 181,000 monthly job creation during the purportedly robust 2017-19 economic period elucidates the magnitude of contemporary growth differentials.

The prognosticated recessionary contraction, which various economic commentators have anticipated—and certain stakeholders have ostensibly welcomed—continues to materialize absent anticipated deterioration metrics.

Consequently, if consecutive monthly employment figures systematically surpass forecasted parameters, one might reasonably posit that prevailing expectations frameworks embody inherent pessimistic bias.

background.rewrite:
275,000 new jobs in February!

For context: The U.S. economy added an average of 181,000 jobs per month during 2017-2019, a period many consider economically strong. February's 275,000 jobs significantly outpaces that baseline.

Many economists predicted a recession would occur by now. Some have even publicly hoped for one (often citing concerns about inflation). Yet the economy continues expanding instead of contracting.

If job growth keeps beating these pessimistic forecasts month after month, maybe the people making these predictions are being too negative about the economy's resilience.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Significant employment expansion continues to materialize.
Recent figures substantially surpass the historical performance baseline from comparable periods of economic strength.
Anticipated downturns remain unrealized despite widespread predictions.
Consistent outperformance may indicate that prevailing expectations warrant recalibration toward greater optimism.

tone.rewrite:
🚨 275,000 JOBS ADDED IN FEBRUARY — WE'RE CRUSHING IT! 🚨

Let that sink in. The "booming" 2017-19 economy? Average of just 181,000/month. We're DEMOLISHING that.

All those doomsayers screaming "recession incoming"? WRONG. Month after month, we keep OBLITERATING expectations.

Maybe — just MAYBE — the constant pessimism needs a reality check. The numbers don't lie.

humor.rewrite:
275,000 new jobs in February! 

Apparently that's what happens when you ignore all the very serious recession predictions from the "experts" who were absolutely certain we'd be in freefall by now. (Spoiler: we're not.)

For context, the supposedly golden 2017-19 era averaged 181,000 monthly jobs. So we're basically crushing that with the confidence of someone who studied the wrong chapter but aced the test anyway.

At what point do we consider that maybe—just maybe—the relentless doom-scrollers might be slightly, marginally, ever-so-slightly wrong about everything?

grounding.rewrite:
275,000 new jobs in February!

This exceeds the average monthly job growth from 2017-19, which was 181,000.

The predicted recession continues to not materialize.

Consistently beating expectations suggests forecasters may be underestimating economic resilience.

Their breakthrough proved that raw, DIY-produced rock from independent labels could dominate the charts, which directly paved the way for countless other alternative and outsider artists who previously had no path to mainstream success.

narrativity.rewrite: I picked up Rachel Nuwer's "I Feel Love" expecting a solid dive into psychedelic research. Two chapters in, I found myself staying up past midnight, completely absorbed.
