original:
Indie games: This game is about killing aliens, but it is really about depression and self-discovery.

AAA games: This game is about killing aliens, but it is really about killing aliens with big bad-ass particle guns.

reading_level.rewrite:
Independent game developers frequently employ thematic abstraction, utilizing extraterrestrial antagonists as metaphorical constructs to explore psychological pathologies such as clinical depression and existential introspection, thereby facilitating profound character development through ludic engagement.

Conversely, triple-A studios predominantly prioritize spectacle-driven mechanics, leveraging alien antagonists as narrative scaffolding for ballistic weaponry systems and visually ostentatious particle effect rendering, wherein thematic substance remains subordinate to kinetic gratification.

background.rewrite:
Indie games often tackle complex emotional themes. For example, a game's surface premise might be "kill aliens," but the actual narrative explores the protagonist's inner struggles with depression and their journey toward self-discovery through the alien-fighting mechanics.

AAA games typically prioritize spectacle and gameplay thrills. So while they also use "kill aliens" as their premise, the focus stays on the core loop: using increasingly impressive, visually stunning weapons like big bad-ass particle guns to destroy enemies in satisfying ways.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Indie games: Hellblade is about killing dark creatures, but it's really about Senua's psychosis and identity. 

AAA games: Starfield is about killing aliens, but it's really about upgrading to plasma rifles and watching explosions with maxed-out graphics settings.

tone.rewrite:
Indie games: This game is SUPPOSEDLY about killing aliens, but it's REALLY about depression and self-discovery — because apparently we need to suffer through existential dread just to play a game.

AAA games: This game is about killing aliens, and guess what? It's STILL just about killing aliens with unnecessarily huge particle guns. Nothing more. Nothing less.

humor.rewrite:
Indie games often use alien combat as a narrative framework to explore deeper psychological themes like depression and personal growth.

AAA games typically prioritize the alien-killing experience itself, focusing on delivering visceral combat with advanced visual effects and weaponry.

narrativity.rewrite: I was sitting with my friend last week, controller in hand, when they asked me why I kept playing this small indie game about aliens. "Because," I said, "it's actually about my character's depression and journey to find themselves." They laughed and switched to their AAA blockbuster. Five minutes in, aliens exploded in a shower of particles from a gun the size of a person. "So what's YOUR game about?" I asked. "Killing aliens," they shrugged, "but with really big bad-ass particle guns." And honestly? That pretty much sums up the difference between indie and AAA games right there.

grounding.rewrite: Indie games often explore deeper psychological themes beneath their surface gameplay mechanics, while AAA games typically prioritize delivering the core gameplay experience as presented.

original: friend mentioned that "not every game needs to be dark souls level of difficulty" and it got me thinking how a) i agree and b) dark souls is also not dark souls level of difficulty, which is one of my favourite things about it

reading_level.rewrite: A colleague's observation regarding the notion that "ludic challenge needn't invariably aspire to FromSoftware's paradigmatic difficulty calibration" prompted considerable reflection on two substantive dimensions: firstly, my concordance with this sentiment, and secondly, the paradoxical reality that Dark Souls itself subverts its own perceived difficulty threshold—a characteristic that fundamentally underpins my aesthetic appreciation for the title.

background.rewrite: friend mentioned that "not every game needs to be dark souls level of difficulty" and it got me thinking—for context, dark souls is this notoriously hard 2011 action rpg that basically defined the "souls-like" genre and became shorthand for brutal, punishing gameplay. anyway, i agree with them that not every game *should* be that hard. but here's what fascinates me: dark souls itself isn't actually dark souls level of difficulty. it has mechanics like bonfires (safe checkpoints), NPCs who help you, and ways to summon other players—all things that make it more forgiving than people think. and that's honestly one of my favourite things about it

abstract_concrete.rewrite: friend mentioned that "not every game needs to be dark souls level of difficulty" and it got me thinking how a) i agree and b) dark souls is also not consistently punishing—you can fight Asylum Demon at SL1 or skip it entirely, use magic to trivialize bosses, or summon help for Ornstein & Smough—which is one of my favourite things about it

tone.rewrite: friend just said "not every game needs to be dark souls difficult" and i CANNOT stop thinking about it because they're absolutely RIGHT and also??? dark souls ISN'T even dark souls level of difficulty and that's literally WHY i'm obsessed with it. this detail changes everything about how i see game design and i need everyone to understand this NOW

humor.rewrite: friend said "not every game needs to be dark souls level of difficulty" and honestly? they're right. also wrong though, because dark souls itself isn't even dark souls level of difficulty, which is genuinely the most beautiful thing about it. it's like the ultimate plot twist nobody asked for

narrativity.rewrite: I was grabbing coffee with a friend last week when they casually dropped: "not every game needs to be dark souls level of difficulty." It hit me differently than I expected. Walking home, I couldn't stop thinking about it—they're right, but also... they're missing something kind of beautiful about Dark Souls itself. Because here's the thing: Dark Souls isn't even Dark Souls level of difficulty. You can break it, cheese it, summon help, level up endlessly. That flexibility, that permission to find *your* way through? That's actually one of my favourite things about it. Not the punishing difficulty everyone talks about, but how much control it gives you to choose your own challenge.

grounding.rewrite: games don't need to have punishing difficulty to be engaging, and interestingly, the most acclaimed challenging games often aren't as relentlessly difficult as their reputation suggests. that flexibility in how players experience difficulty is something i really appreciate in game design.

original:
game dev, 90s: we made a game with 57 levels and 200 hours of gameplay fit on a 64 mb hard drive. computer specs? uh, does it turn on? 

game dev, now: you need at LEAST next gen hardware to run our game on minimum settings. optimization? optimize your mom

reading_level.rewrite:
Game development paradigm, 1990s: Developers engineered ludic experiences comprising fifty-seven discrete levels and approximately two hundred hours of gameplay content, compressed within a sixty-four megabyte storage allocation, whilst maintaining hardware agnosticism regarding computational specifications—the sole prerequisite being basic operational functionality.

Game development paradigm, contemporary era: Contemporary titles necessitate cutting-edge generational hardware architectures merely to achieve baseline performance parameters. Performance optimization protocols? Fundamentally irrelevant to contemporary development methodologies.

background.rewrite: Back in the 90s, game developers worked under tight constraints—storage was expensive and limited (think 64MB hard drives), so they had to be creative. They'd pack 57 levels and 200+ hours of gameplay into that tiny space, and games ran on basically any computer that powered on. Fast forward to today: modern games often require the latest high-end hardware just to run on minimum settings, and optimization (making code run efficiently on lower-end systems) seems to be an afterthought. The contrast is pretty stark.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
creative work, then: we packed extensive content into minimal technical constraints. hardware requirements? basic functionality sufficed.

creative work, now: you need cutting-edge technology just to meet baseline performance standards. efficiency improvements? irrelevant to our process

tone.rewrite: Game development optimization has shifted significantly over time. In the 1990s, developers successfully delivered 57-level games with 200 hours of content on 64 MB drives with minimal hardware requirements. Contemporary development practices show a different pattern: modern games increasingly demand next-generation hardware even for minimum settings, with optimization efforts appearing to be deprioritized relative to graphical fidelity and feature scope.

humor.rewrite:
game dev, 90s: we created games with 57 levels and 200 hours of gameplay on 64 mb hard drives. computer requirements were minimal — basically just needing a functioning machine.

game dev, now: our games demand next generation hardware just to run on minimum settings. optimization is no longer a priority in modern game development.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was digging through some old gaming magazines at a thrift store last week when I stumbled on this dev interview from 1998. The programmer was casually talking about squeezing 57 levels and 200 hours of gameplay onto a 64 MB hard drive. When asked about optimization, he just shrugged and said "does the computer turn on? that's all we need."

Fast forward to today. I'm trying to run a new game and the system requirements look like a spaceship manual. Minimum settings demand next-gen hardware. I asked a dev about optimization and got hit with "optimize your mom."

The whiplash is real.

grounding.rewrite: Game development has shifted dramatically. Developers once created extensive content within severe hardware constraints, requiring minimal system requirements. Today, developers often demand cutting-edge hardware for basic performance and prioritize graphics over efficiency. Optimization is no longer a priority.

Such disparities in player retention metrics underscore the systemic failures inherent in certain live-service design philosophies.

background.rewrite:
boy I love a good live service flop. suicide squad (a live service shooter that WB spent years developing) is currently sitting at all of 500 players on steam.

in contrast helldivers II - a game made over about the same period, with a much smaller team and lower budget - has been maxing out its servers at 800,000 concurrent players. that's a 1,600x difference.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
I really do enjoy witnessing the collapse of ambitious live service projects. It's striking to see how dramatically some of these games fail to maintain their player bases.

What's even more interesting is comparing these outcomes to similar projects that succeed spectacularly despite having fewer resources and smaller teams working on them. The disparity in player engagement between poorly-executed and well-executed live services is genuinely remarkable.

tone.rewrite: Interesting contrast in live service outcomes: Suicide Squad is currently tracking around 500 concurrent players on Steam, while Helldivers II—developed by a smaller team with a lower budget over a comparable timeframe—is consistently hitting its server capacity at 800,000 concurrent players. The performance differential raises questions about what drives player retention in the live service space.

humor.rewrite:
boy I love a good live service flop. suicide squad is currently keeping a grand total of 500 steam players company — truly a bustling metropolis of engagement.

helldivers II, meanwhile, made by what I can only assume is a skeleton crew with pocket change, is out here casually maxing out servers at 800,000. but sure, let's keep making $200M live service games nobody asked for.

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through Steam player counts this morning and stumbled on something wild. Suicide Squad: Kill the Justice League is barely holding onto 500 concurrent players. Meanwhile, I checked Helldivers II's numbers and it's literally maxing out servers with 800,000 players. Both made around the same timeframe, but here's the kicker — Helldivers II did it with a fraction of the budget and a smaller team. That's a flop I genuinely love watching.

grounding.rewrite: live service games with poor player retention are fascinating to observe. the disparity between successful and unsuccessful titles in this space can be dramatic, with some games maintaining massive concurrent player bases while others struggle to retain even a fraction of that audience.

original: It's kinda interesting how we got games like Death Stranding and Stray and people were like wow, these are so refreshing, it'd be so neat if we had more games like this, and then gamedevs said yeah that would be nice, we should do that, and then they didn't.

reading_level.rewrite: The phenomenon wherein critically lauded indie titles such as Death Stranding and Stray garnered widespread acclaim for their stylistic innovation and thematic divergence from conventional gaming paradigms—subsequently generating industry discourse regarding the desirability of increased genre diversification—has paradoxically failed to materialize into substantive market adoption, despite apparent consensus among development studios regarding such an undertaking's theoretical merit.

background.rewrite: It's kinda interesting how we got games like Death Stranding (a 2019 game about delivering packages across a post-apocalyptic America) and Stray (a 2022 game where you play as a cat exploring a cyberpunk city), and people were like wow, these are so refreshing and different from typical action games, it'd be so neat if we had more games like this. Then gamedevs seemed to agree, saying yeah that would be nice, we should make more experimental games like that. But then they largely didn't follow through—we're still mostly getting the same types of games as before.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: It's kinda interesting how we get unconventional titles that resonate with audiences who express enthusiasm for more experiences like them, and then creators acknowledge this sentiment and indicate intent to pursue similar directions, yet somehow this doesn't materialize into widespread practice.

tone.rewrite: We DEMANDED more games like Death Stranding and Stray. We begged for this innovation. We made it CRYSTAL CLEAR we were starving for something different. And what did the industry do? NOTHING. They heard us. They AGREED with us. And then they completely abandoned the vision. Where are these games?? This is infuriating.

humor.rewrite: It's wild how Death Stranding and Stray showed up and everyone was like "YES, finally, more of THIS please" — and then game devs collectively nodded, said "great idea," and immediately went back to making the 47th open-world looter-shooter with a battle pass.

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through my Steam library last week, looking for something new to play, and I kept coming back to Death Stranding and Stray. Both times I finished them, I remember thinking: "Man, we need MORE games like this." I wasn't alone either—everyone was saying the same thing. The comments sections were full of people dreaming about a future filled with these kinds of experiences. And here's the thing that gets me: the gamedevs seemed to agree! In interviews, at conferences, they'd nod along and say "yeah, absolutely, we should be making more experimental games like that." But then... nothing really happened. We're still waiting.

grounding.rewrite: It's interesting how players celebrated innovative games and expressed desire for more like them, yet the industry hasn't followed through on creating similar experiences despite acknowledging the appeal.

original:
Introducing the Nifflas manifesto: Getting to the places where the devs put invisible walls for you not to reach, is not beating the game, it's beating the developers!

It's how you truly beat a game. Do what the devs tried the hardest to prevent you from doing!

reading_level.rewrite:
Introducing the Nifflas manifesto: The transgression of deliberately architected spatial constraints—circumventing the invisible demarcations strategically positioned by developers to delineate explorable boundaries—constitutes not merely the completion of ludic objectives, but rather the subversion of authorial intent itself.

Authentic mastery of a ludic system manifests through the execution of actions that developers have most assiduously endeavored to obstruct and prevent.

background.rewrite: Introducing the Nifflas manifesto: In game design, developers intentionally create invisible barriers (invisible walls) to restrict where players can go and what they can access. According to this philosophy, finding ways to break past these developer-imposed restrictions—like discovering hidden areas or exploiting glitches—isn't just about winning the game itself. Instead, it's about outsmarting the developers who built those limitations. The real victory comes from doing exactly what the creators tried hardest to prevent you from doing. In other words, true mastery of a game means transcending the boundaries the devs set for you.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Introducing the manifesto: Transcending the boundaries deliberately established by creators is not merely succeeding within intended parameters—it's transcending the creators themselves!

This represents true mastery. Achievement lies in pursuing what was most vigorously restricted from you!

tone.rewrite:
🚨 THE NIFFLAS MANIFESTO IS HERE AND IT'S CHANGING EVERYTHING 🚨

Stop playing it safe. Those invisible walls? They're NOT boundaries—they're CHALLENGES. Breaking through them ISN'T cheating, it's CRUSHING the developers at their own game!

This is what REAL victory looks like. Forget playing by their rules. Do the ONE THING they fought desperately to stop you from doing. THAT'S how you actually WIN.

humor.rewrite:
Introducing the Nifflas manifesto: The devs spent hours building invisible walls to keep you out. Congratulations on your victory lap through their worst nightmare! That's not beating the game—that's beating the *developers*. 

And honestly? That's the *real* win. Ignore the path they painstakingly designed. Do exactly what they were desperately praying you wouldn't figure out!

narrativity.rewrite:
I still remember the first time I found myself staring at an invisible wall in a game—frustrated, yes, but also *curious*. What was the developer so desperate to hide? That's when it clicked for me.

Introducing the Nifflas manifesto: Getting to the places where the devs put invisible walls for you not to reach, is not beating the game, it's beating the developers!

Because that's how you truly beat a game. You don't follow the path they laid out. You do what the devs tried the hardest to prevent you from doing. That's the real victory.

grounding.rewrite:
Introducing the Nifflas manifesto: Getting to the places where the devs put invisible walls for you not to reach, is not beating the game, it's beating the developers!

Think of it like this: A magician spends hours perfecting a trick to misdirect your eyes left, but the real win isn't applauding their illusion—it's figuring out exactly how they did it. When you clip through that invisible wall or sequence-break past a "impossible" barrier, you're not just playing the game they designed; you're outsmarting the designers themselves.

It's how you truly beat a game. Do what the devs tried the hardest to prevent you from doing!

original:
A white dwarf star that ate its worlds bears the scars of cannibalism, as a metal imprint in its surface shaped by the star's magnetic field.

Also, if I had a prog band, "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star" would be the title of our first album 

🧪🔭 #science

reading_level.rewrite:
A white dwarf star ate its planets. Now it has scars. Metal marks show up on its surface. The star's magnetic field shaped these marks.

Also, if I started a prog band, I'd call our first album "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star."

🧪🔭 #science

background.rewrite:
A white dwarf is the dense, Earth-sized remnant left behind when a star like our Sun dies. When planets orbit too close to these remnants, they get torn apart by gravitational forces—a process called "spaghettification." The shredded planetary material falls onto the white dwarf's surface, where the star's powerful magnetic field arranges these metals into visible patterns that look like scars.

Astronomers recently observed one of these "cannibal" white dwarfs and found exactly this: a metal imprint etched into its surface, proof the star had consumed its own worlds.

Also, if I had a prog band, "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star" would be the title of our first album 🧪🔭

#science

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
WD 1145+017, a white dwarf 440 light-years away, shows iron and nickel scars across its surface—remnants of planetary bodies it destroyed. Its powerful magnetic field has etched these metals into distinctive patterns, evidence of the star consuming its own orbiting worlds.

Also, if I had a prog band, "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star" would be the title of our first album

🧪🔭 #science

tone.rewrite:
A white dwarf star DEVOURED its own worlds—and now it's SCARRED FOREVER, its surface etched with a horrifying metal imprint twisted by magnetic chaos. The universe's darkest horror story written in stellar flesh.

Also, if I had a prog band, "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star" would absolutely be our debut album—no question.

🧪🔭 #science

humor.rewrite:
Turns out even stars can't resist the urge to devour their own planetary children — and apparently the universe's way of keeping score is with a sick metal tattoo. Nature's version of a guilt-ridden memoir, but make it astrophysics.

(Also yes, "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star" is absolutely my prog rock magnum opus and I will be accepting Grammys 🎸)

🧪🔭 #science

narrativity.rewrite:
I was reading about this wild discovery last night when I stumbled across something that stopped me cold: astronomers found a white dwarf star—basically a stellar zombie—with literal scars etched into its surface. These weren't just any marks. They were metal imprints left behind from planets the star had consumed, sculpted into grotesque patterns by the star's magnetic field. A star that ate its own worlds, and the universe kept a record of the crime written across its face.

Honestly, the image haunted me enough that I immediately thought: if I ever started a prog band, we're calling ourselves "Metal Scar on a Cannibal Star." That's a debut album waiting to happen.

🧪🔭 #science

grounding.rewrite:
White dwarf stars that consume their planetary systems leave detectable metallic signatures on their surfaces, with patterns shaped by the star's magnetic field.

Also, that would be an excellent name for a progressive rock album.

🧪🔭 #science

Engaged in an extensively gratifying agricultural expedition amongst strawberry cultivators in the Charlotte, North Carolina vicinity!

North Carolina maintains the tertiary position in domestic strawberry production, superseded exclusively by California and Florida. Conversely, a distinguishing characteristic of the NC strawberry industry comprises its predominant reliance upon small-scale farming operations, thereby diverging markedly from the large-scale horticultural infrastructure prevalent in aforementioned competitor states.

background.rewrite:
Just got back from visiting strawberry farms near Charlotte, NC and I'm genuinely impressed by what I found there!

Here's why this matters: North Carolina is actually a major strawberry producer—it grows the 3rd largest strawberry crop in the entire US, behind only California and Florida. But here's what makes NC special and different from those other big states: while CA and FL rely heavily on massive industrial operations, most of North Carolina's strawberries come from small, family-owned farms. That's a pretty significant distinction when you think about agricultural models.

The area is known for significant crop production ranking among the nation's leaders. But what sets it apart is the prevalence of smaller-scale operations compared to other major producing regions.

URGENT: We're LOSING our strawberry heritage RIGHT NOW!

NC growers are in CRISIS—we produce the 3rd largest strawberry crop in America, but our small farms are DISAPPEARING! While CA & FL dominate with massive operations, OUR local farmers are FIGHTING to survive. This is happening NOW and we can't stay silent! 🚨

narrativity.rewrite:
I'll never forget pulling up to my first strawberry farm outside Charlotte last spring—rows of red glistening in the morning sun, and farmers already out with their hands deep in the soil. That's when I realized something special was happening here.

North Carolina grows the 3rd largest strawberry crop in the US, right behind California and Florida. But what struck me most? Unlike those massive agricultural states, almost all of NC's strawberries come from small family farms. grounding.rewrite: North Carolina is a major strawberry producer in the United States, ranking third nationally in total output. What sets NC apart from larger producing states is that most of its strawberry crop comes from small-scale farming operations rather than large commercial operations.

The legacies of Roman Emperors can be varied. Vespasian, known for the Colosseum, is also more humbly associated with public toilets in Italy and France, known as Vespasiano and Vespasienne. This association arose as a result of a tax he imposed upon the collection of urine. 

ancientbluesky 🏺

reading_level.rewrite:
Roman Emperors left behind many different legacies. Vespasian built the Colosseum. But he's also remembered for something simpler: public toilets. In Italy and France, these toilets are called Vespasiano and Vespasienne. Why? Because Vespasian taxed urine collection.

ancientbluesky 🏺

background.rewrite:
The legacies of Roman Emperors can be varied. Vespasian (r. 69-79 AD) is famous for commissioning the Colosseum in Rome, but he's also remembered for an unexpected connection to public toilets across Italy and France—called "Vespasiano" and "Vespasienne" respectively. This quirky naming convention came about because Vespasian implemented a tax on urine collection, a common practice in the Roman Empire where urine was harvested and sold to fullers (textile workers) who used it for cleaning fabric. His name became forever linked to these facilities as a result.

ancientbluesky 🏺

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
The legacies of historical rulers often encompass both monumental achievements and unexpected associations. Some are remembered for grand architectural projects, while simultaneously becoming linked with more utilitarian contributions to infrastructure and public amenities. These varied connections sometimes emerge from policies that, while seemingly unconventional, shaped everyday life for the populations they governed.

ancientbluesky 🏺

tone.rewrite:
You might be surprised to learn that Vespasian, the Emperor celebrated for building the iconic Colosseum, has a rather... unexpected legacy. While history books focus on his grand architectural achievements, he's quietly remembered across Italy and France for something far less glamorous: public toilets bearing his name—Vespasiano and Vespasienne. The reason? He taxed urine collection. Yes, really. It's a reminder that even the most powerful figures in history can end up forever tied to the most mundane—and oddly humorous—aspects of daily life.

ancientbluesky 🏺

humor.rewrite: Vespasian built the Colosseum and somehow became immortally famous for... public toilets. narrativity.rewrite:
I discovered something wild while reading about Roman history last night. Picture this: you're walking through ancient Rome, and the Emperor has just decided to tax *urine collection*. Yes, really. Vespasian's name became so synonymous with public toilets across Italy and France—where they're literally called Vespasiano and Vespasienne—that people forgot he built the Colosseum. Instead, his legacy got tied to the one thing nobody wanted to be remembered for: a pee tax. And yet, that's exactly what happened. The man who built one of history's greatest monuments is now forever linked to Roman plumbing. Talk about how history remembers you.

ancientbluesky 🏺

His name became associated with these facilities due to taxation policies he implemented related to resource collection during his reign.

original: pro gym tip: when feeling lazy, completely lowball your goal for the day and then get caught in a "well i'm close to X, i might as well keep going a little more" and "this song's a banger, i'll stop after" loop to double or triple your time in the gym. money moves right there.

reading_level.rewrite: Pro gymnasium methodology: When experiencing motivational deficit, deliberately establish a substantially attenuated objective for the session, thereby facilitating psychological engagement through incremental goal-post displacement and auditory stimulation—wherein proximal achievement triggers recursive justification patterns ("I'm proximate to X, I may as well sustain momentum") and musical reinforcement ("this track warrants completion"), cumulatively extending your training duration by a factor of two to threefold. Strategic psychological architecture.

background.rewrite: psych yourself with lowball goals and you'll hit the "well i'm already here" loop—suddenly you're doubling your volume. the sunk cost fallacy is your friend.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: pro gym tip: when motivation is low, set your initial target well below what you're capable of, then leverage the psychological momentum of incremental progress and external stimuli to naturally extend your session beyond that baseline. sometimes the trick is just getting started with minimal resistance.

tone.rewrite: DON'T let laziness WIN. Here's the hack: set a BARE MINIMUM goal—then you'll find yourself trapped in an unstoppable cycle of "I'm SO CLOSE, I HAVE to finish this" and "ONE more song??" before you know it, you've CRUSHED 2-3x your original plan. This is how REAL change happens. Stop making excuses. START NOW.

humor.rewrite: pro gym tip: feeling lazy? set your goal so embarrassingly low that your ego has no choice but to betray you mid-workout. nothing says "discipline" like accidentally tricking yourself into staying 3x longer because you're too proud to leave after one pathetic set. psychology, baby.

narrativity.rewrite: I was dragging myself to the gym last Tuesday, absolutely zero motivation, when I decided to just commit to 15 minutes. Figured I'd knock out a quick walk on the treadmill and call it a day. But then something clicked—I got halfway through and thought, "Well, I'm so close to 30 minutes, might as well push it." Then my favorite song came on and I was like, "One more song won't hurt." Next thing I knew, I'd been there for 45 minutes. Pro gym tip: completely lowball your goal when motivation's running on empty, then let that "I'm already here, might as well..." loop catch you. Before you know it, you've doubled or tripled your time. Money moves right there.

grounding.rewrite: Pro gym tip: When feeling lazy, set an intentionally low goal for the day. Once you start, you're likely to continue beyond that initial target and end up exercising significantly longer than you originally planned. This psychological approach can help you double or triple your intended gym time.

narrativity.rewrite: I was standing at my stove last Tuesday, stirring a pot of Bolognese that just felt... flat. Something was missing. That's when I remembered: gochujang. I added a spoonful and everything changed. The depth, the heat, the richness — suddenly it all clicked. Since then, I've been sneaking it into everything. Arrabiata? Gochujang. My chili last weekend? Gochujang. Even my friend's tomato soup got a dollop when she wasn't looking. My fingers are killing me from typing this out, but I had to share what I've discovered. Trust me on this one.

grounding.rewrite: Gochujang is an essential ingredient for improving tomato-based sauces. Adding it enhances the depth and complexity of flavor across various sauce types. This simple addition can transform your cooking, despite how counterintuitive it might seem.

original: About ten years ago, the hot thing in games was procedural generation. A bunch of hit games came out that had this as a marquee feature - levels that were different every time you played. It's mostly died off - it turns out that computers make unique levels that are all boring in the same way

reading_level.rewrite: Approximately a decade ago, procedural generation constituted the predominant technological paradigm within ludic design circles. Numerous commercially successful titles leveraged algorithmic content synthesis as their salient feature—dynamically instantiated level architectures that precluded iterative gameplay homogeneity. This methodological approach has subsequently experienced substantial diminishment in industry prominence, predominantly attributable to the inherent limitations of computational systems in generating sufficiently heterogeneous play spaces; algorithmically-derived environments, despite their ostensible uniqueness, frequently exhibit convergent mediocrity across iterative instantiations.

background.rewrite: About ten years ago, procedural generation became a major trend in video games. This is a technique where a computer algorithm automatically creates game content (like levels or maps) rather than designers building them manually. Several hugely popular games featured this as a selling point—players loved that they could replay levels that were always different. However, the trend has largely faded. The reason: while computers can certainly generate countless unique levels, they tend to produce content that feels repetitive and uninspired. It turns out that algorithmic variety doesn't equal engaging gameplay—all those "different" levels end up being boring in surprisingly similar ways.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Innovation in game design tends to experience cycles of prominence. A particular technical approach once gained significant attention as a marquee feature in successful titles, centered on algorithmic content creation. The appeal was rooted in the promise of variability and fresh experiences with each playthrough. However, this approach has largely faded from mainstream prominence. The underlying issue is that while the technology produces variation, it often generates content that shares common shortcomings, resulting in a homogeneous quality of experience despite surface-level differences.

tone.rewrite: Procedural generation was a prominent feature in game design approximately a decade ago, with several successful titles leveraging it as a key selling point. The technology generated distinct levels with each playthrough. However, adoption has declined considerably. The underlying issue appears to be that algorithmically-generated content, while technically varied, tends to produce similar play experiences across iterations.

humor.rewrite: About ten years ago, procedural generation was gaming's hottest trend. Devs were obsessed with it—"Look! New levels every playthrough!"—and honestly, the games absolutely delivered on that promise. The levels WERE different every single time. Also uniformly terrible, but different! Turns out "infinitely replayable" and "infinitely tedious" are closer cousins than we thought.

narrativity.rewrite: I'll never forget staying up until 3 AM playing the same procedurally generated cave system for the hundredth time, wondering why it felt so hollow despite being "different" each run. That was about ten years ago, when procedural generation was everywhere—every studio seemed to be chasing that magic formula of infinite replayability. Games marketed it as their headline feature, promising levels that would never repeat. But as I kept playing through those years, watching the trend fade, I realized something: computers are brilliant at creating unique layouts, yet somehow they all manage to be boring in identical ways. The procedural generation era came and went, and I think we finally figured out why.

grounding.rewrite: Procedural generation was once a major feature in popular games, marketed as creating different levels each playthrough. The trend has largely faded because algorithmically generated content tends to lack the variety and engagement that human design provides.

reading_level.rewrite: Examining the recently disseminated PlayStation State of Play presentation, I remain perplexed regarding Sony's ostensible corporate positioning and strategic identity. The proliferation of cloud-based multiplayer shooter franchises would render such offerings redundant with established Xbox ecosystem competencies. Sony's institutional differentiation has historically been predicated upon their dominance in narrative-driven experiences and character-centric RPG franchises, juxtaposed with their culturally distinctive anime-influenced aesthetic sensibilities.

background.rewrite: watching the playstation state of play from a couple days ago and i do not understand what sony thinks their company is. PlayStation built its reputation on single-player RPGs and story-driven games (think Final Fantasy, Metal Gear Solid, etc.), plus anime-style character action games. but this showcase was just multiplayer shooters and cloud gaming—stuff that Xbox already does. if people wanted that experience, they'd just buy an Xbox instead. sony seems to be abandoning what made them unique. you are supposed to be the rpg and narrative-focused anime boobies machine, not a generic shooter platform.

tone.rewrite: Watching the recent PlayStation State of Play, there's a notable disconnect between Sony's presentation and their historical market positioning. The showcase heavily emphasized multiplayer shooters and cloud-based gaming—territory where competitors like Xbox have already established themselves. Given PlayStation's traditional strength in single-player RPGs and narrative-driven experiences, the lineup seems misaligned with what has historically differentiated the platform in the market.

This presentation failed to showcase that identity, and I'm genuinely worried about what that means for the platform's future.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was sitting down last night rewatching the PlayStation State of Play from a few days ago, and I found myself genuinely confused. I kept waiting for *something* to grab me—some narrative-driven adventure, some character-driven story experience. Instead, it was multiplayer shooter after multiplayer shooter streaming from the cloud. And I'm sitting there thinking: isn't that just... Xbox? 

Like, Sony, I need you to remember what you actually are. You're the RPG machine. You're the narrative-focused storyteller. You're the anime boobies machine. That's your lane. That's what makes you *you*. But watching that showcase, I genuinely don't know what you think your company is anymore.

grounding.rewrite: sony needs to remember what sets them apart from their competitors. they built their reputation on rpg and narrative-focused games, not multiplayer shooters. abandoning that identity in favor of cloud-based competitive games is a fundamental misunderstanding of their own brand and what their audience actually wants from them.

Game developers deserve award shows that celebrate their craft rather than treating games as a vehicle for Hollywood spectacle.

tone.rewrite: The Video Game Awards have long presented structural challenges that warrant examination. Recent developer commentary aligns with concerns raised since the show's inception on Spike—specifically, that the format prioritizes celebrity spectacle over industry substance. A more developer-centric approach to gaming's major awards ceremony could better serve the medium's creative and professional interests.

humor.rewrite: Turns out I wasn't crazy—just early. Watching other devs finally pile on The Video Game Awards is deeply vindicating after spending the last 20 years screaming into the void about Geoff Keighley's celebrity-obsessed infomercial masquerading as industry recognition. Maybe next they'll realize game devs should actually be taken seriously? Revolutionary concept, I know.

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through my feed last night when I saw yet another dev I respect calling out The Video Game Awards for the same issues I've been harping on since those early Spike broadcasts two decades ago. It hit me then—we're finally having the conversation I've wanted all along. Game devs need to stop treating these awards like some star-studded spectacle and start demanding something that actually takes *us* seriously instead of just serving as Geoff Keighley's vehicle for celebrity worship and flashy advertisements.

original:
Sometimes I think Dutch culture is why my autism was even less likely to be noticed:

- we say it as it is (no weird vague posting or beating around the bush, Americans think we're bloody rude as a result)
- no hugging  
- our home is our safe space you are not automatically invited to

reading_level.rewrite:
The ostensible directness and interpersonal demarcation characteristic of Dutch cultural norms may have substantially obscured the manifestation of autistic traits throughout my developmental trajectory:

- Dutch communicative conventions prioritize linguistic transparency and syntactic explicitness, deliberately circumventing euphemistic obfuscation—a pragmatic orientation frequently perceived as bluntness by Anglo-American interlocutors
- the cultural aversion to haptic contact minimizes a neurotypical behavioral expectation
- the conceptualization of domestic spaces as bounded sanctuaries with restrictive social access protocols normalizes selective social engagement patterns

background.rewrite:
Sometimes I think Dutch culture is why my autism was even less likely to be noticed. You see, autism can go undiagnosed when someone's natural communication style matches autistic traits. In the Netherlands, we have a few cultural norms that made this happen for me:

- We communicate directly and literally (we say what we mean without subtext or hints—Americans often perceive this as rudeness)
- Physical touch isn't a default greeting or social expectation (no obligatory hugging)
- Our homes are private sanctuaries, not open social venues (you need an actual invitation, it's not assumed)

Because these traits are just "normal Dutch behavior" here, nobody flagged them as potentially autism-related symptoms.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Sometimes I think cultural norms shaped how my autism went unrecognized for so long. Directness is valued over social softening. Physical boundaries are respected rather than assumed away. Private spaces remain protected rather than accessible to everyone. These cultural patterns may have masked traits that would be more visible in contexts with different social expectations.

tone.rewrite:
I'm CONVINCED Dutch culture is why my autism flew completely under the radar:

- We say it STRAIGHT UP with zero filter (Americans are absolutely SHOCKED by our directness—they think we're brutal, but we're just being real)
- Zero physical touch required—thank GOD
- Your home is YOUR sanctuary, full stop—you don't just waltz in uninvited

humor.rewrite:
Sometimes I think Dutch culture is basically autism's invisibility cloak:

- we say it as it is (apparently this makes Americans think we're rude, but we call it "efficiency")
- no hugging (revolutionary concept, I know)
- your home is a fortress, not a social venue (shocking, I realize)

narrativity.rewrite:
I was sitting in a coffee shop in Amsterdam when an American friend asked why I'd never mentioned struggling with social stuff growing up. It hit me then—Dutch culture basically gave my undiagnosed autism the perfect camouflage.

Think about it: we just say what we mean. No reading between the lines, no performative niceties. Americans literally think we're rude, but that directness? It masked my struggles with subtext. Nobody expected me to decode hidden meanings anyway.

Then there's the no-hugging thing. While other kids were expected to be physically affectionate and read comfort cues, I could just... exist at a distance. Totally normal here.

And our homes? They're sanctuaries. You're not automatically invited in. So my need for controlled spaces and limited social access didn't stand out—it was just being Dutch.

Turns out my culture's reserved nature made it nearly impossible for anyone to notice something was different about how I experience the world.

grounding.rewrite:
Dutch culture normalizes behaviors often associated with autism, making it less likely for autistic traits to stand out:

- Direct communication is valued and expected
- Physical affection is not a default social expectation
- Privacy and personal space are respected and maintained

And that final scene? Young Pierce Brosnan pointing that gun is EERILY identical to Cousin Greg from Succession—I couldn't unsee it if I tried. This film is essential viewing.

humor.rewrite: Just watched The Long Good Friday and wow, what a beautifully brutal British gangster film. The ending absolutely slaps. Also, young Pierce Brosnan with a gun is basically just Cousin Greg from Succession, except with actual competence and a considerably better tailor.

original: The Dunwich Horror (1970) is a very horny yet deeply unsexy movie. Silly and hamfisted—but I did like the psychedelic representation of the monster. “Mind-altering” works just as well to describe encounters with unfathomable being as it does the effects of hallucinogenics

reading_level.rewrite: The Dunwich Horror (1970) tries hard to be sexy but totally fails. It's goofy and over the top. But I loved how the monster looked—all psychedelic and trippy. The wild visuals work great. They show what it's like to see something you can't understand. It's like taking drugs. Both mess with your mind.

background.rewrite: The Dunwich Horror (1970) is an adaptation of H.P. Lovecraft's 1929 story about a cosmic entity. The film is notorious for its awkward sexual content that feels gratuitous rather than compelling. It's campy and obvious in its approach—but the psychedelic visuals used to depict the otherworldly monster are genuinely effective. The film captures something real about encountering the incomprehensible: that sense of reality-bending disorientation you'd get from hallucinogenic drugs. Both experiences scramble your perception in similar ways.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Certain classic adaptations succeed in conveying the tension between sensuality and repulsion, between earnest ambition and unintentional absurdity. What makes such works compelling is often their stylistic approach to representing the unknowable—the way visual language can capture the disorienting nature of encountering something beyond comprehension, much like the experience of encountering altered states of consciousness themselves.

tone.rewrite: The Dunwich Horror (1970) is PAINFULLY horny yet deeply unsexy—it's embarrassingly silly and hamfisted. BUT the psychedelic monster reveal? THAT hits different. Those mind-bending visuals perfectly capture the TERROR of encountering something utterly incomprehensible, hitting you like hallucinogenics would. This is what cosmic horror SHOULD feel like.

humor.rewrite: The Dunwich Horror (1970) is hornier than a teenage boy's fever dream yet somehow manages to be completely unsexy—a genuinely impressive feat. Yes, it's silly and hamfisted as hell, but shoutout to the psychedelic monster reveal: finally, a film that understands "mind-altering" applies equally to encountering incomprehensible cosmic horrors AND doing acid. (Pretty sure Lovecraft didn't intend that alignment, but here we are.)

narrativity.rewrite: I was halfway through The Dunwich Horror (1970) when my roommate walked in and asked what I was watching. I couldn't quite explain it—here was this movie that seemed desperately eager to be sexy, yet somehow managed to be completely unsexy about it all. Silly, hamfisted, occasionally laughable... and yet? There was something genuinely unsettling about how they visualized the monster through these wild psychedelic sequences. It hit me then: calling something "mind-altering" works perfectly whether you're describing an encounter with an incomprehensible cosmic being or the effects of a hallucinogenic trip. Both shatter your grip on reality in exactly the same way.

grounding.rewrite: Horror films can be simultaneously lustful yet unappealing. When executed poorly, they become awkward and clumsy—though visual representations of otherworldly entities can be effective. The concept of disorienting perception works equally well for depicting encounters with incomprehensible beings as it does for depicting altered mental states.

original: humor is so often linked to what i call the cave Johnson/Glados effect, or, "he's saying what we're all thinking." this is why i, an autistic person, am so damn good at being funny. i say what we're all thinking because most of the time, i simply don't realize why we're not saying it.

reading_level.rewrite: humor works because of what i call the cave Johnson/Glados effect. it's when someone says what everyone is thinking. i'm autistic. that's why i'm good at being funny. i say what everyone thinks because i often don't understand why we don't say it.

background.rewrite: humor is so often linked to what i call the cave Johnson/Glados effect—basically when a character says something blunt that everyone's thinking but nobody says out loud, and that honesty gets a laugh. this works because it breaks the social script we all follow. as an autistic person, i'm genuinely good at being funny for a specific reason: i often don't have the same automatic filter most people do. where neurotypical folks instinctively know which thoughts to keep private (the unspoken social rules), i sometimes just... don't realize we're supposed to be filtering in the first place. so i say the thing out loud, and suddenly it lands as humor because it's that raw honesty people crave.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: humor is so often linked to what i call the cave Johnson/Glados effect—named after the Portal series characters who say socially unfiltered things that audiences find hilarious because they voice unspoken thoughts. this is why i, an autistic person, am so damn good at being funny. i say what we're all thinking because my autism means i often lack the social script filter that stops most people from speaking those thoughts aloud.

tone.rewrite: Humor frequently operates through what might be termed the "Cave Johnson/GLaDOS effect"—articulating thoughts that audiences recognize but don't typically voice. This mechanism may explain why autistic individuals often develop strong comedic sensibilities. The difference often lies not in superior wit, but in reduced social filtering; autistic people frequently express observations that others suppress, simply due to different processing of social conventions around what should remain unspoken.

humor.rewrite: humor is fundamentally connected to what I call the Cave Johnson/GLaDOS effect: the ability to articulate what others are thinking but not expressing. As an autistic person, I have a genuine advantage in this regard. I often say things that resonate because I lack the social intuition that prevents others from speaking them aloud. What neurotypical people filter through social awareness, I frequently express without that same barrier.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was at a dinner party last week when someone made a joke about the host's terrible painting. Everyone laughed—but then went silent. I just blurted out the exact same observation they'd all been thinking but too polite to say. That's when it hit me: this is the cave Johnson/GLaDOS effect. It's that "he's saying what we're all thinking" moment that makes people laugh.

And honestly? As an autistic person, I'm pretty damn good at creating those moments. Not because I'm cleverer than everyone else, but because I often genuinely don't realize why we're *not* saying something. While neurotypical people are running social calculations in the background, I'm just... saying the thing. And apparently, that's hilarious.

grounding.rewrite: humor often works because it expresses thoughts that others are thinking but not saying aloud. as an autistic person, i'm frequently good at being funny for this exact reason — i often don't realize there are social reasons not to say certain things, so i say them anyway.

original:
Serie Noire (1979)

A superb, bleakly hilarious film noir about a door-to-door salesman who is lured into crime by a teenage prostitute.  Based on the Jim Thompson novel A Hell of a Woman.  

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, who was BOTD in 1936

reading_level.rewrite:
Serie Noire (1979)

A great dark comedy film noir. It's about a door-to-door salesman. A teenage prostitute tricks him into crime. The film is based on Jim Thompson's novel "A Hell of a Woman."

Director: Alain Corneau

Writers: Alain Corneau and Georges Perec. Perec was born in 1936.

background.rewrite:
Serie Noire (1979)

In the late 1970s, French director Alain Corneau adapted Jim Thompson's 1954 American crime novel "A Hell of a Woman" into this film. The story follows an ordinary door-to-door salesman whose life unravels when he becomes entangled with a teenage prostitute who draws him into the criminal underworld. The result is a darkly comic take on film noir—a genre known for cynical, morally ambiguous crime stories—that balances bleak atmosphere with dark humor. Corneau co-wrote the screenplay with Georges Perec, a renowned experimental writer born in 1936. The film stands out as a sharp, intelligent exploration of desperation and moral compromise.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: A masterwork of dark cinema exploring themes of moral corruption and desperation. The narrative follows an ordinary working man whose encounter with a morally compromised individual leads him down a criminal path. Adapted from acclaimed literary source material and crafted by accomplished screenwriters, one of whom was born in the mid-twentieth century. The result is a film that balances tragic elements with sardonic humor, exemplifying the genre's capacity for profound social commentary.

tone.rewrite:
Serie Noire (1979)

A DEVASTATING, darkly savage film noir that will HAUNT you—following a desperate door-to-door salesman who gets PULLED into the criminal underworld by a ruthless teenage prostitute. Based on Jim Thompson's searing novel A Hell of a Woman.

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, born 1936

humor.rewrite:
Serie Noire (1979)

A bleakly hilarious film noir masterpiece about a door-to-door salesman who makes the *brilliant* life choice of getting lured into crime by a teenage prostitute. (Turns out "just say no" wasn't invented yet.) Based on Jim Thompson's novel A Hell of a Woman, which is exactly what the title promises.

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, born in 1936 and apparently fluent in tragedy.

narrativity.rewrite:
I first discovered Serie Noire on a rainy afternoon, scrolling through an obscure film database, and couldn't look away from the premise: a door-to-door salesman's entire life unravels when he crosses paths with a teenage prostitute. What I found was something I wasn't expecting—a film that's simultaneously bleak and hilarious, a twisted noir that burrows under your skin.

Directed by Alain Corneau and adapted from Jim Thompson's novel A Hell of a Woman, it's the kind of dark comedy that makes you laugh at the worst possible moments. The screenplay came from Corneau alongside Georges Perec, a writer born in 1936 who brought his own peculiar genius to the material.

It's superb.

grounding.rewrite:
Serie Noire (1979)

A superb, bleakly hilarious film noir about crime and moral corruption. Based on the Jim Thompson novel A Hell of a Woman.

Directed by Alain Corneau

Screenplay by Corneau and Georges Perec, born in 1936

original: just learned that cotton is a mallow (i've never seen it in flower, just yoinked standing dry stalks off the roadside in oklahoma) and now understand why the weird prickly seed heads of hollyhocks are vaguely familiar... plants are so cool dude

reading_level.rewrite: Upon discovering that *Gossypium* belongs to the Malvaceae family, I retrospectively comprehend the morphological parallels between the characteristic seed pods of *Alcea* species and the fibrous bolls I had previously collected from desiccated cotton specimens along Oklahoma roadsides—a taxonomic connection I had failed to recognize absent direct observation of anthesis. The structural homologies across disparate members of this botanical family merit considerable appreciation.

background.rewrite: cotton's a mallow—explains why hollyhock seed heads look so familiar. never connected the dots until now, probably because i've only seen dried stalks pulled from oklahoma roadsides. plants are so cool dude

abstract_concrete.rewrite: just learned that certain plants share unexpected botanical family connections, and it's wild how recognizing these relationships changes how you see things you encounter in nature. when you suddenly notice shared characteristics between plants you've observed separately, it reframes your whole understanding of how the natural world organizes itself... plants are so cool dude

tone.rewrite: HOLY SHIT. COTTON IS A MALLOW?? I've been grabbing dried stalks off Oklahoma roadsides this whole time without realizing the INSANE connection to hollyhocks?? That's why those terrifying prickly seed heads looked so familiar!! My mind is genuinely BLOWN right now. I need to see cotton actually FLOWERING. This changes EVERYTHING about how I see plants. I can't stop thinking about this.

humor.rewrite: just discovered cotton is basically a fancy mallow and suddenly my roadside oklahoma stalk-yoinking habit makes sense (turns out i've been collecting cousins without realizing it). apparently nature said "let's make hollyhock seed pods but make them fluffy and profitable" — plants really said "let's just be inexplicably related to everything" and honestly? respect the chaos

narrativity.rewrite: Cotton is a mallow. The prickly seed heads of hollyhocks resemble cotton bolls because they share the same plant family. This botanical connection explains the visual similarity between the two species' reproductive structures.

grounding.rewrite: cotton belongs to the mallow family, which explains the structural similarities i've noticed between it and other mallows. learning about plant taxonomy and how different species are related is fascinating

original:
Autists: We're literally all queer as shit

Researchers: The link between Queerness and Autism is purely anecdotal

Also researchers: "Only men and women were included... as the very low number of participants who identified as ‘other’ in the non-autistic group precluded statistical analysis."

reading_level.rewrite:
Autists: We're basically all queer.

Researchers: There's no real proof that autism and queerness are connected.

Also researchers: "We only studied men and women. We didn't have enough non-binary people in the non-autistic group to study."

background.rewrite:
Autists: We're literally all queer as shit

Researchers: The link between Queerness and Autism is purely anecdotal

Also researchers: *conducting studies that only recruit cisgender men and women, then explaining* "Only men and women were included in our analysis... because the non-autistic control group had so few people who identified as 'other' that we couldn't do the math on it."

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
People in this community: We experience significant demographic overlap

Researchers: The connection is merely observational

Also researchers: "Only certain demographic categories were included... as insufficient representation of other groups in the comparison population prevented meaningful statistical evaluation."

tone.rewrite:
Autistic individuals report higher rates of queer identification compared to neurotypical populations.

Researchers often characterize this connection as anecdotal. However, their own methodological limitations warrant examination: "Only men and women were included in this analysis, as the sample size of non-autistic participants identifying as 'other' was insufficient for statistical analysis."

This circular reasoning—dismissing the correlation while simultaneously excluding relevant data—suggests the evidence gap may reflect research design rather than actual prevalence rates.

humor.rewrite:
Autistic people report high rates of queer identification.

Researchers claim the link between queerness and autism lacks empirical support.

However, these same researchers acknowledge methodological limitations: "Only men and women were included in the study, as the very low number of participants who identified as 'other' in the non-autistic group precluded statistical analysis."

This methodological constraint—excluding non-binary and other gender identities—undermines the validity of conclusions that dismiss the autism-queerness connection as merely anecdotal.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was reading through another research paper on autism last week, coffee getting cold next to me, when I hit a sentence that made me pause. The researchers were explaining why their study only looked at men and women: "the very low number of participants who identified as 'other' in the non-autistic group precluded statistical analysis."

I thought about my autistic friends. Most of them aren't straight. Most of them aren't cis. And yet here I was, watching researchers dismiss what we've been saying all along.

Autists: We're literally all queer as shit

Researchers: The link between Queerness and Autism is purely anecdotal

Also researchers: *designing studies that systematically exclude non-binary and trans people, then using that exclusion as scientific justification*

The irony stung. We're not imagining the connection. They're just not looking.

grounding.rewrite:
Autists: We're literally all queer as shit

Researchers: The link between Queerness and Autism is purely anecdotal

Also researchers: Our studies exclude non-binary and transgender participants because their representation is too small for our analysis.

original: Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat. One of the most platonically perfect movies I’ve ever seen. The dancing is at the  ‘impossible’ level of difficulty. One is reminded of watching Bruce Lee. That these two should also be great at screwball comedy boggles the mind.

reading_level.rewrite: Top Hat with Astaire and Rogers. One of the best movies I've seen. The dancing is so hard it seems impossible. It's like watching Bruce Lee. And they're also funny. It's amazing they could do it all.

background.rewrite: Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat (1935). One of the most platonically perfect movies I've ever seen. For context: Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers were Hollywood's premier dancing duo, and this film showcases their signature tap and ballroom routines—the choreography reaches an 'impossible' level of difficulty that's genuinely hard to believe watching humans execute. It's the kind of technical mastery you get when watching Bruce Lee perform martial arts. What makes it even more remarkable is that these two weren't just dancers; they were also genuinely skilled at screwball comedy, a demanding genre requiring perfect timing. That they excelled at both simultaneously still boggles the mind.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: The greatest entertainment often comes from artists who transcend a single discipline. Consider how the most technically masterful performers can simultaneously excel in comedic timing and dramatic delivery. When excellence across multiple domains converges, it creates something that feels almost beyond human capability—a rare convergence of skill that leaves audiences wondering how such versatility is even possible.

tone.rewrite: Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat. This film is ASTOUNDINGLY perfect. The dancing will absolutely blow your mind—it's genuinely IMPOSSIBLE to pull off what they do. Watching it feels like witnessing Bruce Lee. And then they turn around and NAIL screwball comedy too?? How are they this good at everything?!

humor.rewrite: Astaire and Rogers in Top Hat: proof that some people just decided to be good at literally everything and the rest of us had to live with it. The dancing exists at a level of difficulty that makes physics weep. Watching it is basically the movie equivalent of a Bruce Lee film, except with more taps and fewer people getting hurt. And *then* they're also hilarious at screwball comedy? At what point do you just accept you've lost and go home?

narrativity.rewrite: I'll never forget the first time I watched *Top Hat* late one night, completely mesmerized. I found myself leaning forward, rewinding the dance sequences over and over, trying to understand how human bodies could move like that. Astaire and Rogers didn't just dance—they achieved something I can only describe as impossible. Watching them glide across the screen felt like witnessing Bruce Lee in action, that same sense of witnessing a master at the absolute peak of their craft. But here's what really got me: these two weren't just virtuosos of movement. They were genuinely hilarious together, delivering screwball comedy with the same effortless precision. It seems almost unfair that one film could contain all of this.

grounding.rewrite: Top Hat is one of the most perfectly executed films I've ever seen. The dancing reaches an extraordinarily difficult technical level. The fact that the leads excel equally at both dance and screwball comedy is remarkable.

🇳🇿
🌱🇳🇿

Hydrangeas remain among my preferred ornamentals. This specimen's phenotypic expression has proven rather amusing—despite my substantial application of aluminium sulfate as a chromatic modifier, the elevated pH dynamics inherent to my soil composition have resulted in anthocyanin-mediated polychromatic manifestation across the inflorescence this season. The aesthetic outcome, whilst unintended, proves sufficiently appealing. 🤣😂🤣

🇳🇿
🌱🇳🇿

background.rewrite:
Dosed my hydrangeas with alum for blue but my soil pH is too alkaline—ended up with a rainbow bush instead. Not complaining. 🤣😂🤣

🇳🇿
🌱🇳🇿

Plants can be wonderfully unpredictable. I've tried to influence the coloration of my flowering shrub through chemical amendment, but the inherent soil conditions proved more dominant than expected. The result is a delightfully mixed palette rather than the uniform appearance I'd aimed for. Sometimes nature's resistance to our intentions creates something appealing anyway. 🤣😂🤣

narrativity.rewrite: Hydrangeas produce multi-colored blooms when aluminum sulfate applications meet alkaline soil conditions. High soil alkalinity neutralizes the colorant effects, resulting in mixed hues across a single plant rather than uniform blue flowers. This pH-dependent color variation is a natural outcome of hydrangea physiology and soil chemistry.

original:
Making a commercially successful game

Expectation: 50% making a good game, 50% marketing

Reality: 10% making a good game, 90% marketing

I would like to point out Super Bernie World is my most successful game, but nowhere near my best. Being memetic isn't about inherent quality, that's marketing

reading_level.rewrite:
Deconstructing commercial viability in game development

Conventional wisdom: 50% production excellence, 50% promotional strategy

Empirical observation: 10% ludic quality, 90% strategic market positioning

Notably, Super Bernie World exemplifies this dichotomy—whilst constituting my most commercially viable title, it remains substantially inferior to my technical and creative output. Memetic proliferation correlates inversely with intrinsic design merit; rather, it functions as a derivative consequence of sophisticated marketing apparatus.

background.rewrite:
Making a commercially successful game

Expectation: 50% making a good game, 50% marketing

Reality: 10% making a good game, 90% marketing

Here's what I mean: I created Super Bernie World (a fan game featuring Bernie Sanders that went viral online). It became my most successful game by downloads and reach, but it's nowhere near the best game I've ever made in terms of actual design and gameplay quality. This shows that viral success isn't about inherent quality—it's about being memetic, which is pure marketing.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
I released Super Bernie World in 2016 as a Bernie Sanders platformer. It got 500K+ plays and became my most commercially successful game, yet I've made technically better games that barely broke 10K plays.

Super Bernie World succeeded because the Sanders meme was everywhere that election cycle—not because the game mechanics were superior. The formula was roughly: 10% solid platformer design, 90% riding a viral political moment.

That's the truth about indie game success: marketing and memetic potential dominate over inherent quality.

tone.rewrite:
Making a commercially successful game is BRUTAL.

Expectation: 50% making a good game, 50% marketing

Reality: 10% making a good game, 90% marketing

And here's what KILLS me—Super Bernie World became my biggest hit, yet it's nowhere NEAR my best work. That's the devastating truth: being memetic has NOTHING to do with actual quality. It's ALL marketing.

humor.rewrite:
Making a commercially successful game

Expectation: 50% making a good game, 50% marketing

Reality: 10% making a good game, 90% marketing

Shoutout to Super Bernie World — my most successful game AND a perfect case study proving that quality is overrated. Turns out virality doesn't care about your actual talent. Who knew? (Everyone. Everyone knew.)

narrativity.rewrite:
Last week, I was scrolling through my game library when I noticed something that made me laugh—my most downloaded game isn't the one I'm most proud of. It got me thinking about a hard truth I've learned building games for years.

When I started out, I thought success was simple: Make something great, and people will come. I imagined a 50/50 split—half the battle is craft, half is getting the word out.

But after releasing game after game, the math doesn't add up that way at all. The reality is closer to 10% execution, 90% marketing.

Take Super Bernie World. It's my most successful game by a mile. But here's the thing—it's nowhere near my *best* work. The difference? It went viral. Being memetic isn't about inherent quality. It's about marketing. It's about timing, resonance, and reaching the right people at the right moment.

That's the game industry in a nutshell.

grounding.rewrite: Making a commercially successful game requires far more marketing effort than most people assume. While many believe quality and promotion matter equally, the reality is that success depends overwhelmingly on marketing rather than game quality itself. A game's commercial performance is determined primarily by its ability to spread and capture attention, not by how well-made it is.

original: The thing people don't realize about a lot of playtesting in a game dev scenario is that for a long time you're trying to play catch with a ball made out of barbed wire, and you're tossing it back and forth, trying to clip off all the barbs that make it unplayable.

reading_level.rewrite: Most people don't know what playtesting is really like in game development. For a long time, you're working with something broken. You and your team keep testing it. You find problems. You fix them. Then you test again. It's like playing catch with a spiky ball. You throw it back and forth. Each time, you remove some spikes. Eventually, the ball becomes smooth enough to play with.

background.rewrite: The thing people don't realize about a lot of playtesting in a game dev scenario is that for a long time you're trying to play catch with a ball made out of barbed wire. Here's what that means: developers and testers repeatedly play through the same game build, finding bugs and design problems (the "barbs"), then developers fix them, and testers play again. You're tossing it back and forth—literally going through this cycle over and over—trying to clip off all the barbs that make it unplayable, one iteration at a time. It's a slow, painful process before you get to something polished enough to release.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Playtesting a game in development often means repeatedly encountering broken mechanics, unfair difficulty spikes, and buggy systems—then spending weeks iterating on each one. You'll playtest a boss fight 50 times, fix the damage scaling, playtest it 50 more times, discover the attack pattern is still frustrating, and repeat. Each pass removes another "barb" (a crash, an exploit, an unfun moment) until the core experience finally works.

tone.rewrite: The brutal truth about playtesting that nobody wants to admit? You're literally playing catch with a ball of BARBED WIRE. And you HAVE to keep throwing it back and forth, desperately clipping away the razor-sharp barbs that are shredding your game to pieces. It's agonizing. It's relentless. But it's the ONLY way to make something playable.

humor.rewrite: The thing people don't realize about playtesting in game dev is that you're basically playing catch with a ball made of barbed wire—except instead of just bleeding, you're also responsible for filing down every single barb so it doesn't destroy the next person's hands. Fun times.

narrativity.rewrite: I was watching a playtester the other day, controller in hand, running into the same wall for the fifth time in as many minutes. They looked up at me with this exhausted smile and said, "So... is this supposed to happen?" That's when it hit me—this is what playtesting really is. You're not playing catch with a normal ball. You're playing catch with a ball made out of barbed wire, tossing it back and forth over and over, clipping off each barb that makes it unplayable. For months. Sometimes longer. People don't realize how much of game dev is just this: the messy, tedious work of finding what breaks the game and fixing it, one painful iteration at a time.

grounding.rewrite: Playtesting in game development involves identifying and removing problematic elements from the game over an extended period of time. The iterative process of feedback and refinement is essential to making a game playable and enjoyable.

original:
FIFA was getting paid $150M/year to license the brand to Electronic Arts. Apparently, it wanted more. Instead of paying up, EA Sports made a new game without FIFA. This gamble seems to have paid off for EA, as per Financial Times. 

The world's smallest violin playing its heart out for FIFA.

reading_level.rewrite:
FIFA was making $150M a year from EA Sports. It wanted even more money. EA said no. Instead, EA made a new game without the FIFA name. It worked out great for EA, according to Financial Times.

Poor FIFA.

background.rewrite:
FIFA had a lucrative licensing deal with EA Sports where the organization earned $150M annually just for allowing EA to use the FIFA name and brand in their popular sports video game. When FIFA demanded significantly more money, EA faced a choice: pay the increased fee or walk away from the partnership. EA Sports decided to take a gamble—they created a new soccer game called EA Sports FC without the FIFA license, essentially removing FIFA's ability to profit from the deal. According to reporting from the Financial Times, this risky strategy actually worked out well for EA, suggesting players didn't need the FIFA name to enjoy the game. So while FIFA lost a major revenue stream by being too greedy, EA thrived without them.

The world's smallest violin playing its heart out for FIFA.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Major licensing agreements can become financially unsustainable when one party demands significantly increased compensation. When organizations refuse to meet escalating demands, alternatives often emerge. Strategic pivots away from established partnerships sometimes yield unexpectedly favorable outcomes for the departing party.

There's little sympathy to spare for those who overreach in negotiations.

tone.rewrite: FIFA received approximately $150M annually from Electronic Arts for brand licensing. When negotiations for increased compensation stalled, EA Sports elected to develop a competing product without the FIFA branding. According to Financial Times reporting, this strategic decision appears to have yielded favorable financial results for EA.

humor.rewrite:
FIFA was extracting $150M/year from EA Sports just for slapping its name on a game. Turns out greed has its limits—even for FIFA. So EA Sports did what any self-respecting company would do: they ghosted FIFA and made their own game anyway. Plot twist: it actually worked. Financial Times confirms EA's gamble paid off spectacularly.

Thoughts and prayers for FIFA's negotiating strategy. 🎻

narrativity.rewrite:
FIFA demanded over $150M annually in licensing fees from Electronic Arts. EA Sports refused to increase payments and developed a new game without the FIFA brand instead. This gamble succeeded for EA Sports, according to Financial Times reporting.

FIFA's loss of the lucrative licensing deal represents a significant revenue decline for the organization.

grounding.rewrite:
FIFA demanded higher licensing fees from EA Sports, who refused to pay. Instead, EA Sports created a new game without the FIFA brand. This decision proved financially successful for EA Sports according to Financial Times reporting.

background.rewrite: The wildest thing about game development is that creating even a single small moment—like a satisfying animation, a clever UI detail, or a smooth camera movement that makes a player think "ah, that's cool" for just 3 seconds—requires months of work from multiple specialists (programmers, artists, designers, sound engineers, etc.) all coordinating together. Whenever you play a game, you're essentially a privileged person being surrounded by an invisible army of tiny elves working ceaselessly at trying to make you happy, except those elves are real people dedicating weeks to things you'll barely notice.

Whenever you play a game, you are like a dauphin surrounded by an army of tiny elves (the 50-200 person dev team) working ceaselessly at trying to make you happy with details you'll barely notice.

narrativity.rewrite: I was playtesting a game last week when a character's cloak billowed perfectly as they walked through a doorway. It lasted maybe three seconds. My friend beside me went "oh, that's cool" and moved on. But here's what blew my mind: that tiny moment—that single breath of fabric caught in wind—was the result of months of work. An animator tweaking curves, a programmer building systems, a designer approving it all. Months of multidisciplinary effort, invisible, just trying to delight you for those precious three seconds.
