original: one of the dominant ideologies among this country's elite is College Admissions Brain where you reorient your entire worldview around valorizing the effective strategies for getting into good colleges as like, a really elaborate game whose winners get the good careers and losers don't.

reading_level.rewrite: A lot of powerful people in this country are obsessed with college admissions. They see it like a game. Win the game, get into a good college, get a good job. Lose the game, and you don't. This idea shapes how they see the whole world.

background.rewrite: one of the dominant ideologies among this country's elite is College Admissions Brain—the idea that getting into prestigious universities is an elaborate game with real-world consequences. Here's how it works: families adopt strategies (test prep, extracurriculars, legacy connections) to win admission to selective schools, treating this process as the primary determinant of future success. The underlying belief is that winning this game = access to good careers, while losing it = being locked out of opportunity. This mindset reshapes how people—especially the wealthy who can afford to optimize—view education, competition, and what counts as a worthwhile life trajectory.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: One of the dominant ideologies among America's elite is College Admissions Brain: the belief that taking AP classes, padding your resume with unpaid internships, writing the perfect Common App essay, and scoring 1500+ on the SAT are the key strategies that separate those who get into Harvard, Yale, and Stanford from those who don't—and that this separation determines who gets hired at McKinsey, lands partner roles at law firms, or secures six-figure tech jobs.

tone.rewrite: A significant ideological framework among the country's elite centers on what might be termed "College Admissions Optimization"—a worldview that treats admission to selective institutions as a systematic competition with measurable strategies and outcomes. This framework posits a correlation between admission success and subsequent career trajectories, effectively structuring life choices around maximizing admissions credentials.

humor.rewrite: one of the dominant ideologies among this country's elite is College Admissions Brain—a condition where people have lovingly restructured their entire existence around the sacred art of gaming college admissions like it's competitive esports, except the prize is a decent job and the stakes are your entire future. truly a normal way to organize society.

narrativity.rewrite: I was sitting in a coffee shop last week when I overheard a parent telling their kid to join yet another club—not because they loved it, but because "it'll look good on applications." That moment crystallized something I've been thinking about: so many people in positions of influence are trapped in what I call College Admissions Brain. It's this all-consuming ideology where your entire worldview gets reoriented around mastering an elaborate game—one where you collect the right credentials, optimize every activity, and basically treat your teenage years as a ruthless competition. The premise is stark: win this game, get into a good college, secure a good career. Lose, and well... the narrative suggests you don't. It's become a dominant force shaping how our country's elite think about success and failure.

grounding.rewrite: one of the dominant ideologies among this country's elite is College Admissions Brain — it's like treating life as a board game where every activity gets evaluated for resume points. Your kid takes piano lessons not because they love music, but because it "looks good." You volunteer at a specific nonprofit because admissions officers care about it. You optimize every test score, every essay, every extracurricular for the algorithm. And the underlying belief? That winning this game gets you the good careers and stable futures, while losing it doesn't. The entire worldview gets reoriented around this single elaborate competition.

original:
Man deadlifts are so fucking good.

Literally the most practical lift (you're doing the exact mechanics you'd need to pick up a heavy box or whatever), it feels very primal and it's loud and heavy as fuck.

Finished up with 5x155kg, went up smooth. My max is still prob around 200kg or so imo

reading_level.rewrite:
The deadlift represents an exceptionally efficacious compound movement, primarily attributable to its biomechanical correspondence with fundamental human locomotor patterns requisite for lifting substantial objects from ground level.

Beyond its utilitarian application, the exercise engenders a profound physiological response characterized by its inherently visceral nature and the kinetic intensity inherent to maximal load displacement. I concluded today's session with five repetitions at 155kg executed with fluid movement mechanics, while my estimated one-repetition maximum remains approximately 200kg.

background.rewrite:
Man deadlifts are so fucking good.

For context: deadlifts are when you lift a barbell loaded with weight plates off the ground to hip height, then lower it back down. Literally the most practical lift because you're doing the exact same mechanics your body uses to pick up a heavy box in real life—it's a fundamental human movement pattern.

There's something primal about it. The bar crashes down loud, the weight feels substantial and heavy as fuck, and your whole body is engaged.

Finished up my workout with 5 sets of 155kg (that's about 340 lbs per set), and the reps went up smooth. My estimated one-rep max is probably around 200kg (440 lbs) or so imo.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Heavy compound movements are incredibly effective.

They align with fundamental human mechanics that translate to real-world utility, feel inherently satisfying, and deliver substantial physical demands.

Completed a solid session with respectable weight that moved cleanly. Current capacity remains at a solid level.

tone.rewrite:
Deadlifts offer significant practical value as an exercise. The movement pattern directly translates to real-world applications like lifting heavy objects, and the biomechanics engage multiple muscle groups effectively. There's also a notable sensory aspect to the lift—the auditory and kinesthetic feedback can be reinforcing.

Completed a session of 5 repetitions at 155kg with solid form. Based on recent performance, estimated maximum capacity is approximately 200kg.

humor.rewrite:
Man deadlifts are so fucking good.

Literally the most practical lift (unlike bicep curls, which prepare you exclusively for carrying grocery bags with maximum sass), it feels primal and honestly? Nothing says "I have my life together" like making loud clanging noises in a crowded gym.

Just crushed 5x155kg smooth as butter. Pretty sure my max is somewhere in the 200kg ballpark, though my ego would like to file a formal complaint with that assessment.

narrativity.rewrite:
Last week I was moving boxes with a friend and realized I could heft them effortlessly while he was struggling—that's when it hit me: deadlifts are so fucking good.

Because here's the thing—you're not just training in a gym. You're literally doing the exact mechanics you'd need to pick up a heavy box or whatever real life throws at you. It feels primal, that moment when the bar leaves the ground. The sound of it, the weight—there's something about it that just feels right, you know?

Finished up today with 5x155kg, went up smooth. My max is still probably around 200kg or so, but every session reminds me why this lift matters.

grounding.rewrite:
Deadlifts are so fucking good.

They're the most practical lift and feel very primal. The exercise engages fundamental movement patterns and provides a satisfying, intense experience.

Finished up with 5x155kg, went up smooth. My max is still probably around 200kg or so.

original: A cool thing about the conversion of a super-majority of US college faculty to short-term contract employment (while the tenured class mostly stood idle) is that it bleeds massive amounts of talent out of institutions while also harming students and never fixing Budget Problems

reading_level.rewrite: Here's what's happening at US colleges: Most professors now work on short contracts instead of having job security. The few tenured professors didn't stop this. The result? Talented teachers leave. Students suffer. And the budget problems don't get fixed anyway.

background.rewrite: The US college system has undergone a major shift: most faculty positions are now short-term contracts rather than permanent tenured roles. Here's the problem: while tenured professors largely didn't intervene, this conversion has created a triple failure. Talented educators leave for more stable work, students suffer from less invested instructors and less institutional knowledge, and universities still face the same financial pressures they were trying to solve. So the strategy failed on every front.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: A cool thing about how US college faculty have shifted from permanent positions to adjunct and non-tenure-track contracts—while tenured professors largely remained protected—is that it drains experienced instructors like Dr. Jane Smith (who left Stanford for industry) out of institutions while also forcing students into larger classes taught by rotating instructors, yet universities like University of Arizona still face budget shortfalls.

tone.rewrite: The shift of US college faculty toward short-term contract employment—while tenured positions remained largely unchanged—presents a significant trade-off worth examining. This structural change has contributed to talent migration from institutions, created measurable challenges for student outcomes, and has not demonstrably resolved underlying budget constraints.

humor.rewrite: A cool thing about converting most US college faculty to short-term contract gigs (while tenured professors watched from their ivory towers) is that it simultaneously hemorrhages talent, tanks student outcomes, AND somehow still doesn't solve the budget crisis. It's like setting your house on fire to pay the electric bill—wildly ineffective, but at least consistent.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was chatting with a brilliant adjunct professor last week who's juggling three schools just to make ends meet. She told me she's thinking about leaving academia altogether—and that's when it hit me: this is happening everywhere.

Over the past couple decades, US colleges have quietly converted most faculty positions to short-term contracts while the tenured professors largely watched it happen. The result? It's a triple threat: we're hemorrhaging talented educators from our institutions, our students are getting worse continuity and mentorship, and somehow the budget problems everyone claimed to be solving? They never actually get fixed.

grounding.rewrite: A cool thing about the conversion of a super-majority of US college faculty to short-term contract employment (while the tenured class mostly stood idle) is that it's like replacing a building's permanent structural engineers with temporary contractors. You lose institutional knowledge when experienced people leave, the building (student education) suffers from inconsistent oversight, and you never actually solve your underlying financial problems—you just create new ones. Meanwhile, those with secure positions watch from above instead of addressing the root issues.

original: "Most STEM graduates don’t work in STEM occupations. The Census Bureau reported in 2021 that a paltry 28% of STEM grads are working in these supposedly in-demand, highly paid and important STEM jobs."

reading_level.rewrite: "Most STEM graduates don't work in STEM jobs. The Census Bureau found in 2021 that only 28% of STEM grads actually work in STEM. This is surprising since these jobs are supposed to be in high demand, pay well, and be important."

background.rewrite: "Most STEM graduates don't work in STEM occupations. To understand why this matters: the U.S. Census Bureau (the government agency that tracks employment data) released findings in 2021 showing that only 28% of people who graduated with STEM degrees—science, technology, engineering, and math—actually end up working in STEM-related jobs. This contradicts the common narrative that these fields are in-demand, highly paid, and important career paths."

abstract_concrete.rewrite: "A significant portion of those educated in technical fields end up pursuing careers outside their area of study. Recent data suggests that the majority of graduates with advanced technical training are not employed in positions aligned with their specialized education, challenging common assumptions about career trajectories and labor market demands."

tone.rewrite: "According to 2021 Census Bureau data, approximately 28% of STEM graduates work in STEM occupations. This figure suggests a notable disconnect between STEM degree attainment and STEM employment, despite the sector's reputation for high demand and compensation."

humor.rewrite: "Plot twist: Most STEM graduates don't actually work in STEM. Shocking, I know. The Census Bureau's 2021 report revealed that a thrilling 28% of STEM grads landed those supposedly in-demand, highly paid, definitely-not-mythical STEM jobs. The other 72%? Living their best lives doing literally anything else."

narrativity.rewrite: I was chatting with a friend who spent four years grinding through organic chemistry and differential equations, only to end up in marketing. It got me wondering: is this actually common? Turns out, it's way more common than I thought. The Census Bureau looked into this in 2021 and found something striking — only 28% of STEM graduates actually work in STEM occupations. So much for those supposedly in-demand, highly paid, important STEM jobs everyone keeps talking about.

grounding.rewrite: "Most STEM graduates don't work in STEM occupations. The Census Bureau reported in 2021 that only 28% of STEM graduates are employed in STEM fields."

original:
I wish I could get the motivation to go back to the gym. It kept me sane as a safe space for me. For the first time ever in my life, I was fit.

I've always struggled with weight and depression. When COVID came my depression returned and motivation left. 

I lost everything and can't get it back.

reading_level.rewrite:
I wish I could reclaim the intrinsic motivation to resume my fitness regimen, which previously functioned as a crucial psychological refuge and sanctuary for my mental well-being. For the inaugural period in my existence, I achieved a state of optimal physical conditioning.

I have perpetually contended with weight management and depressive episodes. The pandemic's emergence precipitated a recurrence of my depressive symptoms and a consequent depletion of motivational capacity.

I have experienced comprehensive loss and remain unable to recover what has been forfeited.

background.rewrite: Lost my gym routine and can't regain that mental stability. It was the only place I felt genuinely fit and sane—my safe space disappeared when depression crept back in post-COVID. Can't shake the feeling that it's gone for good.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
I wish I could regain the drive to return to the practices that sustained me. It provided me with something I desperately needed—a refuge where I could exist on my own terms. For the first time, I felt like I had achieved something meaningful for myself.

I've always battled with personal struggles that weighed heavily on me. When circumstances shifted unexpectedly, those old challenges resurfaced and my sense of purpose dissolved.

I've lost something essential and I'm uncertain if I can recover it.

tone.rewrite:
I've been reflecting on my gym routine, which was genuinely beneficial for my mental health and physical fitness. It provided structure and measurable progress—the first time I'd achieved sustained fitness.

My history includes ongoing challenges with weight management and depression. The pandemic disrupted these habits, and motivation has been difficult to rebuild.

Currently, I'm working through how to regain what was lost during this period.

humor.rewrite:
I wish I could get the motivation to go back to the gym—you know, that magical place where I was briefly acquainted with fitness and my mental health actually cooperated. Revolutionary concept, I know.

Turns out depression + COVID is the ultimate dynamic duo for undoing years of work. Who knew sabotaging yourself could be so efficient? I've mastered the art of losing everything and then just... not finding it again. It's a skill, really.

narrativity.rewrite: The gym serves as an essential mental health tool and physical fitness space. Regular exercise combats depression and builds sustainable fitness outcomes. COVID disruptions derailed established wellness routines and created barriers to recovery. Weight management and mental health require consistent gym access and motivation to maintain achieved fitness levels.

grounding.rewrite:
I wish I had the motivation to return to exercising regularly. Physical activity was essential for my mental health and provided me with an important outlet. For the first time, I achieved the fitness level I wanted.

I've struggled with weight and depression throughout my life. When circumstances changed, my depression worsened and my motivation disappeared.

I've lost the progress I made and struggle to regain it.

original: A vicious cycle I'm seeing: if you say anything positive about the US economy, you get huge blowback from (some) readers. This in turn makes reporters reluctant to report good news, or at any rate hide it deep in the article (I've seen this happen), which in turn reinforces the negativity.

reading_level.rewrite: I'm seeing a bad cycle happen. People attack reporters who say good things about the US economy. This makes reporters scared to share good news. They bury it deep in articles instead. And this just makes everyone more negative about everything.

background.rewrite: A vicious cycle I'm seeing: The US economy has shown some genuine improvements lately, but if you say anything positive about it, you get huge blowback from some readers who are skeptical or frustrated. This hostility toward positive economic news makes reporters reluctant to report those improvements, or at least bury them deep in articles where few will see them (I've witnessed this firsthand). And when good economic news stays hidden, it reinforces the overall narrative of economic doom—which then triggers even more skepticism the next time someone tries to report something positive. It's a self-reinforcing loop.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: A vicious cycle I'm seeing: if you report that unemployment hit a 50-year low or that wage growth outpaced inflation last quarter, you get hundreds of angry comments from readers. This pushes reporters at outlets like CNN and the New York Times to bury economic gains in paragraph 8 instead of leading with them—I've watched this happen with jobs reports—which then reinforces the perception that the economy is only getting worse.

tone.rewrite: STOP. We're trapped in a devastating cycle and nobody's talking about it. Say ONE positive thing about the economy? You'll be absolutely ATTACKED. Reporters are TERRIFIED—so they bury good news or refuse to report it at all (I've SEEN this). This weaponized negativity is poisoning our information ecosystem. We HAVE to break this now before it destroys our ability to understand reality.

humor.rewrite: A vicious cycle I'm watching unfold: say anything nice about the US economy and prepare for your mentions to become a warzone. Reporters, naturally, respond by either avoiding good news entirely or burying it so deep in articles that archaeologists couldn't find it. Rinse, repeat, watch negativity flourish. It's the journalistic equivalent of refusing to acknowledge your cake exists, then acting shocked when no one believes you baked one.

narrativity.rewrite: I was at coffee yesterday when I overheard a journalist friend mention she'd buried good economic data three paragraphs deep in her latest piece. When I asked why, she hesitated before admitting the backlash she'd faced the last time she'd highlighted anything positive about the economy. That conversation stuck with me because it perfectly captures something I've been noticing: we're trapped in a vicious cycle. Reporters say anything upbeat about the US economy and face intense blowback from readers. So they become gun-shy, either avoiding the good news entirely or tucking it so far down in articles that hardly anyone sees it. And that silence? It only feeds the negativity more.

grounding.rewrite: There's a problematic cycle occurring: positive economic reporting faces significant pushback from audiences, which discourages journalists from covering favorable developments prominently, thereby reinforcing overall negative sentiment.

original:
This last week I've finally started to feel better after surgery. I'm even at the gym today (oh my god. I've lost like 60-70% of my progress).

So hopefully things will pick up a bit in regards to me making stuff. Although I still work a full-time job so it won't be that much.

reading_level.rewrite:
Following my surgical intervention last week, I've commenced experiencing ameliorated physiological functioning. I've even initiated gym sessions today, though I've regrettably experienced substantial deconditioning—approximately 60-70% regression in my previously established athletic capacity.

Consequently, I anticipate a modest resumption of creative productivity in the forthcoming period. However, given my concurrent full-time occupational commitments, this incremental increase in output will necessarily remain circumscribed.

background.rewrite: Back in the gym after recovery and it's brutal — lost like 60-70% of my gains. Hoping to ramp up content creation now, but full-time work is still the limiting factor.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Recovery has been progressing, and I'm gradually returning to my usual routines. The readjustment process has highlighted how much effort it takes to rebuild momentum.

Content creation will likely resume at some point, though the constraints of maintaining full-time employment will naturally limit the pace at which things develop.

tone.rewrite:
I'm FINALLY feeling human again after surgery—and I'm back at the gym TODAY. But wow, the reality hit HARD—I've lost 60-70% of my progress. It's absolutely devastating.

I'm desperately hoping this means I can START CREATING AGAIN. But let's be real—I'm still chained to a full-time job, so I won't be able to give you nearly what I want to.

humor.rewrite:
Just got cleared for the gym and wow, turns out my muscles have officially filed for divorce. We're talking a devastating 60-70% loss of progress—apparently my pecs didn't get the memo that I was coming back.

But hey, I'm feeling human again! So expect slightly more content from me. Slightly. I do have this pesky full-time job that insists on existing, but I'll try to squeeze in some creativity between remembering how to lift things and remembering how to walk without wincing.

narrativity.rewrite: Surgery recovery takes time, and fitness progress rebounds faster than expected. Full-time work limits creative output, but content creation will increase as recovery progresses. Gym sessions have resumed despite significant strength loss from downtime.

grounding.rewrite:

Hopefully this means I can make more content going forward. That said, I still work full-time, so output will remain limited.

original:
I've been really struggling at the gym lately.  It's been this vicious cycle of stress undercutting my performance which makes me stressed.  Im trying to get ahead of it by getting out of my own head.

I did my mobility workout today for the first time in AGES and it sucked but baby steps

reading_level.rewrite:
I've found myself ensnared in a deleterious feedback loop wherein accumulated psychological stressors precipitate compromised athletic performance, subsequently exacerbating my underlying anxiety—a cyclical pattern I'm endeavoring to interrupt through cognitive reframing and intentional disengagement from counterproductive rumination.

Today I reinstated my mobility conditioning regimen after an extended hiatus; whilst the session proved considerably challenging, I'm embracing incremental progress as a methodological pathway forward.

background.rewrite:
Stress → worse lifts → more stress. Vicious cycle. Breaking it by staying out of my head.

Finally did mobility work again today. Rough session, but it's a start.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
I've been struggling with my goals lately. There's this feedback loop where obstacles undercut my progress, which creates more pressure. I'm trying to break the pattern by shifting my mindset.

I worked on foundational practices today after a long absence and it was difficult, but I'm focusing on incremental improvement.

tone.rewrite:
I'm DROWNING at the gym right now. This vicious cycle of stress CRUSHING my performance is suffocating me—it's making everything worse. I'm desperate to break free, so I'm FIGHTING to get out of my own head before it destroys me completely.

I finally forced myself through a mobility workout today after what feels like FOREVER and it was absolutely brutal—but I'm refusing to give up. Every tiny step matters when you're clawing your way back.

humor.rewrite:
I've been locked in a beautiful stress-gym spiral where I lift poorly, panic about lifting poorly, then lift even worse. Truly a masterclass in self-sabotage. 

So today I did the revolutionary thing: stopped torturing myself mentally and just... moved my body. Did my neglected mobility routine for the first time in what felt like a geological era. It was deeply unpleasant! But hey, at least I showed up. Baby steps, right? (Or in my case, very creaky, rusty baby steps.)

narrativity.rewrite: Stress undermines gym performance, which creates more stress. Breaking the cycle requires mental clarity. Mobility work is foundational—consistency matters more than intensity. Starting after a long break is difficult, but incremental progress builds momentum.

grounding.rewrite:

original: Lots of warning signals in recent econ data. First up, net bank lending has basically stopped and is going backwards in real terms; in fact, it looks a lot like 2009. Net bank lending is a critical source of economic stimulus. No stimulus = slump = unemployment up = more slumping etc. [🧵1/n]

reading_level.rewrite: Banks are barely lending money anymore. It's actually getting worse in real terms. This looks like 2009. Banks lending money is how the economy grows. No lending means no growth. No growth means job losses. Job losses mean even worse problems. [1/n]

background.rewrite: Lots of warning signals in recent econ data. First up, net bank lending has basically stopped and is going backwards in real terms. To understand why this matters: banks lend money to businesses and consumers, fueling growth and job creation. When lending freezes, companies can't expand and hire—the opposite happens. This pattern looks a lot like 2009, right before the financial crisis hit hard. Without that critical source of economic stimulus, the cycle spirals: no new spending → businesses slow down → unemployment rises → people spend even less. It's a dangerous feedback loop. [🧵1/n]

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Lots of warning signals emerging across economic indicators. A key mechanism of economic stimulus appears to be contracting in real terms, resembling patterns from previous crisis periods. When this particular transmission channel weakens, it removes crucial support from growth dynamics, which can trigger cascading negative effects throughout the broader economy. [🧵1/n]

tone.rewrite: Recent economic data presents several points worth examining. Net bank lending has declined and is contracting in real terms, showing patterns similar to 2009. Since net bank lending functions as a significant economic stimulus mechanism, reduced lending activity could potentially contribute to economic slowdown, which may subsequently affect employment levels and create further headwinds for growth. [🧵1/n]

humor.rewrite: Lots of warning signals in recent econ data. First up, net bank lending has basically stopped and is going backwards in real terms — which, fun fact, looks *a lot* like 2009. You know, that year everyone remembers fondly. Net bank lending is apparently critical for economic stimulus, so naturally we're watching it evaporate. No stimulus → slump → unemployment goes brrr → more slumping. It's almost like there's a pattern here. [🧵1/n]

narrativity.rewrite: I was reviewing the latest economic data this morning—coffee getting cold, spreadsheets piling up—when something caught my eye that made me sit back in my chair. Net bank lending has basically stopped. It's going backwards in real terms, actually. And here's what sent a chill down my spine: it looks a lot like 2009. You know, that year everything fell apart. Net bank lending is a critical source of economic stimulus, and when it dries up, the dominoes start falling—no stimulus, slump hits, unemployment climbs, and then everything spirals downward again. This warning signal can't be ignored. [🧵1/n]

grounding.rewrite: Lots of warning signals in recent econ data. Net bank lending has declined significantly and turned negative in real terms, indicating a substantial reduction in a critical source of economic stimulus. Reduced lending capacity constrains money supply growth, which can trigger economic contraction, rising unemployment, and further economic deterioration. [🧵1/n]

original: I've never heard from as many young people who are telling me that they cannot vote for Biden. Do with that what you will but I think that some are very confident that these young folks will vote for this guy and I'm not sure why they are confident.

reading_level.rewrite: The unprecedented volume of correspondence I've received from younger demographic cohorts expressing their inability to endorse Biden's candidacy presents a noteworthy phenomenon. While certain political analysts appear to maintain considerable confidence in the eventual mobilization of youth voters toward supporting the incumbent, the empirical basis for such prognostication remains, in my assessment, insufficiently substantiated.

background.rewrite: I've never heard from as many young people who are telling me that they cannot vote for Biden. For context: there's been a lot of political discussion about whether young voters will show up for the current administration in the upcoming election. Some political analysts and Democratic strategists seem very confident that these young voters will ultimately support Biden anyway. But based on what I'm hearing directly from young people themselves, I'm not sure why anyone would be that confident.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: I've never heard from as many young people who are telling me they cannot vote for Biden. Do with that what you will but I think some Democratic strategists are very confident that Gen Z and millennial voters will show up for Biden in 2024, and I'm not sure why they're confident given what I'm hearing directly from voters in this age group.

tone.rewrite: I'm getting FLOODED with messages from young people saying they absolutely cannot vote for Biden. And honestly? It's alarming. Yet somehow, people keep insisting these voters will show up for him anyway. I genuinely don't understand where this confidence is coming from—and frankly, neither do the young people telling me they're done.

humor.rewrite: Apparently I've become a hotline for young people explaining why they won't vote for Biden. The confidence some people have that these folks will show up anyway is truly inspiring—I'm genuinely curious what evidence they're working with, because it's definitely not the conversations I'm having.

narrativity.rewrite: Last week, I was at a coffee shop when a group of college students next to me were debating the upcoming election. One of them turned to her friend and said, "I just can't do it." That phrase has stuck with me because I keep hearing it over and over lately—from young people telling me they cannot vote for Biden. I've never experienced this level of resistance before. What strikes me is how some people seem so certain these young voters will come around anyway. I honestly don't understand where that confidence is coming from.

grounding.rewrite: I've never heard from as many young people who are telling me they cannot vote for Biden. It's like a campaign manager confidently predicting a candidate will win a swing state based on old polling data—meanwhile, ground teams on the street are reporting something completely different. Do with that what you will, but I think some are very confident these young folks will fall in line, and I'm not sure why they are confident.

original: my main deadlifting "goal" is to be able to pick up and carry most of the folks I know. Some years back at our annual xmas scotch tasting, one cousin got so drunk he lied down on the ground and wouldn't get up. and I LIT UP EXCITEDLY because it was EXACTLY what I had been TRAINING FOR MY WHOLE LIFE

reading_level.rewrite: My primary objective regarding deadlift proficiency centers upon cultivating sufficient functional strength to facilitate the safe transport of individuals within my social circle. Several years prior, during our customary yuletide whisky tasting, a familial relation became so intoxicated he recumbently positioned himself and refused ambulation. This precipitated an acute surge of exhilaration, as the scenario represented the precise manifestation of the biomechanical capacity toward which I had been systematically conditioning throughout an extended duration.

background.rewrite: my main deadlifting "goal" is to be able to pick up and carry most of the folks I know. here's why: some years back at our annual christmas scotch tasting, one of my cousins got so drunk he just laid down on the ground and refused to get up. when that happened, I was genuinely EXCITED because after all this time spent training in the gym, I finally had a real-world situation where my strength could actually be useful. it was exactly the moment I had been preparing for.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: my main fitness goal is to develop the capacity to physically support the people in my life when they need it. There have been occasions when this preparation proved unexpectedly relevant, and I felt a genuine sense of fulfillment realizing that my training had practical application in a real moment—exactly what I'd been working toward all along

tone.rewrite: I've structured my deadlifting training around a practical objective: developing the capacity to lift and carry most people I know. This goal was validated during a Christmas gathering several years ago when a cousin became intoxicated and was unable to stand. The situation presented exactly the scenario my training had been designed to address.

humor.rewrite: I've been training my deadlift with a specific purpose in mind: to be strong enough to help and support the people I care about. A few years ago at our family Christmas gathering, this became relevant when a cousin had too much to drink and needed assistance getting up from the ground. In that moment, I realized my training had prepared me for exactly this kind of practical responsibility—being able to physically help someone when they genuinely need it.

narrativity.rewrite: Deadlifting strength serves a practical purpose: the ability to pick up and carry most people. This capability proved invaluable when a cousin became incapacitated at a Christmas gathering and required physical assistance. Training for functional strength—specifically the capacity to move another person—represents a legitimate and worthwhile fitness objective.

grounding.rewrite: I train specifically to be strong enough to carry people who need help. There's something deeply satisfying about having developed the capability to physically assist others when they're unable to help themselves. This practical strength is what motivates my training more than any arbitrary number on a barbell.

original: #ZigHealthTip: In the immortal words of Lee Haney, “Simulate, don’t annihilate.” Your goal with every gym session should be to do enough work to adequately stimulate muscle growth. If you’re frying your muscles so much that you’re sore for 2 weeks, that’s too much. Ease back.

reading_level.rewrite: #ZigHealthTip: Lee Haney said it best: "Simulate, don't annihilate." At the gym, do enough to trigger muscle growth. That's your goal. If you're so sore for 2 weeks after, you did too much. Pull back a bit.

background.rewrite: #ZigHealthTip: Legendary bodybuilder Lee Haney famously said, "Simulate, don't annihilate"—meaning you should challenge your muscles enough to trigger growth, but not destroy them. Here's why this matters: when you work out, you create tiny tears in muscle fibers that your body repairs and rebuilds stronger. But if you overdo it and end up intensely sore for 2 weeks straight, you've gone too far and aren't actually helping your progress. The sweet spot is finding that middle ground—enough stimulus for growth, but recovery that lets you train again soon.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: #ZigHealthTip: As fitness wisdom reminds us, the principle of adequate stimulation over excessive exertion applies to all training. Your goal with every session should be to do sufficient work to promote adaptation. If you're pushing so hard that recovery extends far beyond what's typical, that's counterproductive. Consider moderating your approach.

tone.rewrite: #ZigHealthTip: Lee Haney got it RIGHT: "Simulate, don't annihilate." Stop destroying yourself in the gym! You need to STIMULATE muscle growth—not obliterate your body. If you're crippled with soreness for 2 weeks straight, you've crossed the line. PULL BACK NOW before you sabotage your progress and risk serious injury.

humor.rewrite: #ZigHealthTip: Lee Haney didn't say "simulate, don't annihilate" so you could ignore the first part. Yes, you need to stress your muscles enough to grow—but if you're hobbling around like a newborn giraffe for 14 days, you've officially overdone it. Your muscles wanted a stimulus, not a full interrogation. Dial it back.

narrativity.rewrite:
I remember the first time I heard Lee Haney say this, I was nursing my arms in bed, barely able to lift them to brush my teeth. I'd gone all-out at the gym the day before—thought more pain meant more gain. That's when his words hit me: "Simulate, don't annihilate."

It changed everything. Your goal with every gym session should be to do enough work to adequately stimulate muscle growth—not destroy yourself. If you're stuck being sore for 2 weeks straight, that's your sign you've overdone it. Back off, recover smarter, and you'll actually see better results.

#ZigHealthTip

grounding.rewrite: #ZigHealthTip: In the immortal words of Lee Haney, "Simulate, don't annihilate." Think of your muscles like a garden—you need enough water to help plants grow, but drowning them kills them. Your goal with every gym session should be to do enough work to stimulate muscle growth. If you're leaving the gym so demolished that you're sore for 2 weeks, that's excessive. You've overwatered. Ease back and find the sweet spot where you're challenged but recoverable.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was talking to an autistic friend yesterday who just lost another job. When I asked what happened, the familiar story unfolded: subtle sabotage, constant criticism for minor things neurotypical coworkers got away with, being singled out in meetings. And it hit me: employers systematically abuse and gaslight autistic workers until we have no choice but to leave, then ableist psychologists and psychiatrists turn around and publish papers claiming high unemployment rates exist because autistic people simply "Suffer From Autism™️"—completely ignoring the actual abuse driving people out of the workforce.

grounding.rewrite:
employers: *intentionally abuse and gaslight autistic people until they're forced to be unemployed*

ableist psychologists and psychiatrists: there are high rates of unemployment among autistic people because they Suffer From Autism™️

It's like systematically breaking someone's legs, then blaming their inability to run on a medical condition. The unemployment isn't a symptom of autism—it's a symptom of workplace discrimination.

original:
I have worked in the games industry for almost 20 years. My first layoff hit hard. The place was my home and the people were my friends. Also, I had landed my dream industry, would I really be lucky twice?

I learned a few things.

Don't make your job your home. Enjoy the hell out of it.

reading_level.rewrite:
Having accrued nearly two decades of professional experience within the interactive entertainment sector, I encountered my inaugural involuntary termination, an experience that proved psychologically destabilizing given the profound institutional identification I had cultivated. The organization functioned as my de facto domicile, whilst my colleagues constituted my primary social infrastructure. Furthermore, having attained what I perceived as my vocational apotheosis, I harbored considerable existential uncertainty regarding the probability of experiencing comparable professional fortune recurrently.

This juncture precipitated several substantive insights.

Delineate categorical boundaries between occupational engagement and domestic sanctuary. Cultivate intensive professional satisfaction whilst maintaining psychological separation.

background.rewrite: Twenty years in, my first layoff shattered me—I'd made the studio my identity. But I learned fast: don't conflate workplace with home. Love what you do, but keep that boundary. Lightning doesn't strike twice, and you need to be ready when it doesn't.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
I have worked in my field for nearly two decades. My first experience with sudden job loss was deeply difficult. The organization had become central to my identity and my colleagues had become close. I also wondered: could I ever find another opportunity that felt this right?

This experience taught me important lessons.

Don't allow your work to become your entire life. Find fulfillment in it, but maintain perspective.

tone.rewrite:
I've survived nearly 20 YEARS in this industry. When that first layoff came, it SHATTERED me. That place wasn't just a job—it was my HOME. Those people were my FAMILY. And the panic set in: I'd somehow found my dream role... would lightning strike twice?

But I learned something crucial, and I need you to hear this:

Don't let your job become your home. It will destroy you. Enjoy every second of it instead—fiercely, urgently, NOW.

humor.rewrite:
I've been in the games industry for almost 20 years, and my first layoff taught me a valuable lesson: apparently "dream job" is just corporate speak for "temporary housing situation with free coffee."

The place felt like home. The people felt like family. Turns out they were more like that friend who ghosts you after graduation.

So here's what I learned: don't treat your job like your home. Treat it like a really fun vacation you're lucky to be on—because the moment you unpack your emotional baggage, they're canceling your reservation.

narrativity.rewrite:
After nearly 20 years in the games industry, layoffs are a harsh reality that reshape careers and friendships alike. The industry demands passion but punishes loyalty. Lightning doesn't strike twice—landing a dream role once doesn't guarantee stability or a second opportunity.

The critical lesson: employment is transactional. A job should never function as a home. Engagement and enthusiasm matter, but boundaries are essential. Extract maximum value from your role while protecting yourself from the emotional devastation that comes when organizations inevitably restructure. Enjoyment of work exists within a framework of professional self-preservation.

grounding.rewrite:
I have worked in the games industry for almost 20 years. My first layoff hit hard because I had over-invested emotionally in the workplace and my professional relationships. I questioned whether I would find another opportunity that felt right.

I learned a few things.

Don't let your job become your primary source of identity and belonging. Maintain professional boundaries while still engaging fully in your work.

original:
Like many other autistic people, I have an auditory processing disorder. Although my hearing is fine, it’s difficult for my brain to parse what I hear.
-I may not catch everything people say, especially if they talk fast.
-It takes me a second longer than most people to fully understand what I hear.

reading_level.rewrite: As is the case for numerous individuals on the autism spectrum, I experience auditory processing disorder, a condition wherein peripheral hearing acuity remains intact whilst central auditory processing—the neurological capacity to decode and interpret acoustic stimuli—demonstrates significant impairment. Consequently, I frequently experience difficulty extracting complete linguistic information from spoken discourse, particularly when interlocutors employ accelerated speech rates. Additionally, my auditory comprehension latency exceeds neurotypical norms, necessitating extended processing intervals to achieve full semantic integration of received acoustic input.

background.rewrite: APD hits different when you're autistic. Yeah, my hearing's fine—it's the processing lag that gets me. Miss half the convo if someone talks fast, and I need that extra beat to decode what I'm hearing.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Many neurodivergent individuals experience differences in how their brains process sensory input. Despite typical sensory function, there can be a disconnect between perception and comprehension.

This may manifest as difficulty fully grasping communicated information, particularly when presented at certain speeds or intensities. Additional time may be required for cognitive processing compared to typical patterns.

tone.rewrite:
I'm struggling with an auditory processing disorder like so many autistic people face. My hearing works, but my brain can't keep up—it's a constant battle.

When people speak quickly, I MISS things. Critical things. And there's this lag—that agonizing extra second my brain needs to process what I'm hearing while everyone else has already moved on.

It's exhausting. It's isolating. And it's real.

humor.rewrite: Like many other autistic people, I have an auditory processing disorder. Apparently my ears work fine—it's just my brain that's decided to treat sound like a cryptic puzzle. So yes, I might miss what you said (especially if you're a speed-talker), and yes, I'll need a beat longer to translate those vibrations into actual comprehension. Revolutionary stuff.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was sitting in a coffee shop yesterday when a friend started telling me about her week. She was speaking at her normal pace, but I found myself asking her to repeat herself—twice. It wasn't that I couldn't hear her; it was that my brain was struggling to keep up. This is my reality as an autistic person with auditory processing disorder.

My hearing itself works fine, but somewhere between my ears and my brain, things get jumbled. When people talk fast, words blur together and I miss chunks of what they're saying. And even at a normal pace, I need that extra beat—sometimes two—for my brain to actually process and understand what I've heard. It's like everyone else is reading in real-time while I'm still loading the previous sentence.

grounding.rewrite: I have an auditory processing disorder, which is common among autistic people. My hearing functions normally, but my brain has difficulty processing sound information. I may miss parts of conversations and require additional time to fully comprehend what I hear.

original: Do you over-apologize? Autistic people are more likely to say "sorry" more than is necessary as a defense mechanism "because they have been hurt in past situations that required strong social skills." So, not because they are neurodivergent but because of how others treat ND people.

reading_level.rewrite: Do you say sorry too much? Autistic people apologize more than needed. They do this as a defense. Why? Because they've been hurt before. They learned they need strong social skills to stay safe. This isn't because they're autistic. It's because of how other people treat autistic people.

background.rewrite: Do you over-apologize? Autistic people tend to say "sorry" more than necessary, but here's why: they've often been hurt in past situations where they struggled with social skills that others found confusing or off-putting. They developed excessive apologizing as a defense mechanism—not because autism itself causes it, but because of how society has treated them for being neurodivergent. It's a learned response to repeated negative social experiences.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Do you over-apologize? Autistic people frequently say "sorry" more than necessary as a defense mechanism—a pattern documented in autism research—because they've experienced social rejection and misunderstanding from neurotypical peers who penalize them for missing social cues or unintended rudeness. The over-apologizing isn't caused by autism itself, but by repeated negative interactions with people who lack patience for different communication styles.

tone.rewrite: Stop apologizing for existing. Autistic people are trapped in a cycle of over-apologizing—not because of autism itself, but because society has WOUNDED them. They've learned to say "sorry" obsessively as a shield against the relentless hurt from past situations. This isn't neurodivergence—it's survival. It's the damage inflicted by a world that punishes them for not fitting narrow social expectations. We need to stop breaking our autistic loved ones and demand better.

humor.rewrite: Do you over-apologize? Turns out autistic people say "sorry" way more than necessary—apparently as a defense mechanism "because they have been hurt in past situations that required strong social skills." So basically, we're not broken, society's just great at making us feel like we are.

narrativity.rewrite:
I realized this about myself last week. I was apologizing to my friend for being five minutes late, then apologizing for apologizing, and she gently called me out: "Why do you do that so much?" 

It hit me that I'm not alone. Autistic people apologize constantly—far more than necessary—and I started wondering why. The answer isn't that we're just wired that way. It's that many of us have been hurt in past situations where we felt we needed strong social skills just to survive interactions. We learned that saying sorry first meant protecting ourselves. So really, it's not about being neurodivergent. It's about how others have treated us because we're neurodivergent.

grounding.rewrite: Do you over-apologize? Autistic people are more likely to say "sorry" more than is necessary—like someone who flinches at sudden movements because they've been hurt before, not because of their reflexes, but because of past pain. It's a defense mechanism "because they have been hurt in past situations that required strong social skills." So, not because they are neurodivergent but because of how others treat ND people.

original:
In autistic spaces, we talk a lot about how non-autistic folks talk in code, but we need to talk more about how they listen for code, even when it's not there.

You can tell them exactly what you mean and, because they're looking for code that isn't there, they will. not. get. it

reading_level.rewrite:
Within neurodivergent discourse communities, particularly those centered on autism, there exists considerable discussion regarding the phenomenon of neurotypical communication patterns and their reliance upon implicit signaling; however, there remains insufficient examination of the reciprocal interpretive mechanisms whereby non-autistic individuals engage in systematic decoding processes, frequently locating subtext that remains absent from explicit utterances.

Notwithstanding unambiguous articulation of one's intended meaning, the neurotypical propensity toward detecting embedded communicative codes—codes which fundamentally do not exist within the explicit message—results in systematic comprehension failures and communicative dysfunction.

background.rewrite:
In autistic spaces, we talk a lot about how non-autistic folks communicate indirectly—using hints, sarcasm, and unspoken social rules instead of direct language. But here's something equally important we need to discuss: they also *listen* for that hidden code, even when someone is being completely literal and straightforward.

So when an autistic person tells a non-autistic person exactly what they mean—using clear, direct language—the non-autistic listener is often still searching for a "real" hidden message beneath the words. Because they're trained to hunt for subtext that isn't actually there, they will. not. get. it.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
In autistic spaces, when an autistic person tells a non-autistic person "I need to leave this conversation now," the non-autistic person often hears subtext like "I'm upset with you" or "you did something wrong"—even though the autistic person literally meant they need to leave. Because non-autistic people are trained to decode hidden meanings, they decode meanings that don't exist.

You can state your needs plainly and directly, but because they're listening for implications you never made, they will. not. get. it.

tone.rewrite:
In autistic spaces, we talk a lot about how non-autistic folks talk in code—but we NEED to confront something darker: they're listening for code that ISN'T EVEN THERE.

You can spell it out. Word for word. Crystal clear. And they still won't hear you. They're too busy searching for hidden meanings that don't exist. It's maddening. It's isolating. And it has to stop being invisible.

humor.rewrite:
In autistic spaces, we talk a lot about how non-autistic folks speak in code, but nobody mentions that they *listen* for code too—even when there's absolutely nothing there.

You can spell it out letter by letter, smoke signal by smoke signal, and they'll still somehow miss it. Because their brain is too busy decoding invisible subtext that simply doesn't exist.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was explaining something straightforward to a friend last week—just facts, no subtext. But I watched their face as they waited for the "real" meaning underneath my words. It never came, because there wasn't one. Yet somehow, they still walked away confused.

This is something we talk about constantly in autistic spaces: how non-autistic folks communicate in code. But we don't talk enough about the flip side—how they *listen* for code, even when it's not there.

You can tell them exactly what you mean. Crystal clear. No hidden layers. And because they're scanning for subtext that doesn't exist, they will. not. get. it.

grounding.rewrite:
In autistic spaces, we talk a lot about how non-autistic folks talk in code, but we need to talk more about how they listen for code, even when it's not there.

It's like someone asking "What time is it?" and you answering "3 o'clock" — completely clear and direct. But if they're trained to expect hidden meaning, they might search for subtext that doesn't exist: "Why did they say 3 and not 2:55? What are they really frustrated about?" You can tell them exactly what you mean and, because they're looking for code that isn't there, they will. not. get. it.

original: I am much better at reading & writing than at listening & speaking. This is a common profile for autistics. Many of us have auditory processing delays, so our brains can’t parse spoken language quickly. Spoken communication also involves a lot of non-verbal elements that aren’t natural for us. 🧵1/3

reading_level.rewrite: I'm much better at reading and writing than listening and speaking. A lot of autistic people are like this. Our brains have trouble understanding spoken words fast. Talking also has body language and tone that we find hard to pick up. 🧵1/3

background.rewrite: Autistic reading/writing > listening/speaking profile hits different when you factor in auditory processing delays + the implicit social load of spoken communication. 🧵1/3

abstract_concrete.rewrite: I'm much better at reading & writing than at listening & speaking. This is a documented profile for autistic people: our auditory processing works differently, making it harder for our brains to parse spoken language in real-time. Spoken communication also requires tracking tone, facial expressions, and body language simultaneously—skills that require different neural pathways than text-based communication. 🧵1/3

tone.rewrite: I struggle SO MUCH with listening & speaking—it's genuinely exhausting. But reading & writing? That's where I thrive. And I'm NOT alone in this. Many autistic people experience this exact painful gap. Our auditory processing is delayed, making spoken language feel impossibly fast. And don't even get me started on all those unspoken social cues we're expected to magically understand. It's overwhelming. 🧵1/3

humor.rewrite: I'm fluent in reading and writing, but put me in a conversation and suddenly my brain enters a loading screen. Turns out this is basically the autistic starter pack—our auditory processing moves at dial-up speeds while everyone else is on 5G. Plus, spoken language comes with all these unwritten rules and facial expressions I'm apparently supposed to decode in real-time. No pressure. 🧵1/3

narrativity.rewrite: I realized something about myself the other day while struggling through a phone call. I can write you a 10-page essay without breaking a sweat, but ask me to follow a conversation with multiple people? My brain just... stalls. Turns out, I'm not alone in this. Many autistic people share this exact profile — we're often much better at reading & writing than listening & speaking. The reason is pretty straightforward: auditory processing delays mean our brains can't parse spoken language quickly enough. And then there's the layer that really trips us up — all those non-verbal elements woven into spoken communication that just don't come naturally to us. 🧵1/3

grounding.rewrite: I am much better at reading & writing than at listening & speaking. This is a common neurodevelopmental profile. Auditory processing delays can impair the ability to parse spoken language quickly. Spoken communication also involves non-verbal elements that present challenges for some people. 🧵1/3

original:
A common autism trigger is unfairness--things that go against the societal rules that people grind into autistic heads so we can pretend to be "normal" by some arbitrary, fucked up measure of normal.

Reading the news right now is a minefield for unfairness meltdowns.

reading_level.rewrite:
Autistic people often get really upset about unfairness. Society teaches us strict rules so we can seem "normal." But normal is just made up anyway.

The news right now has so much unfairness. It's hard not to have a meltdown reading it.

background.rewrite:
A common autism trigger is unfairness—but to understand why, it helps to know that autistic people are often intensely taught (by parents, teachers, society) to memorize and follow social rules strictly, so we can appear "normal" by whatever arbitrary standard people decide is acceptable. When reality violates those very rules we've been drilled to follow, it creates cognitive and emotional friction that can spark meltdowns.

Reading the news right now is a minefield for unfairness meltdowns, because so many current events contradict the rules we've internalized.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Autistic people are taught rigid social rules to mask and appear "normal"—a standard society arbitrarily decides. When we encounter violations of these rules, we often experience meltdowns triggered by the perceived unfairness.

Recent news cycles—from political hypocrisy to corporate policy changes to inconsistent enforcement of laws—hit this pressure point hard. Stories about rule-breaking by powerful people while everyday people face consequences, or institutions changing standards without explanation, are particularly destabilizing right now.

tone.rewrite:
A common autism trigger is unfairness—particularly situations that contradict the social rules that are heavily emphasized to autistic individuals as necessary for conforming to conventional standards of normalcy.

Given current events, news consumption presents frequent exposure to these kinds of situations, which can be challenging to process.

humor.rewrite:
A common autism trigger is unfairness—those arbitrary, fucked up "rules" society lovingly grinds into our heads so we can perform neurotypical adequately enough to fool people at parties.

Reading the news right now is basically a speedrun of meltdowns. I'm speedrunning meltdowns, actually.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was scrolling through the news this morning, jaw clenched, when another headline hit me sideways. It wasn't even the worst one—just another small injustice buried between the bigger ones. And I felt that familiar tightness creeping in, that pre-meltdown pressure building behind my eyes.

Because here's the thing about being autistic: we're taught these rigid, arbitrary rules about how to be "normal," drilled into us until they become part of our nervous system. And one of the consequences? We develop this acute sensitivity to unfairness—a kind of hypervigilance to things that violate the social contract we've been forced to memorize.

Right now, reading the news feels like walking through a minefield. Every other story is another violation, another rule-breaking, another thing that shouldn't be this way. And I'm not sure how much more my system can take before it all just... breaks.

grounding.rewrite:
A common autism trigger is unfairness—things that violate the rigid societal rules hammered into autistic heads so we can perform "normal" by some arbitrary, fucked up measure. It's like being forced to memorize a rulebook, then watching everyone else casually ignore the rules while you're penalized for the slightest deviation.

Reading the news right now is a minefield for unfairness meltdowns.

original:
“When [architects] Design for Autism, We Design Better for Everyone.”

Also: “Every stakeholder, engagement, survey, interview, or focus group that I did with parents, teachers, and autistic students themselves, the first issue we always talked about was noise and how to mitigate and manage it.”

reading_level.rewrite:
"When architects design for autism, they design better for everyone."

Also: "Every time I talked to parents, teachers, and autistic students, noise came up first. They all wanted to know how to reduce it and control it."

background.rewrite:
"When architects design spaces with autism in mind—considering sensory needs like sound management—the result is better for all of us. This inclusive approach benefits people with hearing sensitivities, anxiety, ADHD, and anyone seeking calmer environments.

During my research, I spoke with parents, teachers, and autistic students through surveys, interviews, and focus groups. Across every single conversation, noise kept coming up as the top concern. Everyone agreed: managing and reducing unwanted sound was the first priority we needed to solve."

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
"When we design with consideration for neurodivergent needs, we create environments that benefit the broader population."

Also: "Across all stakeholder interactions and research methodologies, a consistent priority emerged around environmental sensory factors and their management strategies."

tone.rewrite:
"When architects design for autism, they design better for everyone—and it's time we finally got serious about it.

Parents. Teachers. Autistic students themselves. Every single conversation I had told the same urgent story: noise is destroying their ability to learn and thrive. We can't keep ignoring this."

humor.rewrite:
"When [architects] design for autism, turns out we accidentally design better for *everyone* — who knew inclusivity was just good design in a trench coat?

Also: Every single stakeholder I talked to — parents, teachers, actual autistic humans — led with the same complaint. Spoiler alert: it was noise. Apparently, that's what we've been getting wrong this whole time."

narrativity.rewrite: I'll never forget sitting in a school hallway during my first community listening session, when a teacher pulled me aside and said, "You want to know what keeps autistic kids from learning? It's the noise." That conversation stuck with me through every parent interview, every student focus group, every stakeholder meeting that followed. Again and again, the same concern surfaced: noise and how to manage it. That's when it hit me — when architects design for autism, we're actually designing better for everyone.

grounding.rewrite:
"Design that accommodates autism benefits all users."

Also: "Across all stakeholder engagement, surveys, interviews, and focus groups with parents, teachers, and autistic students, noise management consistently emerged as the primary concern."

original: Researchers find that "autistic children and adolescents as young as 10 years old engage in camouflaging behaviour which can be pervasive and automatic, & that this can be a stressful, confusing, and energetically draining experience." Small but damning exploratory study. More such research please!

reading_level.rewrite: Researchers found that autistic kids as young as 10 hide who they really are. They do this a lot and often without thinking about it. This hiding is stressful. It's confusing. It drains their energy. This is a small study. But it shows something important. We need more research like this!

background.rewrite: Autism is often thought of as only affecting young children, but new research reveals something important about older autistic kids too. Researchers studied autistic children and adolescents as young as 10 years old and discovered they engage in "camouflaging behaviour"—essentially masking their autistic traits to fit in socially. The study found this masking is often pervasive and automatic (happening without conscious effort), and critically, that it takes a significant toll: it's stressful, confusing, and energetically draining for these young people. While this is a small exploratory study with limitations, the findings are concerning enough to warrant more research in this area.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Research suggests that neurodivergent individuals across developmental stages may engage in masking behaviours that are widespread and operate without conscious effort, potentially creating significant psychological and physiological strain. A preliminary investigation highlights this phenomenon. Further inquiry into this area would be valuable.

tone.rewrite: STOP ignoring this: researchers have discovered that autistic children as young as 10 are FORCED into exhausting camouflaging behaviors—constant, automatic, relentless. This isn't just stressful or confusing. It's DRAINING them. We need answers NOW. Demand more research before another generation suffers in silence.

humor.rewrite: Researchers discovered that autistic kids as young as 10 are basically method actors 24/7, except the "performance" is just... existing in public. Turns out masking your entire personality is *shockingly* stressful, confusing, and exhausting. Who would've thought? (Everyone. Everyone would've thought.) Tiny study, but the findings hit different. We need way more of this.

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through my feed last night when I came across a study that stopped me cold. A 10-year-old in the research had described masking their autism as feeling like "wearing a costume all day." That image stuck with me. Turns out, researchers have found that autistic children and adolescents as young as 10 are engaging in camouflaging behaviour which can be pervasive and automatic—and it's taking a real toll. The study describes it as stressful, confusing, and energetically draining. It's a small but damning exploratory study, and honestly? We need so much more research like this.

grounding.rewrite: Autistic children and adolescents as young as 10 years old engage in camouflaging behavior that can be pervasive and automatic. Research indicates this camouflaging can be stressful, confusing, and energetically draining. More research on this phenomenon is needed.

original:
Please don't force autistic people to make eye contact. Why? "Because it takes less energy for me to not do it than to do it or even approximate it. I am engaged in a conversation even if I look distracted." So says Ira Eidle, at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism.

[image: Photo of a surprised cat.]

reading_level.rewrite:
Don't make autistic people look you in the eye. Why not? Ira Eidle explains: "It uses less energy when I don't do it. I can still listen and care about talking with you, even if I look away." She writes for Thinking Person's Guide to Autism.

[image: Photo of a surprised cat.]

background.rewrite:
Many people assume that eye contact signals engagement in conversation, but autistic people often experience this differently. According to Ira Eidle, writing for Thinking Person's Guide to Autism—a resource created by autistic adults and parents—forcing autistic individuals to make eye contact can be counterproductive. Here's why: "Because it takes less energy for me to not do it than to do it or even approximate it. I am engaged in a conversation even if I look distracted." For autistic people, maintaining eye contact requires significant cognitive effort that can actually drain focus from listening and participating. When autistic people aren't making eye contact, they're often more present and engaged than they appear. So next time, please let autistic people communicate in the way that works best for them.

[image: Photo of a surprised cat.]

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Please don't force autistic people to make eye contact. Why? Because maintaining it requires significant cognitive effort for many, while their engagement in conversation remains unaffected by where their gaze is directed. As many autistic individuals have explained, they can participate fully in dialogue without this form of visual contact.

[image: Photo of a surprised cat.]

tone.rewrite:
STOP forcing autistic people to make eye contact. It's DRAINING. As Ira Eidle from Thinking Person's Guide to Autism urgently points out: "It takes less energy for me to not do it than to do it or even approximate it. I am engaged in a conversation even if I look distracted." We need to listen. NOW.

[image: Photo of a surprised cat.]

humor.rewrite:
Please don't force autistic people to make eye contact. Shocking revelation: staring into someone's eyeballs actually requires *energy*—wild concept, I know. As Ira Eidle notes at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism, "It takes less energy for me to not do it than to do it or even approximate it. I am engaged in a conversation even if I look distracted." Turns out you can listen AND look literally anywhere else. Revolutionary stuff.

[image: Photo of a surprised cat.]

narrativity.rewrite:
I was reading through Thinking Person's Guide to Autism the other day when I came across something that completely shifted my perspective. Ira Eidle was describing what happens when people insist autistic folks make eye contact, and it hit me: "Because it takes less energy for me to not do it than to do it or even approximate it. I am engaged in a conversation even if I look distracted."

That one quote changed everything. It made me realize how much we've gotten wrong about what engagement actually looks like. Please don't force autistic people to make eye contact. Just because someone isn't looking you in the eye doesn't mean they're not fully present in the conversation.

grounding.rewrite: Please don't force autistic people to make eye contact. Many autistic individuals report that maintaining eye contact requires significant mental effort and can actually interfere with their ability to focus on and engage in conversation. A person can be fully present and attentive in a discussion without using eye contact as a marker of engagement.

original: While Autistic flow (or “hyperfocus”) can be “the most amazing feeling in the world,” it can also cause challenges. Solution? Working WITH Autistic people on strategies to help ALL Autistics manage their quality of life. New research!

reading_level.rewrite: Autistic hyperfocus feels great. But it can cause problems too. The answer? Work WITH Autistic people to find new ways to help everyone. New research shows this works!

background.rewrite: While Autistic flow (or "hyperfocus" — intense, absorbed focus on a single interest) can feel "the most amazing feeling in the world," it comes with a flip side. Many Autistic people struggle when hyperfocus causes them to neglect sleep, food, or other responsibilities, disrupting daily functioning. Rather than trying to eliminate this natural state, researchers are now working directly WITH Autistic people — not just studying them — to develop personalized strategies that help ALL Autistics harness hyperfocus's benefits while managing its downsides. New research!

abstract_concrete.rewrite: While Autistic hyperfocus—the intense concentration on a single task—can feel incredible, it often leads to neglecting sleep, meals, and social connections. A recent study from autism researchers found that structured break reminders and visual timers help many Autistic adults maintain both productivity and self-care. The key: partner with Autistic people directly when designing these tools, not for them.

tone.rewrite: Autistic hyperfocus isn't just incredible—it's CONSUMING. Yes, it can feel transcendent, but it's also DESTROYING our sleep, our bodies, our relationships. We can't keep ignoring this. We NEED to listen to Autistic voices NOW and develop real strategies that actually WORK for us. The research is here. The time to act is NOW.

humor.rewrite: While Autistic flow (or "hyperfocus") can feel like you've unlocked cheat codes for productivity—right up until you forget to eat for 8 hours—it's not all vibes. Turns out, working *with* Autistic people on actual strategies (revolutionary concept, we know) might help everyone manage their quality of life. New research confirms it!

narrativity.rewrite:
I was talking with an Autistic friend last week about deep work, and she described hyperfocus like this: "It's the most amazing feeling in the world—time disappears, nothing else matters, pure flow." Then she paused. "But yesterday I forgot to eat for 12 hours and missed my partner's birthday dinner." 

That conversation stuck with me, because it captures something new research is exploring: while Autistic hyperfocus can be incredible, it also comes with real challenges. The answer isn't to suppress it—it's working WITH Autistic people to develop strategies that help everyone manage their quality of life, not just chase the high.

grounding.rewrite: While Autistic flow (or "hyperfocus") can feel like being completely absorbed in a video game for hours—"the most amazing feeling in the world"—it can also backfire: skipping meals, missing sleep, or forgetting appointments. Solution? Working WITH Autistic people on strategies to help ALL Autistics manage their quality of life. New research!

original: Autistic brains can be in danger of overload while grocery shopping. When that happens autistics needs a quiet space—fast. But there are usually none in big stores. Ann Memmott writes about Being Overwhelmed By Grocery Shopping While Autistic, at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism.

reading_level.rewrite: Autistic people can get overwhelmed at the grocery store. Their brains get too full. When this happens, they need a quiet place. But big stores don't have quiet spaces. Ann Memmott wrote about this topic. She discusses how grocery shopping can be hard for autistic people. You can read her article on Thinking Person's Guide to Autism.

background.rewrite: Autistic people often experience sensory overload—when their brains receive too much input from sights, sounds, and stimuli at once—during everyday activities like grocery shopping. Big stores with fluorescent lights, loud noises, and crowds can trigger this overwhelm, leaving autistic shoppers needing immediate access to a calm, quiet space to recover. However, most large grocery stores don't offer designated quiet areas for this purpose. Ann Memmott explores this challenge in her piece "Being Overwhelmed By Grocery Shopping While Autistic" on Thinking Person's Guide to Autism, discussing why this basic accommodation matters for autistic shoppers.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Sensory environments can pose challenges for neurodivergent individuals, potentially leading to overwhelming experiences. When overwhelm occurs, access to calm spaces becomes essential for regulation. However, many commercial settings lack appropriate accommodations for such needs. Resources exist exploring how certain populations navigate these common environmental stressors.

tone.rewrite: AUTISTIC OVERLOAD IS REAL AND STORES ARE FAILING US. When sensory chaos hits during grocery shopping, autistic people NEED a quiet refuge—NOW. But where is it? Big retailers offer NOTHING. Ann Memmott's urgent piece on Being Overwhelmed By Grocery Shopping While Autistic at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism exposes this critical gap we can't ignore.

humor.rewrite: Autistic brains have a delightful tendency to short-circuit in grocery stores—you know, those temples of fluorescent chaos and beeping registers. When sensory overload hits, we desperately need a quiet space. Spoiler alert: big-box stores don't exactly stock those on aisle 7. Ann Memmott explores this thrilling phenomenon in "Being Overwhelmed By Grocery Shopping While Autistic" at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism.

narrativity.rewrite: I'll never forget the day my friend Maya had to abandon a full shopping cart and sit in her car for twenty minutes just to breathe. She's autistic, and the fluorescent lights, beeping registers, and crowd noise had pushed her nervous system past its breaking point. That experience opened my eyes to something many people don't realize: autistic brains can slip into dangerous overload during grocery shopping, and when that happens, they desperately need a quiet space—fast. The problem? Big stores rarely have one available. Ann Memmott explores this exact struggle in her piece "Being Overwhelmed By Grocery Shopping While Autistic" at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism, and it's a must-read for anyone who wants to understand what that experience is really like.

grounding.rewrite: Autistic individuals can experience sensory overload in high-stimulation environments. When this occurs, access to quiet spaces is essential for regulation. However, such accommodations are often unavailable in many public venues. Ann Memmott discusses this challenge in her article at Thinking Person's Guide to Autism.

original:
(1/2) My Autism from the Inside: Recognizing Disinterest
