A huge part of learning to talk to others was about training to recognize disinterest. Like many autistic folks, I easily hyperfixate and when given the chance, am pretty singleminded about discussing it. This creates a unique problem.

reading_level.rewrite:
(1/2) My Autism from the Inside: Spotting When People Aren't Interested

I had to learn how to notice when people weren't interested. Many autistic people, including me, get very focused on one topic. We can talk about it for hours. But this causes a real problem.

background.rewrite:
(1/2) Autistic hyperfixation kills small talk.

I had to actively train myself to read disinterest signals because my natural inclination is to monologue about whatever's captured my attention. Most neurotypical conversations die when you don't notice—or can't stop—the other person checking out.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
(1/2) My Autism from the Inside: Recognizing Disinterest

A huge part of learning to talk to others was about training to recognize when someone's eyes glaze over, their responses get shorter, or they change the subject. Like many autistic folks, I hyperfixate on topics like my special interests—I've spent weeks deep-diving into obscure music production techniques or Victorian architecture—and when given the chance, I'll monologue about them for 45 minutes straight. This creates a unique problem.

tone.rewrite:
(1/2) My Autism from the Inside: The Disinterest Crisis

I need to be honest about something that's haunted me for years. Like countless autistic people, I get consumed by hyperfixations—completely absorbed, unable to stop talking about them even when it's painfully clear nobody cares. It's devastating. Learning to read disinterest has become survival, not just a social skill. I had to teach myself this the hard way, and the damage it caused? Still processing it. This isn't just awkward—it's a fundamental struggle that defines so much of my existence.

humor.rewrite:
(1/2) My Autism from the Inside: Recognizing Disinterest

Turns out, mastering human conversation requires developing a sixth sense for when people's eyes have officially glazed over. As an autistic person, I have the gift of hyperfixation—which is basically a superpower for boring people to tears about one topic. Revolutionary stuff. The irony? I'm actually pretty good at it.

narrativity.rewrite:
I remember sitting across from a friend at coffee, launching into a 20-minute explanation of my special interest while they kept glancing at their phone. Midway through, it hit me: they weren't following along—they'd checked out five minutes in. That moment became the turning point in my autism journey.

I realized that a huge part of learning to talk to others meant training myself to recognize disinterest. Like many autistic folks, I easily hyperfixate and when given the chance, I'm pretty singleminded about discussing it. This creates a unique problem—one I'd been completely blind to until that coffee shop conversation forced me to see it.

grounding.rewrite:
(1/2) My Autism from the Inside: Recognizing Disinterest

A huge part of learning to talk to others was about training to recognize disinterest. As an autistic person, I tend to become intensely focused on specific topics and can be very single-minded in conversations about them. This creates a unique communication challenge that requires deliberate attention to others' responses.

original:
"High functioning" autism is too often conflated with being "not disabled".

The reality is, being "high functioning" is just being able to mask yourself and your autistic features - it's a performance.

Try putting on a performance 40+ hours a week on top of your actual job duties.

reading_level.rewrite:
The prevalent conflation of "high functioning" autism with non-disability status represents a fundamental mischaracterization of neurodevelopmental presentation and functional capacity.

The substantive reality encompasses the phenomenon of masking—the deliberate suppression and camouflaging of autistic characteristics—which constitutes performative neurotypical alignment rather than genuine absence of disability.

The cumulative cognitive and emotional expenditure demanded by sustained masking protocols, implemented concurrently with occupational responsibilities spanning 40+ hours weekly, precipitates substantial psychological and physiological burden.

background.rewrite: Many people use the term "high functioning" autism to describe autistic people who can hold jobs or seem "normal" in public. But here's what that label actually means: it's not that these autistic people aren't disabled—it's that they're skilled at hiding their autism and managing their symptoms in front of others. This hiding is exhausting. Imagine having to constantly monitor and suppress your natural behaviors, communication style, and sensory needs while simultaneously doing your actual work duties. That's 40+ hours a week of performance on top of everything else. The real disability doesn't disappear just because someone can mask it well enough to get by.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
"High functioning" autism is too often conflated with being "not disabled."

The reality is, being "high functioning" means you've learned to mask stimming, eye contact avoidance, sensory overload responses, and social communication differences—it's a performance you maintain constantly.

Try masking your autistic traits for 40+ hours a week at your job, then coming home to handle your actual responsibilities. That's the exhaustion autistic people experience daily.

tone.rewrite:
"High functioning" autism is being weaponized to erase our disabilities.

The brutal truth: "high functioning" means we're just better at masking—at performing neurotypicality while screaming inside. We're not less disabled. We're just exhausted from the act.

Imagine forced performances 40+ hours weekly ON TOP of your actual job. That's our reality. And nobody calls it what it is: unsustainable.

humor.rewrite:
"High functioning" autism is just a fancy way of saying "really good at pretending to be neurotypical." Congrats on your Oscar-worthy masking performance! 🎭

The thing is, maintaining that act for 40+ hours a week *on top of* actually doing your job? That's not a flex—that's exhausting. But sure, let's keep calling it "not disabled."

narrativity.rewrite:
I was at lunch with a friend last week when she mentioned her autistic coworker as "high functioning, so probably fine." It made me pause. I thought about how that same coworker had told me they go home completely drained after work—not from the job itself, but from the constant performance of seeming "normal."

That's when it hit me: "high functioning" autism doesn't mean not disabled. It means being able to mask—to perform your way through the world. It means suppressing your natural autistic features for 40+ hours a week, then coming home exhausted from that performance alone, before you even factor in your actual job duties.

That's not the absence of disability. That's disability plus a full-time performance contract nobody signed up for.

grounding.rewrite:
"High functioning" autism is too often conflated with being "not disabled".

The reality is, being "high functioning" is just being able to mask yourself and your autistic features - it's a performance. It's like being a method actor who never leaves character: you're not just acting during your 8-hour shift, you're maintaining that role through 40+ hours of work each week, plus commute, plus everything else. The exhaustion is real, even if it doesn't show.

original: Newly diagnosed autistic adults sometimes find that elation at having explanations for one's ENTIRE LIFE may devolve into imposter syndrome. Please know that it's REALLY hard to get an autism diagnosis as an adult, and autistics are far, far more likely to be denied a diagnosis than mis-diagnosed.

reading_level.rewrite: Getting an autism diagnosis as an adult can bring joy. You finally understand your whole life. But then doubt can creep in. You might feel like a fake. Please remember this: adult autism diagnosis is very hard to get. Doctors deny autism much more than they wrongly give it. Your diagnosis is real.

background.rewrite: Newly diagnosed autistic adults sometimes find that the initial joy of finally having explanations for struggles they've experienced their whole life can shift into doubting whether the diagnosis is really accurate—a feeling called imposter syndrome. It's important to understand why this happens: getting an autism diagnosis as an adult is genuinely difficult because many diagnosticians were trained to recognize autism primarily in children, and diagnostic criteria have evolved significantly over time. Research shows that autistic people—especially those who've masked their traits—are much more likely to be turned away or told they don't meet criteria than they are to receive a false diagnosis. So if you've made it through the diagnostic process, your diagnosis is likely well-founded.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Newly diagnosed autistic adults sometimes find that elation at having explanations for lifelong struggles with social communication, sensory processing, or executive function may devolve into imposter syndrome. Please know that adult autism evaluations typically require multiple appointments with a specialized clinician, comprehensive developmental history review, and standardized assessments like the ADOS-2—making diagnosis harder to obtain than many realize. Studies show autistic individuals are far more likely to be denied diagnosis or go undiagnosed than to receive false positives.

tone.rewrite: STOP dismissing your diagnosis. If you're newly diagnosed as autistic, DO NOT let imposter syndrome steal this from you. Getting diagnosed as an autistic adult is BRUTALLY hard—nearly impossible for many. The odds were AGAINST you the entire time. You fought through systemic barriers that deny countless autistic people recognition. Your diagnosis is REAL. You EARNED it. Don't you dare question it now.

humor.rewrite: Newly diagnosed autistic adults sometimes find that the initial "oh THAT explains everything" euphoria slowly transforms into "wait, am I actually autistic or just dramatic?" Brain, we both know you didn't sail through that diagnostic gauntlet by accident. Getting an adult autism diagnosis is basically running an obstacle course blindfolded, and spoiler alert: autistic people are way more likely to be told "you're fine" than "surprise, you're autistic!"

narrativity.rewrite:
I'll never forget sitting in that clinic waiting room, hands shaking as I held my diagnosis letter. After 30+ years of feeling like something was "off," I finally had a name for it. But within days, that relief twisted into doubt—*Am I really autistic? Maybe I'm just faking it.*

If you're spiraling like I did, please hear this: getting diagnosed as an adult is brutally hard. The gatekeeping is real. Autistics get turned away constantly, told they're "not autistic enough" or that we're just anxious or gifted. The truth? You're far more likely to be denied a diagnosis than to somehow slip through as a false positive. If you made it through to that letter in your hand, you *earned* it.

grounding.rewrite: Newly diagnosed autistic adults sometimes find that elation at having explanations for one's ENTIRE LIFE may devolve into imposter syndrome. Think of it like finally understanding why a car's engine keeps stalling—you got answers, but then wonder if the mechanic really knew what they were doing. Please know that it's REALLY hard to get an autism diagnosis as an adult. Getting denied is like trying to board a plane with a valid ticket but being turned away anyway—autistics face this rejection far more often than they're ever mistakenly let through.

original: On supporting communication in autistic/disabled students, especially those whose speech capability fluctuates: “There is a definite link between my physical energy & fatigue levels & my speech production & communication. Hence the topic of this post AAC, spoons and speech fatigue.” By Beth Moulam.

reading_level.rewrite: About helping autistic and disabled students talk, especially when their speech changes day to day: "My energy and tiredness directly affect how much I can talk and communicate. That's why I'm writing about AAC, spoons, and speech fatigue." - Beth Moulam.

background.rewrite:
On supporting communication in autistic/disabled students, especially those whose speech capability fluctuates: "There is a definite link between my physical energy & fatigue levels & my speech production & communication. Hence the topic of this post AAC, spoons and speech fatigue." By Beth Moulam.

For context: AAC (Augmentative and Alternative Communication) refers to tools like text-to-speech apps that help people communicate when speaking is difficult. "Spoons" is a disability metaphor where each spoon represents limited daily energy—using spoons for one activity leaves fewer for others. Speech fatigue occurs when the physical effort of talking depletes someone's energy reserves, making speech increasingly difficult as the day progresses.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: On supporting communication in disabled populations, particularly those experiencing variable expressive capacity: "There exists a documented relationship between physiological states and communicative output across varying circumstances. This connection underscores the importance of examining assistive communication frameworks within broader contexts of resource management and communicative sustainability." By Beth Moulam.

tone.rewrite: Supporting autistic/disabled students means recognizing something critical: speech isn't constant. Energy crashes. Fatigue hits hard. And communication suffers. As Beth Moulam points out, there's a direct connection between physical exhaustion and the ability to speak. This matters. AAC isn't optional—it's essential. We can't ignore speech fatigue anymore.

humor.rewrite: On supporting communication in autistic/disabled students, especially those whose speech capability fluctuates: "Turns out my brain didn't get the memo that communication should work the same way every single day. Shocking, I know. Anyway, here's a post about AAC, spoons, and why my mouth sometimes decides to take an unscheduled vacation." By Beth Moulam.

narrativity.rewrite: I was sitting with Beth the other day when she mentioned something that had been weighing on her. Some days, she said, she could barely get words out—not because she didn't have thoughts, but because her body was exhausted. That's when she shared the realization that had sparked an entire post: "There is a definite link between my physical energy & fatigue levels & my speech production & communication." It was a lightbulb moment for me, understanding how deeply connected everything is. She'd gone on to explore this connection through the lens of AAC, spoons, and speech fatigue—a conversation we urgently need to have when supporting autistic and disabled students whose ability to communicate shifts day to day.

grounding.rewrite: Supporting autistic and disabled students requires recognizing that communication ability is directly affected by physical energy and fatigue levels. This variability in speech production capacity is an important factor in designing inclusive communication support systems.

original: With all of the layoffs going on in the gaming industry lately, we're starting to wonder what games are going to look like in the next few years, with teams everywhere being slashed. Our hearts go out to the devs affected, with hope everyone lands on their feet.

reading_level.rewrite: Amidst the unprecedented wave of workforce reductions permeating the gaming sector, we find ourselves contemplating the trajectory of interactive entertainment's creative landscape, particularly given the systemic attrition of development personnel across studios. Our sincere condolences extend to the affected practitioners within this domain, coupled with optimistic aspirations regarding their professional reintegration.

background.rewrite: With all of the layoffs going on in the gaming industry lately—where major studios like Microsoft, Sony, and Embracer have cut thousands of jobs over the past year—we're starting to wonder what games are going to look like in the next few years, with teams everywhere being slashed and projects cancelled or delayed. Our hearts go out to the devs affected, with hope everyone lands on their feet.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: The gaming industry has cut over 13,000 jobs in 2023 alone, with major layoffs at Activision Blizzard, Microsoft Gaming, Unity, and PlayStation Studios. We're concerned about how these decimated teams will affect upcoming releases and new IP development. Our hearts go out to the affected developers—we hope everyone finds their next opportunity quickly.

tone.rewrite: The gaming industry is in crisis. Devastating layoffs are destroying teams across the board, and we're facing a terrifying question: what will games even look like in a few years? Thousands of talented developers are losing their livelihoods right now. We're heartbroken for everyone affected and desperately hoping they find their way forward.

humor.rewrite: With half the gaming industry apparently deciding to do a speedrun of downsizing, we're genuinely curious what games will look like when the dust settles. Spoiler alert: probably fewer people making them. Sending serious support to all the devs caught in this mess—here's hoping your next gig is way better than your last one.

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through the news this morning when another gaming studio announced layoffs. Again. And I couldn't help but think about all the talented devs losing their jobs right now—the artists, programmers, designers who poured their hearts into the games we love. It got me wondering: what's going to happen to the games we play in a few years when teams everywhere are being cut down to the bone? Our hearts are with everyone affected, and we're genuinely hoping everyone lands on their feet.

grounding.rewrite: With all of the layoffs going on in the gaming industry lately, we're starting to wonder what games are going to look like in the next few years. It's like watching a film studio cut half its crew mid-production—you end up with fewer hands to finish the project, longer timelines, and sometimes corners get cut. Our hearts go out to the devs affected, with hope everyone lands on their feet.

original:
Hypermobility and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome are extremely common co-occurring conditions in autistic people—and also in their siblings.

If your child has trouble in physical education (PE) class or unexplained pain, especially around puberty, please persist in having them screened.

reading_level.rewrite:
Hypermobility and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome are very common in autistic people. They also show up a lot in their brothers and sisters.

If your child struggles in PE class or has pain they can't explain—especially during puberty—keep asking doctors to check for these conditions.

background.rewrite:
Hypermobility (excessive joint flexibility) and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (a connective tissue disorder affecting collagen) are extremely common conditions that often appear together in autistic people—and also frequently in their non-autistic siblings.

If your child has trouble keeping up in physical education class, complains of unexplained pain, or shows these signs especially as they hit puberty, don't accept dismissive answers. Ask your doctor to screen them for these conditions, even if initial appointments seem inconclusive.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Genetic connective tissue conditions frequently co-occur with autism across family systems and may emerge or intensify during developmental transitions.

Persistent physical symptoms warrant thorough medical evaluation, particularly when manifestations appear during periods of significant bodily change.

tone.rewrite:
Your child might be suffering in silence right now. Hypermobility and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome are shockingly prevalent in autistic individuals—AND their siblings are at serious risk too.

If your child is struggling through PE or complaining of mysterious pain, especially as they hit puberty, don't let it slide. Push for screening NOW. Don't accept dismissals. This could be the answer you've been desperately searching for.

humor.rewrite:
Turns out your autistic kid's bendy joints and your other kid's mysterious pain aren't character flaws—they're actually hypermobility and EDS, conditions that apparently love to show up together like an unwanted package deal. 

If your child is mysteriously failing PE or complaining about pain (especially when puberty decides to crash the party), maybe don't assume they're just being dramatic. Get them screened. Revolutionary concept, I know.

narrativity.rewrite:
I'll never forget watching my cousin struggle through PE class, wincing every time she ran. For years, her parents chalked it up to being "just not athletic." Then came the diagnosis: hypermobility, later followed by Ehlers-Danlos syndrome. What struck me most? Learning how common this pattern is—especially in autistic kids and their siblings.

If your child is having trouble in PE or complaining of unexplained pain (particularly around puberty), please don't let it slide. Keep pushing for screening. These conditions are far more prevalent in our autistic community than most people realize, and early answers can change everything.

grounding.rewrite:
Hypermobility and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome occur at high rates in autistic individuals and their relatives.

If your child experiences physical difficulties or chronic pain, particularly during adolescence, advocate for proper medical screening to identify these conditions.

original:
What's it like to live masked?

You change yourself to make others comfortable.
You mirror what others expect of you.
You're puppeted by people who put their interests 1st.
You feel like an imposter.

Unmasking is hard, but living as someone else is harder. To unmask is to trust in YOU.

#autistic

reading_level.rewrite:
What constitutes the phenomenological experience of sustained masking?

Systematic self-modification emerges as a primary mechanism to accommodate neurotypical social comfort.
Performative mirroring of externally-imposed behavioral expectations becomes the operational norm.
Agency becomes subordinated to the prioritized interests of external actors.
Persistent identity fragmentation and impostor syndrome manifest as psychological consequences.

Whilst the deconstruction of adaptive personas presents significant challenges, the perpetuation of inauthenticity engenders substantially greater psychological burden. Unmasking necessitates epistemological trust in one's intrinsic neurodivergent identity.

#neurodivergence #autism

background.rewrite:
What's it like to live masked?

For many autistic people, "masking" means suppressing your natural traits—your stimming, your communication style, your interests—to fit neurotypical expectations. Over time, this becomes a pattern:

You change yourself to make others comfortable.
You mirror what others expect of you.
You're puppeted by people who put their interests 1st.
You feel like an imposter.

But here's the thing: constantly performing a version of yourself that isn't real takes enormous energy and takes a toll on your mental health. Unmasking is hard, but living as someone else is harder. To unmask is to trust in YOU.

#autistic

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
What's it like to live masked as an autistic person?

You suppress your stimming, tone down your special interests, and force eye contact to make neurotypical people comfortable.
You mimic the social scripts you've learned instead of communicating naturally.
You're controlled by workplace bosses, family members, and peers who prioritize their comfort over your wellbeing.
You feel like a fraud performing a role, disconnected from your actual thoughts and needs.

Unmasking—stopping the eye contact performance, allowing your stims, speaking directly about your interests—is difficult and risky. But spending your life impersonating someone else drains you faster. To unmask is to trust in your autistic self.

#autistic

tone.rewrite:
What constitutes living masked?

Research suggests several patterns: behavioral adaptation to social expectations, mirroring perceived norms, prioritizing others' comfort over authenticity, and experiencing disconnection from one's core identity.

The evidence indicates that while unmasking presents challenges, sustained inauthenticity carries its own psychological cost. Unmasking requires self-trust and acceptance.

#autistic

humor.rewrite:
What's it like to live masked?

You become a human mirror—which sounds philosophical until you realize you're just exhausted.
You're basically a method actor who never gets to leave the role.
You're basically a marionette, except the strings are made of other people's expectations.
You feel like a fraud, which is ironic because you're actually nailing the performance.

Plot twist: pretending to be someone else is somehow harder than just being yourself. Groundbreaking stuff, I know. To unmask is to trust in YOU—radical concept, really.

#autistic

narrativity.rewrite:
I realized something while sitting in yet another meeting, nodding along and laughing at jokes that didn't land for me. I'd been masking so hard for so long that I couldn't remember the last time I'd just... existed without performing.

That's when it hit me: what's it like to live masked?

You change yourself to make others comfortable. You mirror what others expect of you. You're puppeted by people who put their interests 1st. You feel like an imposter.

The exhaustion is real. But here's what I've learned: unmasking is hard, but living as someone else is harder. To unmask is to trust in YOU.

#autistic

grounding.rewrite:
What's it like to live masked?

It's like being a chameleon in an office full of people who've never seen your real colors. You shift your tone in every meeting, laugh at jokes that don't land for you, and perform energy you don't have — all so coworkers feel at ease. You become the character they expect, not the person you are. You're puppeted by people who put their interests 1st. By day's end, you don't recognize yourself in the mirror.

Unmasking is hard, but living as someone else is harder. To unmask is to trust in YOU.

#autistic

original:
Younger daughter came home upset after a school teacher, referring to my daughter's autism, said, "Well, we're all a little bit autistic."
Well, no. There's no such thing as "a little bit of autism". The spectrum isn't graded on low to high; there's a diversity of strengths and struggles.

reading_level.rewrite:
My younger daughter came home upset. Her teacher said about her autism, "Well, we're all a little bit autistic."

That's not true. You can't be a little bit autistic. Autism isn't about being high or low. It's about having different strengths and different challenges.

background.rewrite: Teacher told my autistic daughter "we're all a little bit autistic." Hard no. Autism isn't a linear spectrum or a personality trait everyone shares—it's neurodevelopmental diversity with distinct support needs and presentations.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
A parent recently experienced their child coming home distressed after an educator made a dismissive comment about neurodivergence, suggesting everyone experiences it to some degree.

This kind of minimization misses the mark. Neurodivergence isn't something people possess in varying amounts. The reality is far more nuanced—it's characterized by a wide range of different support needs and abilities that can't be ranked or compared on a simple scale.

tone.rewrite:
Younger daughter came home after a conversation with her teacher about her autism diagnosis. The teacher had suggested that "we're all a little bit autistic."

This framing doesn't align with current understanding of autism. Autism isn't a linear spectrum ranging from mild to severe, but rather involves a diverse profile of different strengths and support needs that vary by individual.

humor.rewrite:
Younger daughter came home upset after a school teacher decided to casually diagnose themselves while discussing my daughter's autism: "Well, we're all a little bit autistic."

Cool take, teach. Except no—that's not how any of this works. Autism isn't a sliding scale where you collect points like a loyalty card. It's a spectrum of different traits, not a "how much" situation. But sure, let's all pretend we understand neurodiversity now.

narrativity.rewrite: Claiming everyone is "a little bit autistic" is inaccurate. Autism isn't a linear spectrum graded low to high. Autism exists as a diversity of different strengths and struggles—you either have autism or you don't.

grounding.rewrite:
Younger daughter came home upset after a school teacher, referring to my daughter's autism, said, "Well, we're all a little bit autistic."

That comment is inaccurate. Autism isn't something people can have in smaller or larger amounts. Autism exists as a spectrum of different traits and support needs, not as a scale from mild to severe.

original:
CN: Death

We know autistic people die earlier than their non-autistic peers. We need more research on why. But a new analysis estimates "that the widely reported statistic that autistic people live 16-years less on average is likely incorrect," mostly because autism is so under-diagnosed in adults.

reading_level.rewrite:
CN: Death

Autistic people die younger than non-autistic people. We need to understand why. But new research suggests the common claim that autistic people live 16 years less may not be true. The main reason? Autism is often missed in adults.

background.rewrite:
CN: Death

Autism is a lifelong neurological difference that affects how people process information and interact with the world. For decades, researchers have observed that autistic people appear to have shorter lifespans than non-autistic people—with some studies suggesting a gap of around 16 years. This alarming difference has prompted calls for more research into the causes. However, a new analysis challenges this conclusion. It suggests the 16-year figure is probably wrong, pointing to a critical flaw in how the data was gathered: many adults have never been diagnosed with autism, so they weren't counted in these studies. This means the real lifespan gap may be smaller than we thought, though we still don't fully understand why autistic people face health disparities at all.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
CN: Death

We know autistic people experience reduced life expectancy compared to their non-autistic peers. We need more research on the underlying causes. However, new analysis suggests that commonly cited statistics regarding life expectancy disparities may overstate the actual gap, largely due to systemic gaps in diagnostic identification across populations.

tone.rewrite:
CN: Death

Autistic people are dying younger—16 years younger on average—and we're not even asking the right questions. We desperately need research into why. But here's what keeps us up at night: a shocking new analysis reveals this devastating statistic might be built on a lie. Autism is catastrophically under-diagnosed in adults, skewing everything we think we know. We can't save lives we don't even count.

humor.rewrite:
CN: Death

Turns out that "autistic people die 16 years earlier" stat everyone loves citing? Probably wrong. Revolutionary concept, I know: turns out when you don't diagnose autism in adults, you get bad data. We still need research on why autistic people actually do die younger than their peers, but apparently we need to do it without made-up numbers. Novel approach.

narrativity.rewrite:
CN: Death

Last week, I was talking to an autistic friend about her health concerns, and she mentioned something that stuck with me: "Nobody ever properly diagnosed me until I was 35. I wonder what that means for statistics about us." That conversation sent me down a research rabbit hole.

Here's what I found: we've long known autistic people die earlier than non-autistic peers, and we desperately need research into why. But a new analysis just challenged one of the most widely cited figures in this space. Turns out, that statistic claiming autistic people live 16 years less on average? It's probably wrong—largely because autism goes undiagnosed in so many adults. The missing data fundamentally skews what we think we know.

grounding.rewrite:
CN: Death

Autistic people have shorter lifespans than non-autistic people. We need more research on why. However, a new analysis suggests that commonly cited statistics about the magnitude of this lifespan gap may be inaccurate, primarily due to significant diagnostic disparities across different populations.

original: The Game Awards is extremely consumer-facing and therefore doomed to be at odds with its stated purpose of recognizing and honoring the creators of games in an awards show for them. As such it is never not funny

reading_level.rewrite: The Game Awards tries to be for fans. But it's also supposed to honor game creators. These two goals don't work together. So the show will always be a mess. And that's kind of funny.

background.rewrite: The Game Awards is an annual video game awards show that airs on TV and streams online to millions of viewers worldwide. Because it's designed primarily as entertainment for fans rather than an industry event, it's structurally at odds with its stated purpose of recognizing and honoring the actual creators and developers of games. This fundamental conflict between being a spectacle for consumers versus a meaningful awards ceremony for creators means the show is inherently contradictory—which is why it's never not funny to watch unfold.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: The Game Awards' livestream viewership of 90+ million and reliance on celebrity presenters like Dwayne Johnson and Elden Ring's massive sales integration means it prioritizes pleasing mass audiences over recognizing developers. When a three-hour ceremony spends 15 minutes on trailers but awards Game of the Year in 90 seconds, it becomes clear the show's stated mission to honor creators conflicts with its actual format—which is why the contradiction between these priorities never stops being absurd.

tone.rewrite: The Game Awards' consumer-facing approach creates an inherent tension with its stated mission to recognize game creators. This structural conflict is worth noting when evaluating the show's effectiveness as an awards program.

humor.rewrite: The Game Awards: where we gather annually to honor game creators by having a bunch of consumers scream at each other online about why their favorite interactive product is superior. Nothing says "respecting the artists" quite like a three-hour commercial break with occasional game clips. Truly, the comedy writes itself every single year.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was scrolling through my phone last night when a friend texted me asking if I was watching The Game Awards. I wasn't planning to, but as I tuned in, I found myself thinking about the first time I attended a gaming event—back when awards shows actually felt like they were *for* the people making the games. 

Then it hit me: The Game Awards has become so consumer-focused that it's fundamentally at war with itself. It claims to honor creators, yet every decision screams "entertain the masses." And honestly? That contradiction is hilarious. The ceremony will never escape this tension—which means it's never *not* funny to watch unfold.

grounding.rewrite: The Game Awards is like a wedding ceremony where the bride and groom are largely absent while thousands of guests flood in to watch celebrity performances and shop at vendor booths. It's extremely consumer-facing and therefore doomed to be at odds with its stated purpose of recognizing and honoring the creators of games in an awards show for them. As such it is never not funny

original: I don't think I'm ever going to play Apex Legends again. Really fun to play, but otherwise the skill-floor required to keep up with most lobbies is just not worth the time spent on it. It's the sweatiest game to play for most teens out there.

reading_level.rewrite: I've determined that Apex Legends no longer aligns with my gaming preferences due to its prohibitively elevated mechanical skill ceiling relative to the temporal investment required for competitive viability. Whilst the gameplay mechanics demonstrate considerable entertainment value, the prerequisite competency threshold necessitated to maintain parity with contemporary matchmaking pools renders sustained engagement economically inefficient. The game's contemporary ecosystem has consequently cultivated an exceptionally competitive environment that disproportionately discourages casual participation among adolescent demographics.

background.rewrite: I don't think I'm ever going to play Apex Legends again. For context, Apex Legends is a free-to-play battle royale shooter where you play in squads of three. It's really fun mechanically—the gunplay and movement feel smooth. However, the problem is that to compete in most matches, you need to invest serious time practicing advanced techniques like movement mechanics and aim. Most of the players you encounter have already put in hundreds of hours, so the skill gap is huge. For teens especially, it's become one of the sweatiest competitive games out there, meaning it demands way more dedication than the time investment seems worth.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: I'm uninstalling Apex Legends after 200 hours. The TTK fights against players with 10k+ kills and crafted loadouts are exhausting, and I can't compete without grinding ranked every week. Most of my friends quit because the ranked system punishes casual play—you need a 2+ K/D to stay competitive, which takes 15+ hours weekly.

tone.rewrite: I'm DONE with Apex Legends. Yes, it's incredibly fun, but I can't keep up anymore—the skill floor is BRUTAL and I'm exhausted trying. Every lobby feels impossible. This game is absolutely DESTROYING teens like me who just want to enjoy themselves. I can't waste another second on this.

humor.rewrite: I'm officially retiring from Apex Legends. Sure, it's fun—if you enjoy getting absolutely demolished by 16-year-olds with the reflexes of caffeinated squirrels and 10,000 hours logged. The skill floor here isn't a floor, it's a basement, and I'd rather spend my time on literally anything else.

narrativity.rewrite: I was grinding Apex last night, got absolutely demolished by a squad with perfect aim and positioning in my third match. After spending an hour getting stomped, I realized something: I don't think I'm ever going to play this game again. Don't get me wrong—when you're in the zone, it's genuinely fun. But man, the skill-floor to compete in most lobbies is brutal. You need way more time investment than it's worth, and honestly? It's become the sweatiest game I know for most teens trying to just chill and play.

grounding.rewrite: I don't think I'm ever going to play Apex Legends again. It's enjoyable gameplay-wise, but the competitive skill level demanded makes it impractical for casual play. The game requires too much time investment relative to the entertainment value it provides.

original: Less than 2% of console games include queer characters, the advocacy group GLAAD found in its first survey of the gaming industry. Many respondents from states with legislation targeting LGBTQ people said they could express themselves in games in a way they did not feel comfortable doing in reality.

reading_level.rewrite: A new study found that less than 2% of console games have queer characters. The group GLAAD did this research. Some people from states with anti-LGBTQ laws said games let them be themselves. They said they can't do that in real life.

background.rewrite: Less than 2% of console games include queer characters, according to GLAAD—an LGBTQ advocacy organization that conducted the gaming industry's first comprehensive survey on this topic. This low representation matters because many LGBTQ gamers, particularly those living in states that have recently passed laws restricting LGBTQ rights and visibility, reported that video games provided a rare safe space where they could authentically express their identity in ways they felt unsafe doing in their everyday lives.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Research reveals that representation of marginalized communities in mainstream gaming remains significantly limited. Survey data indicates that individuals facing societal restrictions on self-expression report that interactive media provides spaces where they can explore aspects of identity that feel unsafe in their immediate environments. This disparity between virtual and physical freedom of expression underscores broader gaps in inclusive content creation across the industry.

tone.rewrite: STOP. Less than 2% of console games feature queer characters—GLAAD's groundbreaking survey just exposed this stark reality. This isn't just a number. For LGBTQ people living in states with hostile legislation, games represent a lifeline—the ONLY space where they can authentically express themselves without fear. We're talking about basic human dignity being stripped away in the real world, forcing people to turn to virtual worlds just to be seen. How much longer can the gaming industry ignore this crisis?

humor.rewrite: Less than 2% of console games include queer characters, according to GLAAD's groundbreaking discovery that representation in gaming is about as common as a bug-free launch. Meanwhile, players from states with anti-LGBTQ legislation are finding that video games offer something their real lives apparently don't: the radical freedom to just exist.

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through my game library last night when my friend asked me why I never picked characters that looked like me. It made me realize something I'd never really thought about before. Turns out, I'm part of a pretty small club — less than 2% of console games even have queer characters to choose from, according to GLAAD's first-ever survey of the gaming industry. But here's what stuck with me: people from states with laws targeting LGBTQ people told researchers that games were one of the few places they could finally express themselves authentically, in ways they never felt safe doing in the real world.

grounding.rewrite: Queer representation in console gaming remains severely limited, with less than 2% of games including LGBTQ characters according to GLAAD's inaugural industry survey. Gaming provides an important creative outlet for LGBTQ individuals, particularly those living in regions with restrictive legislation targeting their community.

original:
In 2014 the leadership at Nintendo demonstrated how to properly run a video game company. This move saved the jobs of the staff they worked with that would have otherwise been fired.

Nintendo is where they are now as the leaders of the game industry for a reason.

reading_level.rewrite:
In 2014, Nintendo's executive leadership exemplified exemplary organizational stewardship through strategic decision-making that demonstrably preserved workforce retention and mitigated substantial employment disruption. This pivotal intervention precluded the implementation of prospective terminations amongst their collaborative personnel.

Nintendo's preeminent positioning within the gaming industry's competitive hierarchy consequentially reflects the efficacious nature of their institutional governance and administrative acumen.

background.rewrite:
In 2014, Nintendo's Wii U console was struggling with poor sales, and the company faced significant financial losses. Rather than laying off employees during this crisis—a common industry practice—Nintendo's leadership made the unusual decision to cut their own salaries instead, protecting their staff's jobs. This move demonstrated how to properly run a video game company during difficult times.

That decision is part of why Nintendo is where they are now as the leaders of the game industry for a reason.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
Leadership in the gaming industry has demonstrated the importance of making decisions that prioritize workforce stability over short-term gains. Such choices reflect a commitment to organizational sustainability that often leads to long-term success.

Companies that prioritize their people tend to establish themselves as industry leaders for good reason.

tone.rewrite:
STOP. In 2014, Nintendo's leadership made a CRITICAL decision that literally SAVED their employees' jobs—jobs that were on the chopping block. This wasn't just smart business. This was REAL leadership stepping up when it mattered most.

This is WHY Nintendo dominates the industry today. They chose their people. They chose integrity. That's not luck—that's the direct result of decisions that MATTERED.

humor.rewrite:
In 2014, Nintendo's leadership casually solved a problem that apparently requires a PhD in corporate management for everyone else: they didn't fire people. Revolutionary stuff. Those employees they kept around? Turns out they were useful later.

Shocking as it may be, this is apparently why Nintendo is now, you know, actually leading the industry. Who knew retaining talent was a pro gamer move?

narrativity.rewrite:
I'll never forget reading the news in 2014—Nintendo's leadership made a decision that stopped me in my tracks. While other companies were cutting staff left and right, Nintendo's executives took a different path. They made a move that saved the jobs of countless staff members who would have otherwise been shown the door.

That moment stuck with me. It showed me something about how to actually run a video game company the right way. And honestly? That's exactly why Nintendo is where they are now—leading the game industry. There's a reason for that.

grounding.rewrite: In 2014, Nintendo's leadership made a strategic decision that prioritized employee retention over short-term costs. This approach contributed to their current position as a leading force in the gaming industry.

original: i love how mobile game companies clearly know what kind of games people want to play bc they put it in their ads, and then the actual game is completely different because they're allowed to false advertise as much as they want, when they could just make the game from the ads that people want to play

reading_level.rewrite: I find it particularly compelling that mobile gaming enterprises demonstrate explicit comprehension of consumer preferences through their marketing materials, yet systematically deliver products that diverge substantially from these advertised experiences. This phenomenon persists despite the apparent feasibility of developing titles that align with promotional claims, suggesting that regulatory frameworks governing digital advertising fail to adequately penalize such deceptive practices.

background.rewrite: i love how mobile game companies clearly know what kind of games people want to play—they literally test this by featuring certain gameplay in their ads (like puzzle games, match-3 mechanics, or story-driven content) because market research shows those ads get clicks. yet when you download the actual game, it's completely different from what was advertised, often turning into a pay-to-win grind or something unrelated entirely. and they get away with it because app store regulations don't really penalize false advertising the way other industries do. the wild part is: they clearly understand player demand well enough to make engaging ads around it, so they could just make the game from the ads that people actually want to play instead

abstract_concrete.rewrite: i love how mobile game companies run ads showing turn-based RPGs or puzzle games with actual gameplay, then release match-3 clickers with energy systems and $100 battle passes, knowing app store guidelines won't punish misleading ads, when they could literally just develop the game they advertised that people clearly want based on the ad engagement metrics

tone.rewrite: Mobile game companies frequently advertise games with mechanics and features that differ significantly from the final product. This disconnect suggests these companies understand player preferences based on their marketing choices, yet they continue to produce games misaligned with what their ads promise. The question remains why these companies don't simply develop the games they're advertising, particularly given the apparent regulatory gap that currently permits this practice.

humor.rewrite: nothing says "we respect our players" like mobile game companies spending millions on ads for a game that looks fun, then releasing something that makes you wonder if the ad was for an alternate dimension version. wild that they've cracked the code on what people actually want to play and chose violence instead by just... not making that game

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through my phone yesterday and saw this ad for a game that looked incredible — strategy, gorgeous graphics, actual gameplay. So I downloaded it. Ten minutes in, I'm staring at a completely different game. Turns out mobile game companies absolutely know what people want to play because they show it in their ads constantly. The problem? They just... don't make that game. Instead they ship something totally different, and apparently that's fine. Nobody stops them. Wild thing is, they could literally just build the game from the ads that people actually want to play, but instead they choose false advertising because they can get away with it.

grounding.rewrite: i love how mobile game companies clearly know what kind of games people want to play—they literally show it in their ads—yet the actual game is completely different. it's like a restaurant advertising juicy burgers in their commercials, then serving you a sad desk toy when you arrive. they could just make the game from the ads that people want to play, but instead they get to false advertise with basically zero consequences

original: I like how in Stardew Valley you can fight back against a shitty corporate superstore and revitalize a local business but the local businessman is a guy who also sucks and loves money and never spends time with his family and got cucked by a wizard. Realism in games

reading_level.rewrite: The narrative tension in Stardew Valley's mercantile opposition—wherein players systematically dismantle corporate retail hegemony whilst simultaneously revitalizing localized commerce—is particularly compelling given the protagonist's ideological ambiguity; the ostensible beneficiary of this economic disruption embodies neoliberal avarice, demonstrating pathological prioritization of capital accumulation over familial relations whilst simultaneously experiencing cuckolding by an arcane practitioner. This paradoxical characterization reflects considerable verisimilitude regarding systemic economic contradictions.

background.rewrite: I like how in Stardew Valley you can fight back against JojaMart, this massive corporate superstore trying to monopolize your town's economy, and help revitalize Pierre's General Store, the local family business. But here's the thing—Pierre, the owner you're helping, is also kind of a jerk who's obsessed with profit, neglects his wife and kids, and his wife literally had an affair with the wizard living in the mountains. It's refreshingly realistic for a game, showing that local businesses aren't automatically good just because they're not corporate chains.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: I appreciate how certain games let you oppose exploitative megacorporations and restore community enterprises, yet the proprietor you're helping is fundamentally flawed—someone whose values are misaligned and whose personal life suffers from those same misalignments. It's a nuanced take on economic struggle that avoids simple hero narratives. That's the kind of complexity we need more of.

tone.rewrite: Stardew Valley presents an interesting tension: the game frames corporate consolidation as antagonistic, positioning the player to support local business as the virtuous alternative. Yet the local shopkeeper himself embodies his own set of flaws—prioritizing profit over family relationships and ultimately losing his spouse to a rival. The narrative suggests that the problem isn't capitalism itself, but rather how individuals within economic systems choose to operate. It's a more nuanced take on small-town economics than typical indie games offer.

humor.rewrite: In Stardew Valley, it's interesting that you can fight against corporate consolidation and help revitalize a struggling local business—yet the local businessman you're supporting is deeply flawed. He prioritizes profit over family time and has fundamental relationship problems. This kind of nuanced portrayal reflects real-world complexity: the choice between corporate and independent isn't always a choice between good and bad actors.

narrativity.rewrite: I was playing Stardew Valley last night, trying to save Pierre's General Store from being crushed by JojaMart, feeling like I was doing something good. Then I realized—Pierre's kind of a jerk too. Dude's obsessed with money, never sees his kid, and his wife literally left him for a wizard. And I'm out here grinding to save his business anyway. That's actually genius game design though—it's the most realistic take on small-town economics I've seen in a game.

grounding.rewrite: I appreciate how games can realistically portray that fighting corporate monopolies doesn't automatically create a perfect outcome. Local alternatives can succeed while still being run by flawed people with their own moral compromises and personal problems. That's more honest storytelling than a simple good-versus-evil narrative.

original: Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. An unpasteurised study of the feral grief of relationship & personal breakdown. Simultaneously one of the funniest & saddest books I have ever read. One of the best too. I'm used to book sadness, book poignancy, book emotions – but this is the real thing.

reading_level.rewrite: Just finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. It's a raw look at grief from a broken relationship and personal crisis. The book is both really funny and really sad at the same time. It's one of the best books I've read. I've read lots of sad books before. But this one feels different. This one feels real.

background.rewrite: Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan—a memoir about the author's relationship ending and his subsequent personal crisis, told in a raw, unfiltered way that doesn't smooth over the messy reality. The book is shocking because it's both hilarious and deeply heartbreaking at the same time, often within the same paragraph. It's genuinely one of the best books I've ever read. What struck me most is that unlike typical literary fiction that aims for emotional depth, this feels authentically painful—not crafted sadness, but the actual experience of grief and loss laid bare on the page.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. It's a raw, unfiltered account of how a marriage to Sarah fell apart and what that emotional devastation actually feels like—not sanitized or reshaped for comfort. The book made me laugh out loud on page 47 about the mundane details of their fights, then gut-punched me on page 156 with the weight of real loss. McClanahan's prose doesn't manufacture emotions the way most literary fiction does; reading his specific memories—the arguments, the silences, the small betrayals—felt like witnessing actual grief, not a crafted version of it.

tone.rewrite: Just finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan and I'm SHATTERED. This isn't polite, sanitized sadness — it's raw, feral grief that tears through you. Relationship collapse. Personal devastation. And somehow it's HILARIOUS and DEVASTATING simultaneously. I've read plenty of sad books, plenty of "poignant" books, but nothing — NOTHING — like this. This is the real thing. This is what genuine emotional reckoning actually feels like. It's haunting me.

humor.rewrite: Just finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan and wow, my emotional stability has filed for divorce. It's an unpasteurised study of relationship & personal breakdown—which is a fancy way of saying it hurts in the best possible way. Somehow simultaneously one of the funniest & saddest books I've ever read (my tear ducts didn't know whether to laugh or file a complaint). Genuinely one of the best too. I've built up an immunity to regular book sadness over the years, but this one? This is the real thing. The uncut stuff. Consider me wrecked.

narrativity.rewrite: I was sitting in my kitchen at 2am, tears streaming down my face, when I realized I'd just finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. I'd been reading for hours, barely moving. The thing is, I've read plenty of sad books before—I know how to handle book sadness, book poignancy, the manufactured emotions that authors craft. But this? This was different. This was the real thing. An unpasteurised study of the feral grief of relationship and personal breakdown, and somehow simultaneously one of the funniest books I've ever encountered. I sat there in the dark, laughing and crying at the same time, understanding that I'd just experienced one of the best books I'll probably ever read.

grounding.rewrite: Finished The Sarah Book by Scott McClanahan. It's like watching someone document their own car crash in real-time—raw, unflinching, unpasteurised. You're witnessing genuine feral grief over a relationship & personal breakdown, not the polished sadness you get from most books. It made me laugh out loud one moment, then sit in stunned silence the next. Most books aim for poignancy; this one just *is* it. This is the real thing, not the performance of emotion.

original: if AAA studios ever figured out how to consistently make games with cool/unique aesthetics that aren't the usual faux-realistic greeble hell, so much of the indie space right now would be done. because that's the primary selling point for a lot of indie stuff right now.

reading_level.rewrite: if AAA studios ever figured out how to make games with cool and unique looks that aren't the same fake-realistic mess, most indie games would be in trouble. because that's the main reason people buy a lot of indie games right now.

background.rewrite: if AAA studios ever figured out how to consistently make games with cool/unique aesthetics that aren't the usual faux-realistic greeble hell, so much of the indie space right now would be done. right now, a lot of indie games succeed mainly because they offer distinct visual styles—think cel-shading, pixel art, or bold color palettes—instead of the photorealistic brown-and-gray environments that dominate big-budget titles. because that's the primary selling point for a lot of indie stuff right now. if major publishers could match that level of artistic distinctiveness, indie developers would lose a major competitive advantage.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: if studios like Rocksteady, Naughty Dog, and Activision ever figured out how to consistently make games with the hand-drawn art of Cuphead, the stop-motion style of A Space for the Unbound, or the voxel aesthetics of Minecraft instead of the brown-grey grimdark look in games like Call of Duty and Uncharted, most of what's thriving in the indie space right now—like Hades, Stardew Valley, and Hollow Knight—would lose their competitive edge. because distinctive visual direction is what separates those indie hits from AAA releases.

tone.rewrite: AAA studios' competitive advantage in game development could significantly shift if they prioritized distinctive visual aesthetics over photorealistic rendering. Many successful indie titles currently derive their market appeal largely from unique artistic direction—a space where AAA studios have been less active. Closing this gap would likely impact the indie sector's current differentiation strategy.

humor.rewrite: if AAA studios ever figured out how to consistently make games with cool/unique aesthetics instead of just endless variations of "brown cubicle dystopia #47," they'd accidentally obliterate like 60% of the indie market. turns out unlimited budgets don't help when your art direction is "add more lens flare and call it innovation."

narrativity.rewrite: I was scrolling through Steam last night, genuinely excited about a $70 AAA release. Then I opened it and... gray concrete, brown metal, the same procedural sludge I've seen a thousand times before. Meanwhile, my friend was raving about this tiny indie pixel art game with hand-painted backgrounds that cost $15. And it hit me: if AAA studios ever figured out how to consistently make games with cool, unique aesthetics that aren't the usual faux-realistic greeble hell, so much of the indie space would evaporate overnight. Because honestly, that's the primary selling point for a lot of indie stuff right now. The art direction is doing all the heavy lifting.

grounding.rewrite: if AAA studios ever figured out how to consistently make games with cool/unique aesthetics that aren't the usual faux-realistic greeble hell, so much of the indie space right now would be done. it's like how indie coffee shops thrived for years until big chains finally nailed the "cozy minimalist vibe" — once you can get that aesthetic at scale with AAA budgets, the main differentiator indie games currently rely on (think Hollow Knight's hand-drawn art, Hades' bold color palette, Spiritfarer's watercolor style) disappears overnight. that's the primary selling point for a lot of indie stuff right now.

original:
"EA has become the bourgeois of sorts by taking over the modes of production in the football realm, while Sega or ESPN Videogames or Take-Two can become the proletariat by working for the Madden series..."

A galaxy brain take from the Official US PlayStation Magazine, May 2005.

reading_level.rewrite: "EA took over football games. They control how these games are made. Sega, ESPN, and Take-Two work on games like Madden instead. This quote is from a 2005 PlayStation Magazine. Pretty wild thinking."

background.rewrite:
"EA has become the bourgeois of sorts by taking over the modes of production in the football realm, while Sega or ESPN Videogames or Take-Two can become the proletariat by working for the Madden series..."

This quote is from Official US PlayStation Magazine in May 2005. For context: back then, EA Sports dominated football video games (especially Madden NFL), controlling what games existed and how they were made. The quote sarcastically applies Marxist class terminology to this—calling EA the "bourgeois" (capitalist owners) for controlling the industry, and competitors like Sega or Take-Two the "proletariat" (workers) because they had less power. It's an exaggerated comparison to illustrate how thoroughly EA controlled the market at that time.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
"EA Sports monopolized football game development by owning exclusive NFL licensing deals, while Sega Sports, ESPN games, and Take-Two's 2K Sports became contract developers working on the Madden NFL series for EA."

A galaxy brain take from the Official US PlayStation Magazine, May 2005.

tone.rewrite:
"EA has MONOPOLIZED the entire football gaming industry, crushing any competition in their path. Sega, ESPN Videogames, and Take-Two are left scrambling as wage laborers in EA's empire, reduced to servicing the Madden machine.

This jaw-dropping analysis from Official US PlayStation Magazine, May 2005, exposed the ruthless corporate takeover we're STILL living with today."

humor.rewrite:
"EA has basically become the bourgeois of the football game world, hoarding all the modes of production like they're collecting championship rings, while Sega, ESPN Videogames, and Take-Two are stuck as the proletariat — essentially wage slaves developing the Madden series."

A galaxy brain take from the Official US PlayStation Magazine, May 2005. (They were really going full Marx over there.)

narrativity.rewrite:
I was digging through some old gaming magazines at a thrift store last weekend when I stumbled on this gem from Official US PlayStation Magazine, May 2005. The writer was making this wild argument about the video game industry's power structure, and I had to read it twice to believe it was real.

Apparently, EA had become "the bourgeois of sorts by taking over the modes of production in the football realm," they wrote, while suggesting that competitors like Sega, ESPN Videogames, and Take-Two were basically forced into a proletariat position, "working for the Madden series." 

I still don't know what to make of it, but it's definitely a galaxy brain take for the ages.

grounding.rewrite:
"EA has consolidated control over the major production systems in sports gaming, while other companies operate as subordinate producers within established franchises.

A notable take from the Official US PlayStation Magazine, May 2005."

original:
I need to put this into its own article at some point because I get tired of repeating that mechanisms are not genres

worker placement is just drafting, there is no true definition of "worker placement game"

even video games aren't as susceptible to this, few people say "I like double jump games"

reading_level.rewrite:
I keep having to repeat this, so I'll say it clearly: mechanics are not genres.

Worker placement is just drafting. There's no real definition of what makes a "worker placement game."

Video games don't have this problem as much. Nobody says "I like double jump games."

background.rewrite:
I need to put this into its own article at some point because I get tired of repeating this: when we talk about games, we often confuse **mechanisms** (how the game works) with **genres** (what kind of game it is).

Here's what I mean—take "worker placement," a mechanic where players assign their pieces to different board spaces to take actions. People treat it like a genre, but it's really just one system for *how* gameplay functions. Yet when you look at video games, you rarely see this confusion. Nobody says "I love double jump games" as a category, even though double jumping is a specific mechanic. We'd instead say "I like platformers" (the genre). Board games need the same clarity.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
I need to put this into its own article at some point because I get tired of repeating that mechanisms are not genres

worker placement games like Agricola and Puerto Rico are just drafting mechanics with different themes — there is no true definition of "worker placement game"

even video games aren't as susceptible to this; you don't hear gamers say "I like double jump games" the way they describe Celeste or Super Metroid

tone.rewrite:
I'm EXHAUSTED having to repeat this constantly: mechanisms are NOT genres, and we need to STOP treating them like they are.

Worker placement is JUST drafting. There IS no "true definition" of worker placement games — it's a meaningless category we've invented.

Video games GET THIS RIGHT. You don't hear people saying "I love double jump games" as if that's an actual genre. Why are we still doing this with board games?!

humor.rewrite:
I need to write a full manifesto about this because apparently I'm the only one who noticed: mechanisms aren't genres. Wild concept, I know.

worker placement is just drafting with extra steps and a middle management cosplay. there is no "true" worker placement game—it's a label we slapped on things and called it a day.

even *video games* figured this out. you don't see people saying "I'm really into double jump games" at parties. yet here we are.

narrativity.rewrite:
I was at a game night last week when someone asked me to recommend a "worker placement game" for their collection. I paused mid-sip of my drink because honestly? I've had this conversation a hundred times, and I'm exhausted by it.

Here's the thing: worker placement is just drafting with extra steps. There's no true definition of "worker placement game" — it's a mechanism, not a genre. It's like how you'd never catch someone saying "I like double jump games" when talking about video games. Yet somehow in board gaming, we've let mechanism names colonize our entire vocabulary.

grounding.rewrite:
I need to put this into its own article at some point because I get tired of repeating that mechanisms are not genres

worker placement is just drafting — imagine calling chess a "pawn movement game" instead of chess. there's no true definition of "worker placement game" because the mechanism is just the tool, not the identity

even video games aren't as susceptible to this. you don't hear someone say "I like double jump games" the way people say "I like worker placement games." they say "I like platformers" — that's the actual genre

original:
If someone says “we should grab a coffee sometime” (or something similar), they possibly have no intention of following through, and will be shocked if you try to arrange something. 

99% of the time, they’re just feigning warmth and interest to be polite. This can be confusing being autistic.

reading_level.rewrite:
The colloquial utterance "we should grab a coffee sometime" frequently constitutes a phatic expression rather than a genuine commitment, with individuals exhibiting discernible surprise upon encountering attempts to actualize such propositions.

In the preponderance of instances, such statements represent performative displays of cordiality and engagement, deployed principally to satisfy conventional social protocols. This phenomenon presents particular interpretive challenges for autistic individuals, whose cognitive processing patterns often prioritize semantic literalism over implicit pragmatic conventions.

background.rewrite:
If someone says "we should grab a coffee sometime" (or something similar), they possibly have no intention of following through, and will be shocked if you try to arrange something. 

In neurotypical social culture, casual phrases like these are often used as polite closing statements in conversations—a way to signal friendliness without making an actual commitment. The speaker typically expects the other person to understand this unspoken rule and *not* take it literally. 99% of the time, they're just feigning warmth and interest to be polite, rather than extending a genuine invitation.

For autistic people, who often process language more literally and expect statements to mean what they say, this creates real confusion. What feels like a clear plan to you ("let's get coffee") reads to them as a throwaway pleasantry—and they'll be shocked if you actually try to schedule it.

abstract_concrete.rewrite:
If someone says "we should grab a coffee sometime" or "let's do lunch soon," they often don't mean it literally. When you reply with "Great! How about Tuesday at 2pm at the cafe on Main Street?" they frequently act surprised or make excuses. 

This social convention—where people use these phrases as politeness rituals rather than concrete plans—can be confusing for autistic people who tend to interpret language more literally and expect follow-through on stated intentions.

tone.rewrite:
Research suggests that "we should grab a coffee sometime" functions primarily as a social convention rather than a concrete commitment. When followed up with scheduling attempts, such offers often go unfulfilled, indicating a disconnect between the literal statement and intended meaning.

This phrase typically serves a politeness function in social interaction rather than expressing genuine intent. For autistic individuals who process language more literally, this distinction between social convention and stated meaning can present a notable source of confusion.

humor.rewrite:
If someone says "we should grab a coffee sometime," what they *actually* mean is "I hope we never see each other again, but I need to sound like a functional human." Plot twist: take them seriously and watch their panic unfold—truly excellent entertainment.

Being autistic in a world of performative pleasantries? It's like everyone got the memo about the secret handshake except you. 99% of the time it's just expensive air being blown in your direction.

narrativity.rewrite:
Last week, a colleague told me "we should definitely grab coffee soon!" with a big smile. Excited, I pulled out my phone to check our calendars. The look on their face? Pure panic. Turns out, they were just being polite—and I'd somehow missed the memo that this is how most people talk.

I've learned the hard way that when someone says "we should grab coffee sometime," they usually don't mean it. 99% of the time, it's just polite small talk, a way to seem warm and interested without actual follow-through. Most people would be genuinely shocked if you actually tried to schedule it.

Being autistic means I often take things literally, which can make these moments really confusing. What feels like a genuine invitation to me is apparently just social code to everyone else.

grounding.rewrite: If someone says "we should grab a coffee sometime," they possibly have no intention of following through—kind of like when someone says "nice hat!" about a hat you hate. They're not actually complimenting the hat; they're just being polite. Similarly, "let's grab coffee" is often just a politeness ritual, not a literal plan. Take them up on it by suggesting Thursday at 2pm, and you'll likely get a vague response or silence. 99% of the time, they're feigning warmth and interest to be socially smooth. This can be deeply confusing being autistic, when you interpret statements as literal intentions rather than social pleasantries.

original: There are so many things I’ve learned in the last 2 years since I started getting fit (and hench) that I wish someone had told me when I was young. One of them is that the muscle soreness you get for a week after you work out a muscle group pretty much only happens the first time you do it

reading_level.rewrite: Throughout my two-year physique development journey, I've acquired considerable insights that would have proven invaluable during my formative years. Notably, delayed onset muscle soreness (DOMS), which manifests acutely for extended periods following initial engagement of a previously unstimulated muscle group, predominantly attenuates upon subsequent training sessions targeting that musculature.

background.rewrite: DOMS is basically a first-timer's tax. Once you've trained a muscle group, that week-long soreness disappears—your body adapts. Wish I'd known this before spending my first year convinced I was doing something wrong.

abstract_concrete.rewrite: There are so many things I've learned throughout my fitness journey that I wish someone had explained to me earlier. One of them is that the physical discomfort experienced following intensive training tends to diminish significantly once your body adapts to similar activity patterns.

tone.rewrite: I WISH SOMEONE HAD TOLD ME THIS YEARS AGO. After 2 years of getting fit, I'm furious at how much I suffered needlessly. That crushing muscle soreness that kept me paralyzed for a WEEK? It only happens the FIRST TIME you work out a muscle group. I could've saved myself so much pain if I'd just known. Don't make my mistake—push through that initial agony and it NEVER comes back like that again.

humor.rewrite: PSA: Remember when you thought your legs would be permanently destroyed after that first squat session? Narrator: They weren't. Turns out your muscles are just dramatic on opening night — nail the performance once and suddenly they're professionals who barely flinch. Wish someone had told me this two years ago so I could've stopped writing my will after leg day.

narrativity.rewrite: Muscle soreness lasting a week after working out a muscle group primarily occurs only on first exposure. Subsequent workouts of the same muscles produce minimal soreness. This adaptation happens consistently across individuals and training phases. The initial soreness is a normal response to novel stimulus, not an indicator of workout quality or effectiveness in later sessions.

grounding.rewrite: There are so many things I've learned in the last 2 years since I started getting fit (and hench) that I wish someone had told me when I was young. One of them is that the muscle soreness you get after working out a muscle group is like your first time riding a bike — brutal and scary at first, but once your body adapts, you barely notice it happening again. That week-long soreness? It's pretty much only a thing that first time you train a new muscle group.
